questions.specification:
The experiment focuses on investigating the relationship between temperature and the rate of evaporation of three different volatile liquids: methanol, ethanol, and propan-1-ol.

Three identical Petri dishes are labelled A, B, and C, each containing 3.0 cm³ of a different alcohol: methanol (A), ethanol (B), propan-1-ol (C). Each dish is placed inside a temperature-controlled chamber (e.g. warm water bath or incubator) at 60 °C. A digital balance is used to measure mass loss after 5 minutes of heating.

The following data were collected:

| Alcohol     | Initial mass / g | Final mass / g | Temperature / °C |
|-------------|------------------|----------------|------------------|
| Methanol    | 2.38             | 0.62           | 60               |
| Ethanol     | 2.40             | 1.15           | 60               |
| Propan-1-ol | 2.55             | 1.98           | 60               |

questions.specification:
The reaction between sodium thiosulfate and hydrochloric acid forms a precipitate:

$\mathrm{Na_2S_2O_3(aq)} + 2\mathrm{HCl(aq)} \rightarrow 2\mathrm{NaCl(aq)} + \mathrm{S(s)} + \mathrm{SO_2(g)} + \mathrm{H_2O(l)}$

labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (a) asks for an experimental method to determine reaction rate (disappearing cross/time measurement in a precipitation reaction).

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Reactivity 2. How much, how fast and how far? > R2.2 How fast? The rate of chemical change > R2.2.3 Factors affecting the rate of reaction
labeledSubSubTopics.title: R2.2.3 Factors affecting the rate of reaction
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (b) asks how temperature changes the time for the cross to disappear, i.e., the effect of temperature as a factor affecting reaction rate.

parts.content: Describe a method to determine the rate of this reaction.
parts.markscheme:
- Place beaker over black cross
<marks>1 mark</marks>
- Measure time taken until cross is no longer visible
<marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Understand the reaction
steps.content: In this reaction, solid sulfur ($S_{(s)}$) is produced as a precipitate. This makes the solution turn increasingly cloudy (turbid) over time. We can use this turbidity to measure the reaction rate using the **Disappearing Cross Method**.

steps.title: Setup the apparatus
steps.content: Place a reaction vessel (such as a beaker or conical flask) containing the sodium thiosulfate solution on top of a piece of paper marked with a distinct black cross.

steps.title: Measure the reaction time
steps.content: Add the hydrochloric acid to the flask and start a stopwatch immediately. Observe the black cross by looking vertically down through the mouth of the flask. Stop the timer at the exact moment the cross is no longer visible due to the sulfur precipitate.

highlights.text: Measure time taken until cross is no longer visible

steps.title: Calculate the reaction rate
steps.content:
The reaction rate is inversely proportional to the time taken ($t$). We assume that the amount of sulfur required to obscure the cross is constant across different trials:

$$\text{Rate} \propto \frac{1}{t}$$

By measuring the time $t$ for various initial concentrations of reactants, you can determine how those concentrations affect the rate of reaction.

steps.title: Identify the observable change
steps.content:
To measure the rate of this reaction, we first look at the state symbols in the chemical equation. We can see that solid sulfur, $\mathrm{S(s)}$, is produced. 

$$\mathrm{Na_2S_2O_3(aq)} + 2\mathrm{HCl(aq)} \rightarrow 2\mathrm{NaCl(aq)} + \mathrm{S(s)} + \mathrm{SO_2(g)} + \mathrm{H_2O(l)}$$

As the reaction progresses, the formation of this solid precipitate makes the solution increasingly cloudy (turbid), which provides a perfect opportunity to use light-based measurements. This topic is covered in **Reactivity 2.2: How fast? The rate of chemical change**.

steps.title: Set up the apparatus
steps.content:
In the **Photometric Light Transmission** approach, we replace the traditional 'disappearing cross' (seen by the human eye) with a more precise light sensor or colorimeter. 

1. Place the reaction mixture (sodium thiosulfate and hydrochloric acid) into a cuvette or a glass beaker.
2. Position a light source on one side of the container and a **light sensor** on the opposite side. 
3. Connect the sensor to a **data logger** to record real-time data.

steps.title: Monitor light transmission
steps.content:
Once the reactants are mixed, the data logger begins recording the **light intensity** or **percentage transmittance** ($T$) of the light passing through the solution. 

As the sulfur precipitate accumulates, less light is able to pass through the solution to reach the sensor. The data logger will show a curve of decreasing transmittance over time ($t$).

steps.title: Calculate the reaction rate
steps.content:
The rate of reaction is proportional to how quickly the light intensity drops. You can determine the rate by:

*   Calculating the gradient (slope) of the transmittance-time graph at the start ($t=0$) for the **initial rate**.
*   Alternatively, measuring the time ($t$) it takes for the transmittance to fall to a specific, predetermined value (like 50%).

$$\text{Rate} \propto \frac{1}{\text{time taken}}$$

This method is much more accurate than the human eye because it removes subjective judgment and provides a continuous stream of data.

steps.title: Relate to the markscheme
steps.content:
In an IB exam, even when using this high-tech method, you are effectively performing the same logic as the markscheme. 

- **Mark 1:** You are essentially placing the 'beaker' in a setup where visibility is monitored (the sensor path).
- **Mark 2:** You are measuring the time it takes for a 'target' (a specific light level) to no longer be reached.

highlights.text: Measure time taken until cross is no longer visible

parts.content: Suggest how temperature affects the time for the cross to disappear.
parts.markscheme:
Time decreases
<marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Temperature and reaction rate
steps.content:
In chemistry, we know that temperature is positively correlated with the rate of a chemical reaction. As temperature increases, particles move faster and collide more frequently with energy exceeding the activation energy ($E_a$). This relationship is represented by the Arrhenius equation in your data booklet:

$$k=A e^{\frac{-E_{a}}{R T}}$$

Here, as the temperature ($T$) increases, the rate constant ($k$) also increases, leading to a faster reaction.

steps.title: Rate vs. time relationship
steps.content:
In this specific experiment, we measure the time ($t$) it takes for a certain amount of sulfur precipitate to form and hide the cross. The rate of reaction is inversely proportional to the time taken:

$$\text{Rate} \propto \frac{1}{\text{time}}$$

If the reaction rate increases, it means the reaction is happening faster, so it will take less time to reach the point where the cross is no longer visible.

steps.title: State the effect
steps.content: By combining these two ideas, we can conclude that increasing the temperature will increase the rate of reaction, which in turn causes the time for the cross to disappear to **decrease**.

highlights.text: how temperature affects the time for the cross to disappear

steps.title: Link temperature to kinetic energy
steps.content:
To understand how temperature affects reaction time, we first look at the particles. Increasing the temperature increases the average kinetic energy of the reacting particles (the thiosulfate and hydrogen ions). 

You can review this concept in **Topic S1.1.3: Temperature and Kinetic Energy**.

steps.title: Apply collision theory
steps.content:
According to collision theory, for a reaction to occur, particles must collide with sufficient energy (greater than or equal to the activation energy, $E_a$). At a higher temperature:
1. Particles move faster, leading to a higher **frequency of collisions**.
2. A greater proportion of particles have energy $\ge E_a$, leading to a higher frequency of **successful collisions**.

This is mathematically represented by the Arrhenius equation in your data booklet:

$$k = A e^{\frac{-E_a}{RT}}$$

As temperature ($T$) increases, the rate constant ($k$) increases, which speeds up the reaction.

steps.title: Determine the effect on time
steps.content:
Since the rate of reaction increases, the solid sulfur precipitate ($\mathrm{S(s)}$) is produced more quickly. Because the sulfur forms faster, it takes less time for the solid to obscure the cross. 

Therefore, as temperature increases, the **time for the cross to disappear decreases**.

highlights.text: Suggest how temperature affects the time for the cross to disappear.

parts.content: Explain this effect in terms of activation energy.
parts.markscheme:
- Higher temperature $\rightarrow$ more particles exceed $E_a$
<marks>1 mark</marks>
- Faster formation of sulfur precipitate
<marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Temperature and kinetic energy
steps.content:
In chemistry, temperature is a measure of the average kinetic energy of particles. When we increase the temperature of the reaction mixture, the particles (the $\mathrm{Na_2S_2O_3}$ and $\mathrm{HCl}$ ions) move faster and with more energy.

This is a fundamental concept from **Topic S1.1.3: Temperature and Kinetic Energy**.

steps.title: Exceeding activation energy
steps.content:
For a collision to result in a reaction, the particles must collide with energy equal to or greater than the **activation energy** ($E_a$). 

At a higher temperature, the distribution of particle energies shifts. This means a significantly larger proportion (or more) of the particles now possess kinetic energy $E \ge E_a$.

steps.title: Successful collision frequency
steps.content:
Because more particles have sufficient energy to overcome the energy barrier ($E_a$), the frequency of **successful collisions** (those that actually result in a reaction) increases.

This addresses the first mark in the markscheme:
$$\text{Higher temperature} \rightarrow \text{more particles exceed } E_a$$

steps.title: Rate of precipitate formation
steps.content:
As the frequency of successful collisions increases, the overall rate of reaction increases. In this specific experiment, this results in the **sulfur precipitate** ($\mathrm{S(s)}$) forming much faster, which makes the solution turn cloudy more quickly.

This completes the second mark:
$$\text{Faster formation of sulfur precipitate}$$

steps.title: Visualize the Maxwell-Boltzmann curve
steps.content:
To explain the effect of temperature, we use the Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution. This graph shows the range of kinetic energies that particles in a sample possess. 

At any given temperature, only a small fraction of particles have enough energy to react when they collide. This minimum required energy is known as the **activation energy ($E_a$)**.

highlights.text: Explain this effect in terms of activation energy.

steps.title: Shift in energy distribution
steps.content:
When the temperature of the sodium thiosulfate and hydrochloric acid mixture increases, the average kinetic energy of the particles increases. 

On the Maxwell-Boltzmann graph, the curve shifts to the right and flattens (the peak becomes lower). This shift means that the entire distribution of particle energies moves toward higher values.

steps.title: Particles exceeding activation energy
steps.content:
Because the curve has shifted to the right, a much larger area under the curve is now located beyond the activation energy ($E_a$) threshold. This area represents the proportion of particles with $KE \geq E_a$.

In your exam, you must state that at a higher temperature, a **greater proportion of particles have energy equal to or greater than the activation energy**.

steps.title: Relate to precipitate formation
steps.content:
Since a higher proportion of particles possess the required activation energy, the frequency of **successful collisions** (those that lead to a reaction) increases. 

This leads directly to the **faster formation of the solid sulfur precipitate** ($S(s)$), which causes the solution to turn cloudy more quickly.

steps.title: Introduce the Arrhenius Equation
steps.content:
To explain the effect of temperature on the reaction rate in terms of activation energy ($E_a$), we use the **Arrhenius equation** found in the IB Chemistry data booklet:

$$k = A e^{\frac{-E_a}{RT}}$$

In this equation:
- $k$ is the rate constant (directly proportional to the reaction rate).
- $E_a$ is the activation energy.
- $T$ is the absolute temperature in Kelvin.
- $R$ is the gas constant.

steps.title: Analyze the Temperature Effect
steps.content:
As the temperature ($T$) increases, the value of the exponent $\frac{-E_a}{RT}$ becomes less negative. This causes the entire exponential factor, $e^{\frac{-E_a}{RT}}$, to increase.

Mathematically, since $k$ is proportional to this exponential factor, the rate constant $k$ increases as temperature rises.

steps.title: Link to Activation Energy
steps.content:
The term $e^{\frac{-E_a}{RT}}$ represents the **fraction of molecules** that possess kinetic energy equal to or greater than the activation energy ($E_a$). 

When the temperature is higher, a significantly larger proportion of the colliding particles have enough energy to overcome the activation energy barrier. This leads to a higher frequency of successful collisions.

steps.title: Conclude with Observable Result
steps.content: Because a greater number of particles exceed the activation energy, the reaction proceeds more quickly. In this specific experiment, this results in the **faster formation of the sulfur precipitate** ($S(s)$), which makes the solution turn cloudy sooner.

parts.content: State what is meant by the term activation energy.
parts.markscheme:
- Define activation energy as the minimum energy required for a reaction to occur. <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Understanding Collision Theory
steps.content:
To understand how ammonia is formed in the Haber process, we look at **Collision Theory** (covered in **Topic R2.2**). This theory states that for a reaction to occur, reactant particles (like $N_2$ and $H_2$) must collide with each other. 

However, a simple collision isn't enough; the particles must collide with sufficient energy to break the existing chemical bonds in the reactants so that new ones can form.

steps.title: Defining Activation Energy
steps.content:
In the context of collision theory, **activation energy** ($E_a$) is the specific "energy barrier" that must be overcome. 

It is defined as the **minimum energy** that colliding particles must possess for a reaction to occur. Without this minimum amount of kinetic energy, the particles will simply bounce off one another without reacting.

steps.title: Final Answer Formulation
steps.content:
To earn the mark in an IB exam, you should focus on the "minimum" requirement. 

**Activation energy:** The minimum amount of energy required for a reaction to occur upon collision.

steps.title: Analyze the energy profile
steps.content: In the provided energy profile diagram, the reaction starts with the reactants ($N_2(g) + 3H_2(g)$) at a specific potential energy level. To progress toward the products, the system must first reach the highest point of the curve, known as the **transition state** or the peak of the energy barrier.

steps.title: Identify activation energy
steps.content:
The activation energy ($E_a$) is represented on the diagram as the energy difference between the **reactants** and the **transition state**. 

It is the "energy hill" that the reactants must climb before they can be converted into products. 

$$\text{Activation Energy} = \text{Energy of Transition State} - \text{Energy of Reactants}$$

steps.title: State the final definition
steps.content:
To earn the mark in an IB exam, you should define it as the minimum energy required for a reaction to occur. This is the energy needed to break the bonds in the reactants so that new bonds can form.

This concept is a key part of **Collision Theory** (Topic R2.2), which explains that only collisions with energy equal to or greater than the $E_a$ will result in a successful reaction.

highlights.text: minimum energy required for a reaction to occur

parts.content: Identify whether the reaction is exothermic or endothermic and justify your answer using the diagram.
parts.markscheme:
- State that the reaction is exothermic. <marks>A1</marks>
- Justify that the products are at a lower energy level than the reactants, indicating that energy is released to the surroundings. <marks>R1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify reactants and products
steps.content:
To determine the nature of the reaction, we first look at the energy profile diagram. The horizontal line on the left represents the potential energy of the **reactants** ($N_2 + 3H_2$), and the horizontal line on the right represents the potential energy of the **products** ($2NH_3$).

This concept is a fundamental part of **Topic R1.1: Measuring enthalpy change** in the IB syllabus.

steps.title: Compare potential energy levels
steps.content:
By observing the vertical axis (Potential Energy), we can see that the line for the products is physically lower than the line for the reactants. 

This means that the products have less stored chemical energy than the starting materials.

highlights.text: Identify whether the reaction is exothermic or endothermic and justify your answer using the diagram.

steps.title: Classify the reaction
steps.content:
Since the products are at a lower energy level than the reactants, the "missing" energy must have been released to the surroundings, usually as heat. 

According to the markscheme, we identify this as an **exothermic** reaction. 

**Justification:** The products are at a lower energy level than the reactants, indicating that energy is released to the surroundings.

steps.title: Compare energy levels
steps.content: To determine the nature of the reaction, we first look at the relative energy levels of the reactants and products on the energy profile diagram. We can see that the products ($2NH_3$) are at a lower energy level than the reactants ($N_2 + 3H_2$). This indicates a "downward step" in energy as the reaction proceeds from start to finish.

steps.title: Determine enthalpy change sign
steps.content:
The enthalpy change ($\Delta H$) is defined as the difference between the energy of the products and the energy of the reactants. Based on the data booklet formula for enthalpy change:

$$\Delta H^{\ominus}=\Sigma(\Delta H_{\mathrm{f}}^{\ominus} \text{ products})-\Sigma(\Delta H_{\mathrm{f}}^{\ominus} \text{ reactants})$$

Since the products have less energy than the reactants, the value of $\Delta H$ will be negative ($\Delta H < 0$). For example, if we represent the product energy as $2$ and reactant energy as $10$, the change is $2 - 10 = -8$.

steps.title: Classify as exothermic
steps.content: Because the enthalpy change is negative, we conclude that the reaction is **exothermic**. This means that energy is released from the system into the surroundings as the chemical bonds in the products are formed. This concept is covered in **Reactivity 1.1: Measuring enthalpy change**.

highlights.text: Identify whether the reaction is exothermic or endothermic

parts.content: Suggest one reason why a high temperature is required for this reaction to proceed at a practical rate.
parts.markscheme:
- Explain that because the reaction has a high activation energy, a high temperature is needed so that more particles have energy $E \ge E_a$ to react upon collision. <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Understand temperature and energy
steps.content:
In chemistry, temperature is a measure of the average kinetic energy of the particles in a substance. When we increase the temperature of the reactants (nitrogen and hydrogen), we are giving the particles more energy and causing them to move faster. 

This concept is a fundamental part of **Topic S1.1.3: Temperature and Kinetic Energy**.

steps.title: Identify the energy barrier
steps.content: Looking at the energy profile diagram, there is a significant energy barrier that must be overcome for the reactants to turn into products. This is known as the **activation energy** ($E_a$). For a collision between molecules to result in a reaction, the colliding particles must possess energy equal to or greater than this value ($E \ge E_a$).

steps.title: Relate temperature to collisions
steps.content:
The Haber process has a relatively high activation energy. At low temperatures, very few particles have enough energy to overcome this barrier. By using a high temperature, we increase the average kinetic energy of the system, which significantly **increases the proportion of particles** that have energy $E \ge E_a$.

Because more particles meet the energy requirement, the frequency of **successful (effective) collisions** increases, allowing the reaction to proceed at a practical rate for industrial use.

steps.title: Identify the Arrhenius Equation
steps.content:
To understand why temperature affects the reaction rate, we look at the **Arrhenius equation** from the IB Data Booklet. This equation shows the relationship between the rate constant ($k$), the activation energy ($E_a$), and the temperature ($T$):

$$k = A e^{\frac{-E_a}{RT}}$$

Where:
- $k$ is the rate constant (directly proportional to the reaction rate).
- $E_a$ is the activation energy.
- $R$ is the gas constant.
- $T$ is the absolute temperature (in Kelvin).

steps.title: Analyze the Energy Profile
steps.content:
Looking at the provided energy profile, we can see a significant energy barrier between the reactants ($N_2 + 3H_2$) and the products. This hump represents the activation energy ($E_a$). 

In the Haber process, the activation energy is high because a large amount of energy is required to break the strong triple bond in the $N_2$ molecule ($N \equiv N$).

steps.title: Evaluate the Exponential Term
steps.content:
In the equation $$k = A e^{\frac{-E_a}{RT}}$$, the term $e^{\frac{-E_a}{RT}}$ represents the fraction of collisions with enough energy to overcome the activation barrier. 

Because $E_a$ is in the numerator of a negative exponent, a **high activation energy** makes the term $e^{\frac{-E_a}{RT}}$ very small. If the temperature ($T$) is low, the rate constant $k$ becomes so small that the reaction is too slow to be useful.

steps.title: Justify High Temperature
steps.content:
To achieve a "practical rate," we need a larger value for $k$. By increasing the temperature ($T$):
1. The value of the fraction $\frac{E_a}{RT}$ decreases.
2. This makes the negative exponent $\frac{-E_a}{RT}$ less negative (closer to zero).
3. Consequently, the value of $e^{\frac{-E_a}{RT}}$ increases significantly, leading to a much larger rate constant $k$.

Therefore, a high temperature is required to ensure that a sufficient fraction of particles have energy $E \ge E_a$ to react upon collision.

gdc.instructions: Evaluate the exponential part of the Arrhenius equation for two different temperatures.

desmos.instructions: Plot the function y = e^(-100000/(8.31*x)) to see how the rate constant factor increases with temperature x.

calculator.description: Visualize how increasing T increases k. If Ea = 100,000 J/mol and R = 8.31, compare k (proportional to the exponential term) at 300K vs 700K.

parts.content: Explain how the use of a catalyst would affect the energy profile diagram.
parts.markscheme:
- State that a catalyst provides an alternative reaction pathway with a lower activation energy. <marks>A1</marks>
- Describe that on the diagram, the peak of the energy barrier would be lower. <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Define the catalyst's role
steps.content:
In chemistry, a catalyst works by providing an **alternative reaction pathway** (or mechanism) for the reactants to turn into products. This is a fundamental concept covered in **Topic R2.2: How fast? The rate of chemical change**. 

Instead of the original high-energy route, the catalyst allows the molecules to react via a different series of steps that is more energetically favorable.

steps.title: Lowering the activation energy
steps.content:
Because this alternative pathway is more efficient, it has a **lower activation energy** ($E_a$). 

Activation energy is the minimum amount of energy that colliding particles must possess for a reaction to occur. By lowering this requirement, a greater proportion of particles will have enough energy to react at any given temperature, which increases the reaction rate.

highlights.text: State that a catalyst provides an alternative reaction pathway with a lower activation energy.

steps.title: Effect on the diagram
steps.content:
On an energy profile diagram, the activation energy is represented by the height of the curve from the reactants to the top of the curve. 

When a catalyst is used, the **peak of the energy barrier** (the maximum point on the curve) is **lower** than the original peak. It is important to note that the energy levels of the reactants and products remain exactly the same; only the "hill" in between them gets smaller.

highlights.text: Describe that on the diagram, the peak of the energy barrier would be lower.

parts.content: The Haber process is a reversible reaction. State and explain one condition used in industry to increase the yield of ammonia.
parts.markscheme:
- State that high pressure is used. <marks>A1</marks>
- Explain that there are fewer moles of gas on the product side (4 moles of reactants $\to$ 2 moles of products), so high pressure shifts the equilibrium position toward the products according to Le Chatelier's principle. <marks>R1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: State the industrial condition
steps.content: To increase the yield of ammonia in the Haber process, the industrial condition used is **high pressure** (typically around 200 atmospheres).

highlights.text: State that high pressure is used.

steps.title: Analyze gaseous stoichiometry
steps.content:
We first look at the coefficients of the gaseous species in the balanced equation:

$$N_2(g) + 3H_2(g) \rightleftharpoons 2NH_3(g)$$

- On the **reactant side**, there are $1 + 3 = 4$ moles of gas.
- On the **product side**, there are $2$ moles of gas.

Since 4 is greater than 2, the product side has a lower total number of gas molecules.

steps.title: Apply Le Chatelier's Principle
steps.content:
According to **Le Chatelier's Principle**, if the pressure of a system at equilibrium is increased, the system will shift to oppose that change by favoring the side with **fewer moles of gas**.

Because the production of ammonia results in fewer moles of gas (2 moles of $NH_3$ vs 4 moles of reactants), increasing the pressure shifts the equilibrium position to the **right** (the product side). This shift increases the percentage yield of ammonia.

highlights.text: Explain that there are fewer moles of gas on the product side (4 moles of reactants $\to$ 2 moles of products), so high pressure shifts the equilibrium position toward the products according to Le Chatelier's principle.

steps.title: Analyze the energy profile
steps.content:
First, let's look at the provided energy profile. We can see that the energy level of the products ($2NH_3$) is lower than the energy level of the reactants ($N_2 + 3H_2$). 

This tells us that energy is released during the reaction, meaning the forward reaction is **exothermic** ($ΔH$ is negative). You can review this concept in **Topic R1.1: Measuring enthalpy change**.

steps.title: State the condition
steps.content: To increase the yield of ammonia ($NH_3$) based on temperature, the condition used is a **lower temperature**.

steps.title: Apply Le Chatelier's Principle
steps.content:
According to **Le Chatelier's Principle** (Topic R2.3.4), if we decrease the temperature, the system will shift its equilibrium position to counteract that change by producing more heat.

Since the forward reaction is exothermic, it releases heat:

$$N_2(g) + 3H_2(g) \rightleftharpoons 2NH_3(g) + \text{heat}$$

Decreasing the temperature causes the equilibrium to shift to the **right** (the product side) to generate more heat, thereby increasing the yield of ammonia.

steps.title: Final Summary
steps.content:
In an exam, your two marks would be awarded as follows:

1.  **State the condition:** Use a low(er) temperature. <marks>A1</marks>
2.  **Explain the shift:** The forward reaction is exothermic, so a decrease in temperature shifts the equilibrium to the right to favor the production of ammonia. <marks>R1</marks>

*Note: While low temperature increases yield, industry actually uses a "compromise" temperature (around $450^{\circ}C$) to ensure the reaction happens fast enough to be profitable!*

steps.title: Identify the industrial condition
steps.content:
To increase the yield of ammonia in the Haber process, one common industrial condition is the **continuous removal of the product**, ammonia ($NH_3$), from the equilibrium mixture as it is formed.

In practice, this is achieved by cooling the gases so that the ammonia liquefies and can be tapped off, while the unreacted nitrogen and hydrogen remain as gases and are recycled back into the reactor.

highlights.text: State and explain one condition used in industry to increase the yield of ammonia.

steps.title: Apply Le Chatelier's principle
steps.content:
According to **Le Chatelier's principle**, if a system at equilibrium is disturbed by a change in conditions, the system will shift its equilibrium position to oppose the change.

By removing $NH_3$, we are decreasing the concentration of the product. The system opposes this decrease by favoring the forward reaction to produce more $NH_3$. This shifts the equilibrium position toward the **right (product side)**.

steps.title: Summarize the effect
steps.content:
Because the ammonia is removed continuously, the system never actually reaches a final static equilibrium. Instead, it is forced to keep reacting in the forward direction:

$$N_2(g) + 3H_2(g) \rightarrow 2NH_3(g)$$

This continuous shift ensures a much higher overall yield of ammonia than would be possible if the products were allowed to remain in the reaction vessel.

questions.specification:
Hydrogen peroxide decomposes slowly at room temperature:

$2\mathrm{H_2O_2(aq)} \rightarrow 2\mathrm{H_2O(l)} + \mathrm{O_2(g)}$

labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: All parts focus on defining/measuring reaction rate and describing how the rate changes during decomposition (e.g., tracking O2 volume or mass loss over time).

parts.content: State one way to measure the rate of this reaction.
parts.markscheme:
Measure volume of $\mathrm{O_2}$ gas produced per unit time
<marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Identify the gaseous product
steps.content:
To measure the rate of a reaction, we need to track how a property of the system changes over time. Looking at the chemical equation provided, we can see that oxygen is produced in the gaseous state:

$$2\mathrm{H_2O_2(aq)} \rightarrow 2\mathrm{H_2O(l)} + \mathrm{O_2(g)}$$

Because a gas is being evolved from a liquid solution, measuring the volume of this gas is an ideal way to determine the reaction rate. This concept is covered in **Reactivity 2.2: How fast? The rate of chemical change**.

steps.title: Select the measurement tool
steps.content:
In an IB chemistry lab, you would typically collect the oxygen gas using a **gas syringe** or by **downward displacement of water** in a graduated cylinder. 

As the reaction progresses, the plunger of the gas syringe is pushed out, allowing you to read the volume of $\mathrm{O_2}$ produced directly from the scale.

steps.title: Define the rate
steps.content:
A "rate" always involves a change over a specific period of time. To get a valid mark, you must mention that you are measuring the volume produced **per unit time** (e.g., $cm^3$ per second or per minute).

$$\text{Rate} = \frac{\Delta \text{Volume of } \mathrm{O_2}}{\Delta \text{time}}$$

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Based on the markscheme, the most direct way to state this is to focus on the quantity being measured and the time factor.

**Final Answer:** Measure the volume of $\mathrm{O_2}$ gas produced per unit time.

highlights.text: Measure volume of $\mathrm{O_2}$ gas produced per unit time

steps.title: Identify the gas product
steps.content:
To measure the rate of a reaction, we need to track how the amount of a reactant or product changes over time. In this reaction, hydrogen peroxide decomposes to produce oxygen gas:

$$2\mathrm{H_2O_2(aq)} \rightarrow 2\mathrm{H_2O(l)} + \mathrm{O_2(g)}$$

Notice that oxygen is the only product in the gaseous state.

steps.title: Apply mass loss principle
steps.content: Since oxygen is a gas, it will escape from an open reaction vessel. As the gas leaves the system, the total mass of the reaction mixture and the flask will decrease. This is a common technique covered in **Topic R2.2: How fast? The rate of chemical change**.

steps.title: Set up the measurement
steps.content:
To monitor this change, you would place the reaction flask on an electronic balance. You can use a cotton wool plug in the neck of the flask to prevent any liquid spray from escaping while still allowing the oxygen gas to exit. 

You then record the mass at regular time intervals as the reaction progresses.

steps.title: State the final method
steps.content:
The rate of the reaction is determined by measuring the **decrease in the mass of the reaction mixture per unit time**. 

Mathematically, the average rate would be:
$$\text{Rate} = -\frac{\Delta \text{mass}}{\Delta \text{time}}$$

(Note: While the markscheme suggests measuring the volume of gas, measuring mass loss is an equally valid IB method for reactions that produce a gas.)

steps.title: Analyze the reaction products
steps.content:
To measure the rate of reaction, we look for a change in a physical property over time. Let's examine the chemical equation:

$$2\mathrm{H_2O_2(aq)} \rightarrow 2\mathrm{H_2O(l)} + \mathrm{O_2(g)}$$

Notice that the reactants are in the aqueous phase, but one of the products, oxygen ($O_2$), is a **gas**. This is key for our measurement method.

steps.title: Apply the gas laws
steps.content:
In a sealed container with a fixed volume, the pressure of a gas is directly proportional to the number of moles of gas present. This relationship is shown in the **Ideal Gas Equation** from your data booklet:

$$PV = nRT$$

As the reaction progresses, more moles of oxygen gas ($n$) are produced. Since the volume ($V$) and temperature ($T$) are kept constant, the total **pressure ($P$)** inside the vessel will increase over time.

steps.title: State the measurement method
steps.content:
To determine the rate using this approach, you would measure the **increase in pressure per unit of time** (e.g., kPa per second). 

For the IB exam, a valid answer for this approach is:
**Measure the change in pressure over time using a pressure sensor or manometer.**

steps.title: Identify the reactant
steps.content:
To measure the rate of this reaction using titrimetric analysis, we need to monitor the change in the concentration of the reactant, hydrogen peroxide ($\mathrm{H_2O_2}$), as it decomposes over time.

$$2\mathrm{H_2O_2(aq)} \rightarrow 2\mathrm{H_2O(l)} + \mathrm{O_2(g)}$$

steps.title: Sampling and quenching
steps.content:
The first step is to take small samples, called **aliquots**, from the reaction mixture at regular time intervals. 

Immediately after taking each sample, we must **quench** the reaction. This stops the decomposition of $\mathrm{H_2O_2}$ at that exact moment, usually by adding the sample to a large volume of cold water or a chemical that stops the catalyst (if one is used). This ensures the concentration we measure represents the reaction state at that specific time.

steps.title: Titrate the samples
steps.content:
Next, we titrate the quenched sample against a standard solution to determine the amount of $\mathrm{H_2O_2}$ remaining. 

For example, $\mathrm{H_2O_2}$ can be titrated against acidified potassium manganate(VII). We use the relationship between moles, concentration, and volume from the data booklet to calculate the concentration of the reactant:

$$n = CV$$

By repeating this for multiple samples, we can track how the concentration $[\mathrm{H_2O_2}]$ decreases over time.

steps.title: State the method
steps.content:
In IB Chemistry, measuring the change in concentration of a reactant or product per unit time is a fundamental way to determine the rate. This falls under **Topic R2.2: How fast? The rate of chemical change**.

Therefore, one way to measure the rate is to **titrate samples of the reaction mixture at timed intervals to determine the change in concentration of $\mathrm{H_2O_2}$ over time**.

highlights.text: State one way to measure the rate of this reaction.

parts.content: State how the rate of reaction changes as the reaction proceeds.
parts.markscheme:
It decreases
<marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Analyze reactant concentration
steps.content:
In any chemical reaction, reactants are consumed to form products. In this specific reaction:

$$2\mathrm{H_2O_2(aq)} \rightarrow 2\mathrm{H_2O(l)} + \mathrm{O_2(g)}$$

The concentration of hydrogen peroxide ($[\mathrm{H_2O_2}]$) will gradually decrease as it is converted into water and oxygen gas. This concept is a fundamental part of **Topic R2.1: How much?**.

steps.title: Apply Collision Theory
steps.content:
According to **Topic R2.2: Collision theory**, the rate of a reaction depends on the frequency of collisions between reactant particles. 

As the concentration of $\mathrm{H_2O_2}$ falls, there are fewer reactant particles in the same volume of solution. This leads to a **lower frequency of successful collisions** per unit of time.

steps.title: Conclude the rate change
steps.content:
Because the frequency of collisions is reducing, the speed at which the reaction occurs must also drop. Therefore, the rate of reaction **decreases** as the reaction proceeds.

To earn the mark in an IB exam, simply stating the direction of change is sufficient.

highlights.text: State how the rate of reaction changes as the reaction proceeds.

steps.title: Represent the reaction progress
steps.content: To analyze the rate of decomposition of hydrogen peroxide ($H_2O_2$), we can look at a graph showing the volume of oxygen gas ($O_2$) produced over time. This is a common way to visualize reaction progress in **Topic R2.2: How fast? The rate of chemical change**.

steps.title: Relate rate to gradient
steps.content:
Using the **Graphical Analysis** approach, we define the rate of reaction at any specific time as the **gradient** (slope) of the curve on a product-time graph.

$$\text{Rate} = \text{gradient of the tangent to the curve}$$

steps.title: Visualize with a calculator
steps.content: We can visualize this by graphing a typical curve for product formation. Notice how the steepness of the line changes as the $x$-value (time) increases.

gdc.instructions:
1. Go to the Graphing menu.
2. Enter the function f1(x) = 1 - e^(-x).
3. Adjust the view window to show the first quadrant.
4. Use the 'Trace' or 'Derivative' tool to observe the slope value decreasing as x increases.

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [Y=].
2. Enter Y1 = 1 - e^(-X).
3. Press [WINDOW] and set Xmin=0, Xmax=5, Ymin=0, Ymax=1.2.
4. Press [GRAPH].
5. Use [2nd] [DRAW] -> [Tangent] at X=0.5 and X=3 to see the slope decrease.

desmos.instructions:
1. Type y = 1 - e^(-x) into the expression bar.
2. Observe the curve starting at (0,0).
3. Note that the curve is steepest at the start and becomes flatter (less steep) as it moves to the right.

calculator.description: Graph a model for product formation over time, such as $y = 1 - e^{-x}$, to see how the slope changes.

steps.title: Determine the rate change
steps.content:
As the reaction proceeds, the concentration of the reactant ($H_2O_2$) decreases. Graphically, this causes the curve to become **less steep** (the gradient decreases). 

Since the gradient represents the rate, we conclude that the rate of reaction **decreases** over time.

highlights.text: State how the rate of reaction changes as the reaction proceeds.

parts.content: Explain your answer to part 2.
parts.markscheme:
- Reactant concentration decreases over time
<marks>1 mark</marks>
- Fewer collisions per second between particles
<marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Analyze reactant concentration
steps.content:
As the reaction progresses, the reactant molecules of $\mathrm{H_2O_2}$ are converted into products ($H_2O$ and $O_2$). Because the reactant is being consumed, the **concentration of hydrogen peroxide decreases** over time.

This concept is a prerequisite for understanding reaction rates, which you can review in **Reactivity 2.1: How much? The amount of chemical change**.

steps.title: Apply collision theory
steps.content:
According to **Collision Theory**, for a reaction to occur, particles must collide with sufficient energy and correct orientation. 

A lower concentration means there are fewer $\mathrm{H_2O_2}$ particles present in a given volume. This results in a **lower frequency of collisions** (fewer collisions per second) between the reactant particles, which causes the reaction rate to slow down.

You can find more details on this in **Reactivity 2.2: How fast? The rate of chemical change**.

steps.title: Summary of marks
steps.content:
To earn full marks on this question, you must mention both the change in concentration and the change in collision frequency:

1.  **Reactant concentration decreases** as the reaction proceeds.
2.  This leads to **fewer collisions per second** (or a lower frequency of collisions) between particles.

steps.title: Link rate to concentration
steps.content:
In chemistry, the rate of a reaction is mathematically related to the concentration of the reactants. According to the **Rate Law**, the rate of decomposition for hydrogen peroxide can be expressed as:

$$\text{Rate} = k[\mathrm{H_2O_2}]^n$$

where $k$ is the rate constant and $n$ is the order of reaction. This equation tells us that the rate is directly proportional to the concentration of the $\mathrm{H_2O_2}$ reactant remaining in the solution.

steps.title: Consumption of reactants
steps.content:
As the reaction proceeds, the hydrogen peroxide ($\mathrm{H_2O_2}$) is used up to form water and oxygen gas. Because the reactant is being consumed, the **reactant concentration decreases over time**. 

Looking back at our rate equation, if $[\mathrm{H_2O_2}]$ gets smaller, the calculated value for the rate must also decrease.

steps.title: Connect to collision theory
steps.content:
Why does a lower concentration lead to a slower rate? From a microscopic perspective, a lower concentration means there are fewer particles in the same volume of space. 

With fewer particles available, there is a lower frequency of collisions, resulting in **fewer collisions per second between particles**. Since chemical reactions require particles to collide with sufficient energy, a drop in collision frequency naturally causes the reaction rate to slow down.

highlights.text: Fewer collisions per second between particles

options.markscheme: The overall order is the sum of the individual orders. This option only accounts for the order with respect to A.
The overall order is the sum of the individual orders. This option only accounts for the order with respect to B.

The overall order of a reaction is the sum of the powers of the concentration terms in the rate equation. Here, the order is $1$ with respect to $[\text{A}]$ and $2$ with respect to $[\text{B}]$. Thus, the overall order is $1 + 2 = 3$.
The overall order is the sum of the individual orders ($1 + 2 = 3$), not their product or an incorrect sum.

questions.specification:
For the reaction $\text{A} + \text{B} \rightarrow \text{products}$, the following data were obtained:

| Experiment | $[\text{A}] / \text{mol dm}^{-3}$ | $[\text{B}] / \text{mol dm}^{-3}$ | Initial rate / $\text{mol dm}^{-3} \text{s}^{-1}$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | 0.10 | 0.10 | $1.2 \times 10^{-3}$ |
| 2 | 0.20 | 0.10 | $2.4 \times 10^{-3}$ |
| 3 | 0.20 | 0.20 | $9.6 \times 10^{-3}$ |

What is the rate equation?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Reactivity 2. How much, how fast and how far? > R2.2 How fast? The rate of chemical change > R2.2.9 Rate equations (Higher Level Only)

labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks for the rate equation by comparing initial rates as reactant concentrations change (method of initial rates), which is directly about determining the form of the rate law.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Reactivity 2. How much, how fast and how far? > R2.2 How fast? The rate of chemical change > R2.2.10 Order of a reaction (Higher Level Only)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: R2.2.10 Order of a reaction (Higher Level Only)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: To choose the correct rate equation, the student must deduce the order with respect to A and B from the experimental data (reaction orders).

options.markscheme:
Comparing Experiment 1 and 2: The concentration of $\text{A}$ doubles while the concentration of $\text{B}$ remains constant. The initial rate doubles ($2.4 \times 10^{-3} / 1.2 \times 10^{-3} = 2$), so the reaction is first order with respect to $\text{A}$.

Comparing Experiment 2 and 3: The concentration of $\text{B}$ doubles while the concentration of $\text{A}$ remains constant. The initial rate quadruples ($9.6 \times 10^{-3} / 2.4 \times 10^{-3} = 4 = 2^2$), so the reaction is second order with respect to $\text{B}$.

The rate equation is: $\text{rate} = k[\text{A}][\text{B}]^2$

parts.content: State the product formed in the reaction besides $\text{MgCl}_2$.
parts.markscheme:
$\text{H}_2$ / hydrogen <marks>A1</marks>

*Accept "hydrogen gas".*

steps.title: Identify the reaction type
steps.content: First, let's look at the substances involved. We have **magnesium** (a metal) and **hydrochloric acid** (an acid). This reaction is a classic example of an acid-metal reaction, which you can find in your syllabus under **Topic R3.2.4: Acid-metal reactions**.

steps.title: Apply general reaction pattern
steps.content:
In chemistry, certain types of reactants follow predictable patterns. The **General Reaction Pattern** for a metal reacting with an acid is:

$$\text{Metal} + \text{Acid} \rightarrow \text{Salt} + \text{Hydrogen gas}$$

This pattern holds true for most reactive metals like magnesium.

steps.title: Match products to pattern
steps.content:
The question mentions that one of the products is **$\text{MgCl}_2$**. Looking at our pattern:

1.  **Metal**: $\text{Mg}$
2.  **Acid**: $\text{HCl}$
3.  **Salt**: $\text{MgCl}_2$ (Magnesium chloride)

According to the pattern, the only remaining product must be **hydrogen gas**.

steps.title: State final answer
steps.content:
The chemical formula for hydrogen gas is $\text{H}_2$. You can provide the name or the formula for your final answer.

$$\text{Mg(s)} + 2\text{HCl(aq)} \rightarrow \text{MgCl}_2\text{(aq)} + \text{H}_2\text{(g)}$$

steps.title: Identify the reactants
steps.content:
First, let's identify what we are starting with. The question mentions that magnesium reacts with hydrochloric acid. 

In chemical terms:
- Magnesium is represented as $\text{Mg}$.
- Hydrochloric acid is represented as $\text{HCl}$.

steps.title: Set up the equation
steps.content:
The law of conservation of mass tells us that atoms cannot be created or destroyed. We know one of the products is magnesium chloride, $\text{MgCl}_2$. We can write a partial equation to see what is still "missing":

$$\text{Mg} + \text{HCl} \rightarrow \text{MgCl}_2 + \text{?}$$

steps.title: Deduce the missing product
steps.content:
Let's look at the atoms on both sides:
- **Reactants side:** We have magnesium ($\text{Mg}$), hydrogen ($\text{H}$), and chlorine ($\text{Cl}$).
- **Products side:** We see magnesium and chlorine in the $\text{MgCl}_2$.

The only element from the reactants that isn't accounted for in the $\text{MgCl}_2$ is **hydrogen**. Since hydrogen is a diatomic gas in its natural state, it must form $\text{H}_2$.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The second product formed in this reaction is hydrogen gas, which is written as $\text{H}_2$.

According to the markscheme, you can provide the chemical formula or the name.

parts.content: Suggest why powdered magnesium reacts faster than a strip.
parts.markscheme:
powdered magnesium has a larger surface area <marks>A1</marks>

more frequent collisions OR more collisions per unit time (between reactants) <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Compare surface area
steps.content: When a solid like magnesium is ground into a powder, its total surface area increases significantly compared to a single strip of the same mass. This means a much larger number of magnesium atoms are exposed and available to interact with the hydrochloric acid solution.

highlights.text: powdered magnesium has a larger surface area

steps.title: Link to collision frequency
steps.content: According to collision theory, a reaction can only occur when reactant particles collide. Because the powder provides more contact points, the particles of hydrochloric acid $(\text{H}^{+})$ can strike the magnesium atoms more often. This results in an increased frequency of collisions (or more collisions per unit time).

steps.title: Relate to reaction rate
steps.content: Since there are more collisions happening every second, the overall rate of the chemical reaction increases. This is why the powdered magnesium reacts noticeably faster than the solid strip.

parts.content: Identify the factor affecting the rate in this case.
parts.markscheme: surface area <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Compare physical forms
steps.content:
The question compares two different physical forms of the same reactant: **powdered magnesium** and a **strip of magnesium**. 

In chemical kinetics, changing the physical form of a solid reactant directly changes how much of that material is available to react at any given moment. This is a key concept covered in **Topic R2.2: How fast? The rate of chemical change**.

steps.title: Identify the factor
steps.content:
When a solid is crushed into a powder, the total area of the material exposed to the surrounding liquid (the acid) increases significantly compared to a single solid strip. 

According to collision theory, more exposed atoms mean more frequent successful collisions between the magnesium and the hydrochloric acid. This specific factor is known as **surface area** (or sometimes particle size).

steps.title: Final Answer
steps.content: The factor affecting the rate of reaction in this case is the **surface area**.

parts.content: State the meaning of the term endothermic reaction.
parts.markscheme: An endothermic reaction is one that absorbs energy from the surroundings. <marks>1</marks>

steps.title: Define energy transfer
steps.content: In chemistry, we classify reactions based on how energy moves between the **system** (the chemicals) and the **surroundings** (everything else). To answer this, we look at the direction of that energy flow.

steps.title: State the definition
steps.content:
An **endothermic reaction** is a reaction that results in a net **absorption of energy** (typically heat) from the surroundings into the system. 

This means the system gains energy, which is why the products end up with a higher enthalpy than the reactants.

steps.title: Relate to photosynthesis
steps.content: You can see this in the provided equation where **light energy** is required as an input. Because the system takes in this energy to form products, the energy level of the products is higher than the reactants on the energy profile diagram.

steps.title: Analyze the energy profile
steps.content: Looking at the energy profile diagram for photosynthesis, we can see that the energy level of the products (glucose and oxygen) is higher than the energy level of the reactants (carbon dioxide and water). This indicates that energy must be absorbed for the reaction to occur.

steps.title: Define endothermic reaction
steps.content: In an **endothermic reaction**, the system absorbs energy from its surroundings. This is why the equation for photosynthesis includes "light energy" on the reactant side—it is the energy input required to drive the process.

steps.title: Identify thermodynamic change
steps.content:
From a thermodynamic perspective, an endothermic reaction is characterized by a **positive change in enthalpy** ($$\Delta H > 0$$). 

This happens because the enthalpy of the products ($$H_{\text{products}}$$) is greater than the enthalpy of the reactants ($$H_{\text{reactants}}$$). Using the logic from the enthalpy change formula in your data booklet:

$$\Delta H = \sum H_{\text{products}} - \sum H_{\text{reactants}}$$

Since the products are at a higher energy level, the result of this subtraction is a positive value.

steps.title: Final Definition
steps.content: To answer the question, an endothermic reaction is one that **absorbs energy from the surroundings**, resulting in a positive enthalpy change ($$\Delta H > 0$$).

steps.title: Analyze the energy diagram
steps.content:
To understand why this reaction is endothermic, let's look at the energy profile diagram. The y-axis represents the potential energy or enthalpy ($H$) of the system. 

Notice the positions of the two horizontal lines:
- The **reactants** ($6\mathrm{CO}_2 + 6\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$) are at a lower energy level.
- The **products** ($\mathrm{C}_6\mathrm{H}_{12}\mathrm{O}_6 + 6\mathrm{O}_2$) are at a higher energy level.

This tells us that the enthalpy of the products is greater than the enthalpy of the reactants ($H_{\text{products}} > H_{\text{reactants}}$).

steps.title: Interpret the enthalpy change
steps.content:
In chemistry, the enthalpy change ($\Delta H$) is calculated as:

$$\Delta H = H_{\text{products}} - H_{\text{reactants}}$$

Since the products are higher on the graph than the reactants, the value of $\Delta H$ is positive ($+$). For the system to move from a lower energy state to a higher energy state, it must have gained energy from its environment.

steps.title: Define endothermic reaction
steps.content:
Based on our graphical observation that energy is taken in to reach the higher product level, we can state the definition. 

An **endothermic reaction** is a chemical reaction that **absorbs energy (heat) from the surroundings**.

In the context of photosynthesis, this energy is absorbed in the form of light energy.

steps.title: Analyze the energy profile
steps.content:
To define an **endothermic reaction**, let's look at what happens during a chemical change from a bond perspective. Every reaction involves two main stages:
1.  **Breaking bonds** in the reactants (this always requires energy).
2.  **Forming bonds** in the products (this always releases energy).

In the provided energy profile for photosynthesis, we can see the products have a higher potential energy than the reactants, indicating that energy has been absorbed overall.

steps.title: Compare bond energy changes
steps.content:
Using the **Bond Energy Perspective**, we compare the energy "cost" of breaking the old bonds to the energy "payback" of making new ones. 

In an endothermic reaction, the energy needed to pull the atoms apart in the reactants is greater than the energy given off when the atoms rearrange into products. This results in a net absorption of energy from the surroundings.

steps.title: State the definition
steps.content:
The formal definition of an **endothermic reaction** from this perspective is:

A reaction where more energy is required to break the bonds in the reactants than is released when new bonds form in the products.

This concept is a key part of **Topic R1.2: Energy cycles in reactions**, specifically when calculating enthalpy changes using average bond enthalpies.

parts.content: State and explain whether photosynthesis is an exothermic or endothermic reaction based on the diagram.
parts.markscheme:
• Photosynthesis is endothermic. 1
  • The products are at a higher energy level than the reactants, meaning energy has been absorbed. 1

steps.title: Analyze the energy diagram
steps.content:
To answer this question, we first need to look at the relative positions of the horizontal lines on the energy profile diagram. These lines represent the potential energy of the substances involved in the reaction.

*   The left-hand side represents the **reactants** ($6\mathrm{CO}_2 + 6\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$).
*   The right-hand side represents the **products** ($\mathrm{C}_6\mathrm{H}_{12}\mathrm{O}_6 + 6\mathrm{O}_2$).

This falls under **Topic R1.1: Measuring enthalpy change**, specifically looking at energy profiles.

steps.title: Compare energy levels
steps.content:
Notice that the products are plotted higher on the vertical axis (Potential Energy) than the reactants. 

$$\text{Energy of Products} > \text{Energy of Reactants}$$

Because the products have more energy than the reactants, the system must have taken in energy from the surroundings during the chemical change. The question equation also gives us a clue: it shows that **light energy** is required for the reaction to proceed.

steps.title: Classify the reaction
steps.content:
In IB Chemistry, we define a reaction where the system absorbs energy from its surroundings as **endothermic**. 

Since the enthalpy change ($\Delta H$) is the difference between the energy of the products and the reactants, a higher product level results in a positive $\Delta H$ value ($+ \Delta H$).

$$\Delta H = H_{\text{products}} - H_{\text{reactants}} > 0$$

steps.title: Final Conclusion
steps.content:
Based on the visual evidence from the diagram, you can state your final answer to earn the 2 marks:

1.  **State:** Photosynthesis is an **endothermic** reaction.
2.  **Explain:** The **products are at a higher energy level than the reactants**, which indicates that energy has been absorbed from the surroundings (in this case, from light).

steps.title: Analyze the energy diagram
steps.content:
To determine the type of reaction, we first look at the relative potential energy levels of the reactants and products in the diagram. Notice that the energy level of the products ($C_6H_{12}O_6 + 6O_2$) is physically higher on the y-axis than the energy level of the reactants ($6CO_2 + 6H_2O$).

This shows that the chemical system gained energy as it moved from the reactant state to the product state.

steps.title: Determine the enthalpy change
steps.content:
The enthalpy change ($\Delta H$) is defined as the difference between the energy of the products and the energy of the reactants:

$$\Delta H = H_{\text{products}} - H_{\text{reactants}}$$

Because the products are at a higher energy level, the value of $\Delta H$ is positive ($> 0$). This positive change occurs because the system has absorbed energy from its surroundings—specifically in the form of **light energy**—to reach the higher energy product state.

steps.title: Classify as endothermic
steps.content:
In IB Chemistry, any reaction where the system absorbs energy from the surroundings and the products have more enthalpy than the reactants is classified as an **endothermic** reaction.

Therefore, photosynthesis is an **endothermic** reaction because its products are at a higher energy level than its reactants, resulting in a positive $\Delta H$.

parts.content: Explain the role of light energy in this reaction.
parts.markscheme:
• Light energy provides the energy required to overcome the high activation energy barrier. 1
  • Without light, the reactants would not have enough energy to reach the transition state. 1

steps.title: Identify the energy barrier
steps.content:
Take a look at the energy profile diagram provided. You'll notice a large "hill" between the reactants ($6 \mathrm{CO}_2 + 6 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$) and the products. This peak represents the **activation energy ($E_a$)**, which is the minimum energy required for a reaction to occur.

In photosynthesis, this energy barrier is quite high, meaning the molecules don't just react on their own under normal conditions.

steps.title: Provide energy for activation
steps.content:
The primary role of light energy is to provide the external energy needed to overcome this high activation energy barrier. 

In chemistry terms, we say that the light energy is absorbed by the system, allowing the reactant molecules to gain enough energy to climb that "hill" on the diagram.

steps.title: Reach the transition state
steps.content:
The very top of the peak on the energy profile is known as the **transition state**. 

Without the input of light energy, the reactant particles ($ \mathrm{CO}_2 $ and $ \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} $) would not have enough energy to reach this transition state. If they can't reach the top of the barrier, they cannot transform into the products ($ \mathrm{C}_6 \mathrm{H}_{12} \mathrm{O}_6 $ and $ \mathrm{O}_2 $).

steps.title: Summary of marks
steps.content:
To earn the full 2 marks for this question, your explanation should include these two key points:

1.  **Light energy provides the energy required** to overcome the high activation energy barrier ($E_a$).
2.  **Without light, the reactants would not have enough energy** to reach the transition state at the peak of the diagram.

This aligns with what we cover in **Topic R2.2: How fast? The rate of chemical change**, specifically regarding energy profiles and activation energy.

steps.title: Analyze the energy profile
steps.content: In the provided energy profile diagram, we can see that the potential energy of the products ($\mathrm{C}_6\mathrm{H}_{12}\mathrm{O}_6$ and $\mathrm{O}_2$) is much higher than the potential energy of the reactants ($\mathrm{CO}_2$ and $\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$). This indicates that photosynthesis is a strongly **endothermic** reaction. To learn more about these diagrams, you can review **Topic R1.1: Energy profiles**.

steps.title: Overcome activation energy barrier
steps.content: Because the products are at a higher energy state, the reaction requires a significant input of energy to proceed. Specifically, light energy is absorbed to provide the energy required to overcome the high **activation energy barrier** ($E_a$) shown by the "hump" in the diagram.

steps.title: Reach the transition state
steps.content:
The activation energy represents the minimum energy needed for reactants to reach the **transition state**. Without the absorption of light, the reactant molecules would not have enough energy to reach this peak, and the reaction would not occur. 

In summary:
1. Light energy provides the energy required to overcome the high activation energy barrier.
2. Without light, the reactants would not have enough energy to reach the transition state.

parts.content: State two ways in which the energy profile diagram would change if a catalyst (e.g., an enzyme) were used.
parts.markscheme:
• The activation energy would be lower. 1
  • The peak of the energy curve would be reduced (the transition state occurs at a lower energy). 1

steps.title: Understand the catalyst's role
steps.content:
In kinetics, a **catalyst** (like an enzyme in photosynthesis) works by providing an alternative reaction pathway. This pathway is more efficient than the original one. 

You can review this concept in **Topic R2.2: How fast? The rate of chemical change**. According to collision theory, for a reaction to occur, particles must collide with energy equal to or greater than the activation energy ($E_a$).

steps.title: Identify activation energy change
steps.content:
The first major change is that the **activation energy** ($E_a$) will be lower. 

On the energy profile diagram, the activation energy is the vertical distance from the energy of the reactants to the top of the peak. By providing an alternative mechanism, the catalyst reduces this "energy barrier."

From the Data Booklet, we can see how this affects the rate constant $k$ in the Arrhenius equation:

$$k = Ae^{\frac{-E_a}{RT}}$$

A smaller $E_a$ results in a larger rate constant $k$, meaning the reaction proceeds faster.

steps.title: Identify transition state change
steps.content:
The second change relates to the **peak of the curve**. Because the activation energy is lower, the maximum energy level reached during the reaction—the energy of the **transition state**—is reduced.

On the graph, you would draw a new dashed line that curves below the original solid line, starting and ending at the same reactant and product levels, but with a much lower "hump."

steps.title: Summarize for marks
steps.content:
To earn the 2 marks, you must state both distinct visual changes to the diagram:

1.  **Lower activation energy**: The value of $E_a$ decreases.
2.  **Lower peak/transition state**: The maximum point on the curve is lower in energy.

Note: The enthalpy change ($ΔH$) between reactants and products remains **unchanged**.

parts.content: Suggest why photosynthesis would not occur in the dark, even if all reactants are present.
parts.markscheme: Photosynthesis needs light energy to overcome the activation energy; in the dark, there is no energy input to start the reaction. <marks>1</marks>

steps.title: Identify the energy barrier
steps.content:
Looking at the provided energy profile diagram, notice the large peak between the reactants (left side) and the products (right side). This peak represents the **activation energy** ($E_a$).

In **Topic R2.2: How fast?**, we learn that even if a reaction is overall possible, it cannot proceed unless the reactants gain enough energy to reach this high-energy "transition state" at the top of the curve.

steps.title: Define activation energy requirements
steps.content:
The activation energy is the minimum energy that colliding molecules must possess for a reaction to occur. We can see this concept reflected in the Arrhenius equation found in the data booklet:

$$k = A e^{\frac{-E_{a}}{RT}}$$

This equation shows that the rate of reaction ($k$) depends heavily on the activation energy ($E_a$). If there isn't enough energy available to overcome this barrier, the reaction rate will be effectively zero.

steps.title: Relate light to energy input
steps.content:
In the chemical equation for photosynthesis, light energy is specified as a requirement. Light provides the necessary energy to "lift" the reactants over the activation energy barrier shown in the diagram.

$$\text{light energy} \rightarrow \text{Activation Energy (} E_a \text{)}$$

steps.title: Explain the dark conditions
steps.content:
If the plant is in the dark, there is no light energy input. Without this external energy source, the reactant molecules ($\mathrm{CO}_2$ and $\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$) do not have enough energy to reach the transition state at the top of the energy profile. 

Consequently, they cannot overcome the **activation energy** barrier, so the reaction does not occur.

highlights.text: Suggest why photosynthesis would not occur in the dark, even if all reactants are present.

steps.title: Analyze the energy profile
steps.content: Looking at the energy profile diagram, we can see that the potential energy of the products is higher than that of the reactants. This indicates that the reaction is **endothermic**, meaning it results in a net absorption of energy.

steps.title: Identify the energy requirement
steps.content:
In an endothermic reaction, the system must take in energy from the surroundings to move from a lower energy state to a higher energy state. This is often referred to as an "uphill" process. This energy gap is related to the enthalpy change ($ΔH$):

$$\Delta H^{\ominus}=\Sigma(\Delta H_{\mathrm{f}}^{\ominus} \text{ products})-\Sigma(\Delta H_{\mathrm{f}}^{\ominus} \text{ reactants})$$

Because the products have a higher enthalpy, $\Delta H$ is positive, and the reaction requires a continuous external energy input to proceed.

steps.title: Explain the role of light
steps.content:
The chemical equation shows that **light energy** is the specific source used to drive this reaction upward. Light provides the necessary energy to overcome the activation energy ($E_a$) and reach the higher energy state of the products. 

In the dark, there is no external energy source to provide this input. Without this "push," the reactants cannot overcome the energy barrier or the endothermic requirement, so the reaction will not occur.

highlights.text: would not occur in the dark
