parts.content: State the meaning of the term activation energy.
parts.markscheme:
Minimum amount of energy required for a reaction to occur / for a collision to be successful. <marks>A1</marks>

[1 mark]

steps.title: Identify the energy barrier
steps.content:
In an energy profile diagram, the "hump" between the reactants and the products represents the energy barrier that must be overcome for the reaction to proceed. This barrier is what we call the **activation energy** ($E_a$). 

This concept is a core part of **Topic R2.2: How fast? The rate of chemical change** in your syllabus.

steps.title: Define using collision theory
steps.content:
According to collision theory, for a reaction to occur, particles must collide with sufficient kinetic energy. 

**Activation energy** is defined as the **minimum** amount of energy required for a collision between reactant particles to be **successful**, resulting in a chemical reaction. 

If the colliding particles have less than this minimum energy, they will simply bounce off each other without reacting.

highlights.text: State the meaning of the term activation energy

steps.title: Final check with markscheme
steps.content:
To earn the mark, you must mention that it is the **minimum energy** and link it to the **reaction occurring** or a **collision being successful**. 

In your data booklet, you'll see $E_a$ appearing in the Arrhenius equation within the **Key Chemistry Equations** section:

$$k=A e^{\frac{-E_{a}}{R T}}$$

While you don't need the math for this part, remembering this formula helps you identify $E_a$ as a fundamental constant for a specific reaction's rate.

steps.title: Analyze the energy profile
steps.content: In chemical kinetics, the energy profile diagram for the decomposition of $\text{CaCO}_3$ shows the energy of the system as the reaction progresses. For the reactants to turn into products, they must first reach the peak of the curve, which represents the transition state. This peak acts as an **energy barrier** that the particles must overcome.

steps.title: Apply energy barrier approach
steps.content:
Using the **Energy Barrier Approach**, we define activation energy ($E_a$) as the minimum amount of energy that colliding particles must possess to reach this transition state. If the particles do not have enough kinetic energy to exceed this barrier, the collision will be unsuccessful and no reaction will occur.

This concept is also found in the Arrhenius equation in your data booklet:

$$k = A e^{\frac{-E_{a}}{RT}}$$

Where $E_a$ represents the height of that energy barrier.

steps.title: State the final definition
steps.content:
To earn the mark, you should define activation energy as the **minimum energy** required for a reaction to occur or for a collision to be successful.

This is a fundamental concept covered in **Topic R2.2.4: Activation energy**.

parts.content: State and explain if the decomposition of calcium carbonate is an endothermic or exothermic reaction.
parts.markscheme:
Endothermic. <marks>A1</marks>

Products are at a higher energy level than the reactants / energy is absorbed from the surroundings. <marks>R1</marks>

[2 marks]

steps.title: Identify levels on the diagram
steps.content:
First, let's look at the energy profile diagram provided. The y-axis represents the potential energy of the chemical species. 

In the chemical equation:
$$\text{CaCO}_3(\text{s}) \to \text{CaO}(\text{s}) + \text{CO}_2(\text{g})$$

We can see that the reactants (starting materials) are on the left side of the curve, and the products (ending materials) are on the right side.

steps.title: Compare reactant and product energy
steps.content:
By looking at the vertical position on the y-axis, compare the horizontal line for the reactants to the horizontal line for the products. 

Notice that the energy level of the products ($\text{CaO} + \text{CO}_2$) is physically higher than the energy level of the reactant ($\text{CaCO}_3$). This indicates that the system has gained potential energy during the reaction.

steps.title: Determine the reaction type
steps.content:
Because the products are at a **higher energy level** than the reactants, energy must have been absorbed from the surroundings to reach that higher state. 

In IB Chemistry, when the enthalpy change ($\Delta H$) is positive (products > reactants), the reaction is classified as **endothermic**.

To earn both marks, you must state the type and provide the reason based on the levels:
1. **Endothermic**
2. **Products are at a higher energy level than the reactants** (or energy is absorbed).

steps.title: Evaluate energy levels
steps.content:
To determine if the reaction is endothermic or exothermic, we first look at the relative positions of the reactants and products on the potential energy axis of the diagram. 

In this profile, the energy level for the reactant, $\text{CaCO}_3(\text{s})$, is lower than the energy level for the products, $\text{CaO}(\text{s}) + \text{CO}_2(\text{g})$. This indicates that the system's energy has increased during the reaction.

highlights.text: State and explain if the decomposition of calcium carbonate is an endothermic or exothermic reaction.

steps.title: Determine enthalpy change sign
steps.content:
The enthalpy change ($\Delta H$) is defined as the difference between the enthalpy of the products and the enthalpy of the reactants. Based on the diagram:

$$\Delta H = H_{\text{products}} - H_{\text{reactants}}$$

Since the energy level increases from the initial reactant state to the final product state, $H_{\text{products}} > H_{\text{reactants}}$, making the value of $\Delta H$ positive.

steps.title: Conclude reaction type
steps.content:
Because the enthalpy change ($\Delta H$) is positive, it means energy has been absorbed from the surroundings into the system. 

Therefore, the decomposition of calcium carbonate is an **endothermic** reaction. 

This concept is a key part of **Topic R1.1: Measuring enthalpy change**. Understanding energy profiles is essential for identifying how energy moves between a system and its surroundings.

parts.content: Calculate the enthalpy change $\Delta H$ for the reaction if the products are $178\text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$ higher in energy than the reactants.
parts.markscheme:
$\Delta H = +178\text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$ <marks>A1</marks>

[1 mark]

steps.title: Define enthalpy change
steps.content:
The enthalpy change, $\Delta H$, is defined as the difference between the total enthalpy of the products and the total enthalpy of the reactants. This is a fundamental concept in thermochemistry, which you can find in **Topic R1.1: Measuring enthalpy change**.

$$\Delta H = H_{\text{products}} - H_{\text{reactants}}$$

steps.title: Analyze the energy difference
steps.content:
From the question, we are told that the products are higher in energy than the reactants. When the energy of the products is greater than the energy of the reactants, the system must absorb energy from the surroundings. This means the reaction is **endothermic**, and the value of $\Delta H$ will be positive.

$$\begin{aligned} H_{\text{products}} &> H_{\text{reactants}} \\ \Delta H &= +178 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Simple identification of the numerical value.

ti84.instructions: Simple identification of the numerical value.

desmos.instructions: Simple identification of the numerical value.

calculator.description: Confirming the magnitude of the difference as stated in the question.

highlights.text: higher in energy than the reactants

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Because the products are exactly $178\text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$ above the reactants, we simply apply the positive sign to denote an endothermic process.

$$\Delta H = +178\text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$$

steps.title: Analyze the energy profile
steps.content:
Looking at the provided energy profile diagram, we can see that the energy level of the products ($\text{CaO} + \text{CO}_2$) is higher than the energy level of the reactant ($\text{CaCO}_3$). This visual gap represents the change in enthalpy, $\Delta H$.

This concept is covered in **Topic R1.1: Measuring enthalpy change**.

steps.title: Determine the reaction type
steps.content:
When the products have more energy than the reactants, the system must have absorbed energy from the surroundings. This signifies an **endothermic reaction**. 

In thermodynamics, the change in enthalpy is defined as:
$$\Delta H = H_{\text{products}} - H_{\text{reactants}}$$

Because $H_{\text{products}} > H_{\text{reactants}}$, the value of $\Delta H$ must be **positive**.

highlights.text: products are 178 kJ mol⁻¹ higher in energy than the reactants

steps.title: State the final enthalpy
steps.content:
The question specifies that the products are $178\text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$ higher than the reactants. Combining the magnitude ($178$) with the positive sign required for an endothermic process, we get:

$$\Delta H = +178\text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$$

Note: In IB Chemistry, it is crucial to include the **+ sign** for endothermic reactions to clearly communicate the direction of energy flow.

parts.content: State one change that could be made to the conditions to increase the rate of this reaction, and explain your answer.
parts.markscheme:
Increase temperature. <marks>A1</marks>
More particles have energy $\ge E_{\text{a}}$ / higher frequency of successful collisions. <marks>R1</marks>

**OR**

Increase surface area / use powdered $\text{CaCO}_3$. <marks>A1</marks>
More particles are exposed to collisions / higher frequency of collisions. <marks>R1</marks>

[2 marks]

steps.title: Select the condition change
steps.content: To increase the rate of the decomposition of calcium carbonate, one effective change we can make is to **increase the temperature**.

steps.title: Link temperature to energy
steps.content:
According to the **Kinetic Molecular Theory** (which you can review in **Topic S1.1.3**), the temperature of a substance is a measure of the average kinetic energy of its particles. 

When we increase the temperature, the $\text{CaCO}_3$ particles gain kinetic energy and move more vigorously.

steps.title: Overcome activation energy
steps.content:
For a reaction to occur, colliding particles must have energy equal to or greater than the **activation energy** ($E_a$). Looking at your energy profile diagram, $E_a$ is the height of the energy barrier that reactants must climb.

By increasing the temperature, a significantly **greater proportion of particles** will possess kinetic energy $E \ge E_a$.

steps.title: Relate to collision frequency
steps.content:
Because more particles meet the energy requirement, there is a **higher frequency of successful collisions**. This relationship is mathematically shown in the **Arrhenius equation** found in your Data Booklet:

$$k=A e^{\frac{-E_{a}}{R T}}$$

As the temperature ($T$) in the denominator of the exponent increases, the rate constant ($k$) increases exponentially, leading to a much faster reaction rate.

steps.title: Final answer summary
steps.content:
To get full marks on this question, make sure to state both the change and the reasoning:

1.  **Change:** Increase the temperature.
2.  **Explanation:** Increasing temperature increases the average kinetic energy of the particles, so a greater proportion of particles have energy $\ge E_a$, leading to a higher frequency of successful collisions.

steps.title: Identify the reactant state
steps.content:
To increase the rate of reaction, we look at the physical state of the reactants. In this equation, calcium carbonate is in the solid state:

$$\text{CaCO}_3(\text{s}) \to \text{CaO}(\text{s}) + \text{CO}_{2}(\text{g})$$

Because it is a solid, the reaction can only occur at the surface where the particles are in contact with the surroundings.

steps.title: Increase the surface area
steps.content: One effective way to increase the rate is to **increase the surface area** of the solid reactant. This can be done by grinding the large pieces of calcium carbonate into a fine powder.

highlights.text: increase the rate of this reaction

steps.title: Apply collision theory
steps.content:
According to **collision theory** (which you can review in **Topic R2.2**), reactions occur when particles collide with sufficient energy. 

By using a powder, more calcium carbonate particles are exposed at the surface and available to react. This leads to a **higher frequency of collisions**, which directly increases the reaction rate.

steps.title: Final Answer Summary
steps.content:
To maximize your marks, ensure you state both the change and the reason:

*   **Change:** Increase the surface area of the calcium carbonate (e.g., use powdered $\text{CaCO}_3$).
*   **Explanation:** More particles are exposed to collisions, leading to a higher frequency of collisions.

steps.title: Identify the change
steps.content: To increase the rate of the decomposition of calcium carbonate, one effective method is the **addition of a catalyst**.

steps.title: Explain the mechanism
steps.content:
A catalyst works by providing an **alternative reaction pathway** that has a **lower activation energy ($E_a$)** than the original uncatalyzed reaction. 

In the energy profile diagram provided, the activation energy is the energy gap between the reactants ($\text{CaCO}_3$) and the peak of the curve.

steps.title: Apply collision theory
steps.content:
According to collision theory, for a reaction to occur, particles must collide with energy greater than or equal to the activation energy. 

By lowering the $E_a$ barrier, a **greater proportion of particles** now possess sufficient kinetic energy to react at the same temperature.

steps.title: Connect to reaction rate
steps.content:
Because more particles can successfully overcome the lower energy barrier, there is a **higher frequency of successful collisions** per unit time. This results in a faster rate of reaction.

Mathematically, we can see this in the **Arrhenius equation** from your data booklet:

$$k = A e^{\frac{-E_a}{RT}}$$

When the activation energy ($E_a$) decreases, the value of the rate constant ($k$) increases, which directly increases the reaction rate.

parts.content: Suggest how the addition of a catalyst would affect the energy profile diagram.
parts.markscheme:
The activation energy would be lowered / a new reaction pathway with lower $E_{\text{a}}$ is provided. <marks>A1</marks>

The peak of the energy profile diagram would be lower. <marks>A1</marks>

[2 marks]

steps.title: Define the Catalyst's Role
steps.content: In chemistry, a catalyst is a substance that increases the rate of a chemical reaction without being permanently changed itself. To understand how it affects the energy profile, we focus on **Topic R2.2.5 (Catalysts)** in the syllabus. The main concept here is that a catalyst provides an **alternative reaction pathway**.

steps.title: Lowering Activation Energy
steps.content:
By providing this alternative pathway, the catalyst reduces the energy barrier that reactants must overcome to turn into products. This means the **activation energy ($E_{\text{a}}$) is lowered**. 

This relationship is mathematically shown in the Arrhenius equation found in your data booklet:

$$k=A e^{\frac{-E_{a}}{RT}}$$

A smaller $E_{\text{a}}$ results in a larger rate constant ($k$), making the reaction faster.

steps.title: Effect on the Diagram
steps.content:
On the energy profile diagram, the activation energy is the vertical distance from the reactants' energy level to the top of the curve. 

Because the catalyst lowers the activation energy, the **peak of the energy profile diagram would be lower** (the 'hump' becomes smaller). Note that the energy levels of the reactants and products remain exactly the same; only the path between them changes.

highlights.text: The peak of the energy profile diagram would be lower.

steps.title: Identify the catalyst's role
steps.content: A catalyst increases the rate of a chemical reaction by providing an alternative reaction pathway. This new pathway requires less energy for the reactants to transform into products. This concept is explored in **Topic R2.2: How fast? The rate of chemical change**.

steps.title: Lowering the activation energy
steps.content:
The primary effect of the catalyst is to reduce the **activation energy** ($E_{\text{a}}$). This is the minimum energy that colliding particles must possess for a reaction to occur. On your diagram, this corresponds to a shorter distance between the reactant energy level and the top of the curve.

According to the markscheme, stating that the activation energy is lowered or a new pathway is provided earns you the first mark.

steps.title: Analyzing relative energetics
steps.content:
When using the **Relative Energetics Comparison** approach, we must consider what does *not* change. A catalyst does not alter the chemical identity or potential energy of the reactants ($\text{CaCO}_3$) or the products ($\text{CaO}$ and $\text{CO}_2$). 

Because these levels remain the same, the overall enthalpy change ($\Delta H$) of the reaction is unaffected. You can see the relationship for enthalpy change in your data booklet:

$$\Delta H^{\ominus}=\Sigma(\Delta H_{\mathrm{f}}^{\ominus} \text{ products})-\Sigma(\Delta H_{\mathrm{f}}^{\ominus} \text{ reactants})$$

Since the standard enthalpies of formation ($\Delta H_{\mathrm{f}}^{\ominus}$) for the substances are constant, the vertical gap between the start and end of the graph remains unchanged.

steps.title: Describing the visual change
steps.content:
To earn the second mark, you must describe how this looks on the graph. Because the activation energy is lower but the reactant/product levels are the same, the **peak of the energy profile diagram would be lower**. 

Imagine the original 'hill' being squashed down while its base (the horizontal lines for reactants and products) stays exactly where it is.

questions.specification:
A reaction has the rate law: $\text{Rate} = k[\mathrm{X}]^2$

The following data were collected at constant temperature:

| [X] / $\mathrm{mol\,dm^{-3}}$ | Rate / $\mathrm{mol\,dm^{-3}\,s^{-1}}$ |
| :--- | :--- |
| 0.10 | $1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ |
| 0.20 | $4.0 \times 10^{-3}$ |
| 0.40 | $1.6 \times 10^{-2}$ |

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Reactivity 2. How much, how fast and how far? > R2.2 How fast? The rate of chemical change > R2.2.9 Rate equations (Higher Level Only)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: R2.2.9 Rate equations (Higher Level Only)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Uses a given rate law Rate = k[X]^2 and tabulated concentration–rate data to confirm reaction order and predict rates, which is application of rate equations.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Reactivity 2. How much, how fast and how far? > R2.2 How fast? The rate of chemical change > R2.2.10 Order of a reaction (Higher Level Only)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: R2.2.10 Order of a reaction (Higher Level Only)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (b) explicitly asks to confirm the order with respect to X from experimental data (doubling [X] quadruples rate), i.e., determining reaction order.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Reactivity 2. How much, how fast and how far? > R2.2 How fast? The rate of chemical change > R2.2.11 Rate constant (Higher Level Only)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: R2.2.11 Rate constant (Higher Level Only)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (c) requires calculating the rate constant k from the rate law and data, including correct units.

parts.content: Confirm the order of reaction with respect to X using data from the table.
parts.markscheme:
Doubling $[\mathrm{X}]$ (e.g., from $0.10$ to $0.20\ \mathrm{mol\,dm^{-3}}$) causes the rate to quadruple (e.g., from $1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ to $4.0 \times 10^{-3}\ \mathrm{mol\,dm^{-3}\,s^{-1}}$). <marks>1</marks>

As $2^2 = 4$, this confirms that the reaction is second-order with respect to X. <marks>1</marks>

steps.title: Select experiments for comparison
steps.content:
To determine the reaction order using the **Comparison by Inspection** method, we first select two experiments from the data table where the concentration of $[\mathrm{X}]$ changes. 

Let's compare the first two experiments:
- Experiment 1: $[\mathrm{X}] = 0.10 \text{ mol dm}^{-3}$
- Experiment 2: $[\mathrm{X}] = 0.20 \text{ mol dm}^{-3}$

steps.title: Inspect concentration change factor
steps.content:
Next, we calculate the factor by which the concentration has changed between our two selected experiments.

$$\text{Concentration Factor} = \frac{0.20 \text{ mol dm}^{-3}}{0.10 \text{ mol dm}^{-3}} = 2$$

This shows that the concentration of $\mathrm{X}$ has been **doubled**.

gdc.instructions: Divide the second concentration by the first.

ti84.instructions: Enter the final concentration divided by the initial concentration.

desmos.instructions: Divide the values directly.

calculator.description: Calculate the factor of change for the concentration.

steps.title: Inspect rate change factor
steps.content:
Now, we look at how the rate changed over that same interval:
- Rate 1: $1.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mol dm}^{-3} \text{ s}^{-1}$
- Rate 2: $4.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mol dm}^{-3} \text{ s}^{-1}$

$$\text{Rate Factor} = \frac{4.0 \times 10^{-3}}{1.0 \times 10^{-3}} = 4$$

The rate has **quadrupled**.

gdc.instructions: Input the division of the two rates.

ti84.instructions: Divide the second rate by the first rate.

desmos.instructions: Divide the rate values.

calculator.description: Calculate the factor of change for the reaction rate.

steps.title: Determine reaction order
steps.content:
By inspection, we observe the relationship between the concentration factor ($2$) and the rate factor ($4$). 

In a rate law $\text{Rate} = k[\mathrm{X}]^n$, the relationship is:
$$\text{Concentration Factor}^n = \text{Rate Factor}$$

Substituting our values:
$$2^n = 4$$

Since $2^2 = 4$, the order of reaction ($n$) with respect to $\mathrm{X}$ is **2** (second-order). This matches the given rate law in the question specification: $\text{Rate} = k[\mathrm{X}]^2$.

gdc.instructions: Verify 2 to the power of 2.

ti84.instructions: Check that 2 squared equals 4.

desmos.instructions: Check the power or use logarithms: log(4)/log(2).

calculator.description: Verify the relationship between factors or solve for the exponent.

steps.title: Define the rate expression
steps.content:
To find the order of reaction with respect to $\mathrm{X}$, we start with the general rate law expression where $n$ represents the order of the reaction:

$$\text{Rate} = k[\mathrm{X}]^n$$

steps.title: Set up the ratio
steps.content:
Using the **Algebraic Ratio Method**, we can compare two experiments from the table. Let's compare Experiment 1 and Experiment 2. We set up a ratio of their rate expressions:

$$\frac{\text{Rate}_2}{\text{Rate}_1} = \frac{k[\mathrm{X}]_2^n}{k[\mathrm{X}]_1^n}$$

Notice that the rate constant $k$ cancels out because the temperature remains constant.

steps.title: Substitute values and simplify
steps.content:
Substitute the values from the table into our ratio equation:

$$\frac{4.0 \times 10^{-3}}{1.0 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{(0.20)^n}{(0.10)^n}$$

Simplifying both sides, we get:

$$4 = (2)^n$$

gdc.instructions: Divide the second rate by the first rate, and divide the second concentration by the first concentration.

ti84.instructions: Enter (4.0 * 10^-3) / (1.0 * 10^-3) to find the rate ratio, then enter 0.20 / 0.10 for the concentration ratio.

desmos.instructions: Input the division for both sides of the equation to see the numerical simplification.

calculator.description: Calculate the ratio of rates and the ratio of concentrations to simplify the expression.

steps.title: Solve for the order
steps.content:
We now solve for $n$ in the equation $4 = 2^n$. 

Since $2^2 = 4$, we can see that $n = 2$.

This confirms that the reaction is **second-order** with respect to $\mathrm{X}$. This matches the rate law provided in the question specification.

gdc.instructions: Use the power function to confirm that 2^2 = 4.

ti84.instructions: Enter 2^2 and press ENTER to confirm it equals 4.

desmos.instructions: Type 2^2 to confirm the result.

calculator.description: Verify the value of n by calculating 2 raised to the power of 2.

steps.title: Final answer summary
steps.content:
By showing that doubling the concentration $[\mathrm{X}]$ results in a four-fold increase in the rate ($2^2 = 4$), we have confirmed the reaction is second-order.

**Marking points:**
- Correct identification that doubling $[\mathrm{X}]$ quadruples the rate (1 mark).
- Solving the algebraic relationship $2^n = 4$ to find $n = 2$ (1 mark).

steps.title: The Rate Constant Consistency approach
steps.content:
To confirm the reaction order using the **Rate Constant Consistency** approach, we calculate the rate constant ($k$) for different sets of data assuming the given rate law is correct. If the calculated values of $k$ are identical (or very close), it confirms that the assumed order of reaction is correct. 

This concept is covered in **Topic R2.2: How fast? The rate of chemical change**.

steps.title: Rearrange the rate equation
steps.content:
The question provides the second-order rate law. We first rearrange this equation to solve for the rate constant $k$:

$$\text{Rate} = k[\text{X}]^2$$

$$\begin{aligned} k &= \frac{\text{Rate}}{[\text{X}]^2} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate k values
steps.content:
Now, we substitute the values from the first two rows of the data table into our rearranged equation to see if $k$ remains constant.

**For the first data point ($[\text{X}] = 0.10$):**
$$k_1 = \frac{1.0 \times 10^{-3}}{(0.10)^2} = 0.10 \, \text{dm}^3\,\text{mol}^{-1}\,s^{-1}$$

**For the second data point ($[\text{X}] = 0.20$):**
$$k_2 = \frac{4.0 \times 10^{-3}}{(0.20)^2} = 0.10 \, \text{dm}^3\,\text{mol}^{-1}\,s^{-1}$$

gdc.instructions: Evaluate the expression for both trials to find the value of k.

desmos.instructions: Type the expressions to verify that both results are 0.1.

calculator.description: Calculate k for the first and second data points to check for consistency.

steps.title: Confirm the reaction order
steps.content:
Because the calculated rate constant $k$ is consistent ($k = 0.10$) for different concentrations, the assumption that the reaction is **second-order** with respect to X is correct.

In an IB exam, showing that the rate quadruples ($2^2$) when the concentration doubles is the most common way to earn these 2 marks, but the consistency of $k$ is the underlying mathematical proof!

parts.content: Determine the value and units of the rate constant, $k$.
parts.markscheme:
$k = \dfrac{\text{Rate}}{[\mathrm{X}]^2} = \dfrac{1.0 \times 10^{-3}}{(0.10)^2} = 0.10$ <marks>1</marks>

Units: $\mathrm{mol^{-1}\,dm^3\,s^{-1}}$ <marks>1</marks>

<note>Award **[1]** for the correct value and **[1]** for the correct units.</note>

steps.title: Rearrange for the rate constant
steps.content:
To find the rate constant $k$, we start by rearranging the given rate law expression to isolate $k$. 

Starting with:
$$\text{Rate} = k[\mathrm{X}]^2$$

We divide both sides by $[\mathrm{X}]^2$:
$$k = \frac{\text{Rate}}{[\mathrm{X}]^2}$$

steps.title: Substitute numerical values
steps.content:
Now, we can pick any row of data from the table and substitute the values into our rearranged equation. Let's use the first row:
- $[\mathrm{X}] = 0.10 \text{ mol dm}^{-3}$
- $\text{Rate} = 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mol dm}^{-3} \text{ s}^{-1}$

Substituting these into the formula:
$$k = \frac{1.0 \times 10^{-3}}{(0.10)^2}$$

Calculating the denominator first:
$$(0.10)^2 = 0.01$$

So:
$$k = \frac{0.001}{0.01} = 0.10$$

gdc.instructions: Type (1.0e-3) / (0.10^2) in the calculation screen.

ti84.instructions: Enter (1.0 * 10^-3) / (0.10^2) and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Input the fraction with 1.0 * 10^-3 in the numerator and 0.10^2 in the denominator.

calculator.description: Calculate the value of k using Row 1 data.

steps.title: Determine the units of k
steps.content:
Units for the rate constant change depending on the overall order of the reaction. We find them by substituting the units into the expression for $k$:

$$\text{units of } k = \frac{\text{units of Rate}}{(\text{units of concentration})^2}$$

$$\text{units of } k = \frac{\text{mol dm}^{-3} \text{ s}^{-1}}{(\text{mol dm}^{-3})^2}$$

Expand the denominator:
$$\text{units of } k = \frac{\text{mol dm}^{-3} \text{ s}^{-1}}{\text{mol}^2 \text{ dm}^{-6}}$$

Simplify the powers:
- For mol: $1 - 2 = -1 \rightarrow \text{mol}^{-1}$
- For dm: $-3 - (-6) = +3 \rightarrow \text{dm}^3$
- For s: remains $\text{s}^{-1}$

This gives us the units: $\text{mol}^{-1} \text{ dm}^3 \text{ s}^{-1}$.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Combining the value and the units, the rate constant is:

$$k = 0.10 \text{ mol}^{-1} \text{ dm}^3 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

This question relates to **Topic R2.2: How fast? The rate of chemical change**. Understanding how to derive units for different reaction orders is a key skill for IB Chemistry Higher Level.

parts.content: Predict the rate if $[\mathrm{X}] = 0.60\ \mathrm{mol\,dm^{-3}}$.
parts.markscheme: $\text{Rate} = k[\mathrm{X}]^2 = 0.10 \times (0.60)^2 = 3.6 \times 10^{-2}\ (\mathrm{mol\,dm^{-3}\,s^{-1}})$ <marks>1</marks>

steps.title: Identify the rate law
steps.content:
To solve this using the **Calculating the Rate Constant** method, we first look at the given rate law. The problem states that the reaction follow a specific mathematical relationship where the rate depends on the square of the concentration of $X$. 

This concept is covered in **Topic R2.2: How fast? The rate of chemical change** (specifically section R2.2.9).

steps.title: Calculate the rate constant
steps.content:
We can determine the value of the rate constant, $k$, by substituting the experimental data from any row of the table into the rate law. Let's use the first set of data:
- $[X] = 0.10\ \text{mol dm}^{-3}$
- $\text{Rate} = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{mol dm}^{-3}\text{s}^{-1}$

Rearranging the rate law to solve for $k$:

$$k = \frac{\text{Rate}}{[X]^2}$$

Substituting the values:

$$k = \frac{1.0 \times 10^{-3}}{(0.10)^2} = 0.10$$

gdc.instructions: In the calculation menu, divide 0.001 by 0.10 squared.

ti84.instructions: Enter the expression (1.0 * 10^-3) / (0.10^2) on the home screen and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type the fraction (1.0 * 10^-3) / (0.10^2) into a new line.

calculator.description: Calculate the rate constant k using the first row of data.

steps.title: Predict the new rate
steps.content:
Now that we have the rate constant $k = 0.10$, we can predict the rate for the target concentration provided in the question. 

Substitute $k = 0.10$ and $[X] = 0.60\ \text{mol dm}^{-3}$ back into the original rate law equation:

$$\text{Rate} = k[X]^2$$
$$\text{Rate} = 0.10 \times (0.60)^2$$

gdc.instructions: Multiply 0.10 by 0.60 squared.

ti84.instructions: Enter 0.10 * (0.60^2) and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Enter 0.10 * 0.60^2 in a new cell.

calculator.description: Calculate the final rate for [X] = 0.60.

highlights.text: Predict the rate if [X] = 0.60 mol dm^-3

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Calculating the final value:

$$\text{Rate} = 0.10 \times 0.36 = 0.036\ \text{mol dm}^{-3}\text{s}^{-1}$$

In scientific notation, this is expressed as:

$$\text{Rate} = 3.6 \times 10^{-2}\ \text{mol dm}^{-3}\text{s}^{-1}$$

According to the markscheme, you earn **1 mark** for the correct final numerical value and units.

steps.title: Identify the proportionality relationship
steps.content: The question provides the rate law: $$\text{Rate} = k[\text{X}]^2$$. This tells us that the rate of reaction is directly proportional to the **square** of the concentration of $\text{X}$. This is a concept covered in **Topic R2.2: How fast? The rate of chemical change**.

steps.title: Select a reference trial
steps.content:
To use the ratio and proportionality approach, we first pick a known data point from the table. Let's use the first trial:
- Concentration $[\text{X}]_1 = 0.10\ \text{mol dm}^{-3}$
- Rate$_1 = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{mol dm}^{-3}\text{s}^{-1}$

steps.title: Calculate the scaling factor
steps.content:
Compare our target concentration ($0.60\ \text{mol dm}^{-3}$) to our reference concentration ($0.10\ \text{mol dm}^{-3}$) to find the scaling factor ($n$):

$$n = \frac{0.60\ \text{mol dm}^{-3}}{0.10\ \text{mol dm}^{-3}} = 6$$

The concentration has increased by a factor of 6.

ti84.instructions: Enter 0.60 / 0.10 and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type 0.60 / 0.10 in a new expression line.

calculator.description: Calculate the ratio of the target concentration to the reference concentration.

steps.title: Determine the rate change
steps.content:
Because the reaction is second order ($[\text{X}]^2$), the rate changes by the **square** of the concentration scaling factor ($n^2$).

$$\text{Rate factor} = n^2 = 6^2 = 36$$

This means the new rate will be 36 times larger than our reference rate.

gdc.instructions: Enter 6^2.

ti84.instructions: Enter 6^2 and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type 6^2 in a new expression line.

calculator.description: Calculate the square of the scaling factor.

steps.title: Calculate the predicted rate
steps.content:
Finally, multiply the reference rate by this factor to find the new rate:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{New Rate} &= \text{Rate}_1 \times 36 \\ &= (1.0 \times 10^{-3}) \times 36 \\ &= 3.6 \times 10^{-2}\ \text{mol dm}^{-3}\text{s}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

This matches the result we would get using any other trial (e.g., using $0.20$ as a reference, the factor is $3$, so $4.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 3^2 = 3.6 \times 10^{-2}$).

ti84.instructions: Enter 1.0E-3 * 36 and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Calculate the final predicted rate.

questions.specification:
The decomposition of hydrogen peroxide follows this rate expression:
$\text{Rate} = k[\mathrm{H_2O_2}]$

labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (a) asks to deduce reaction order from the given rate law (Rate = k[H2O2]), which directly tests determining reaction order from concentration dependence.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Reactivity 2. How much, how fast and how far? > R2.2 How fast? The rate of chemical change > R2.2.9 Rate equations (Higher Level Only)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: R2.2.9 Rate equations (Higher Level Only)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question is built around interpreting a rate expression (rate equation) and using it to infer kinetics behavior.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Reactivity 2. How much, how fast and how far? > R2.2 How fast? The rate of chemical change > R2.2.11 Rate constant (Higher Level Only)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: R2.2.11 Rate constant (Higher Level Only)

parts.content: Deduce the order of the reaction with respect to $\mathrm{H_2O_2}$.
parts.markscheme:
First order
<marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Identify the rate expression
steps.content:
First, let's look at the rate expression provided in the question. This equation shows how the speed of the reaction depends on the concentration of the reactants.

$$\text{Rate} = k[\mathrm{H_2O_2}]$$

steps.title: Inspect the exponent
steps.content:
In chemistry, the **order of reaction** with respect to a specific reactant is the power (exponent) to which its concentration term is raised in the rate equation. 

Looking at the expression:
$$\text{Rate} = k[\mathrm{H_2O_2}]^1$$

Even though the number "1" isn't explicitly written, mathematically, any term without a visible exponent is understood to be raised to the power of 1.

steps.title: Determine the order
steps.content:
Because the exponent for $[\mathrm{H_2O_2}]$ is 1, we can conclude that the reaction is **first order** with respect to hydrogen peroxide. 

This means if you were to double the concentration of $[\mathrm{H_2O_2}]$, the rate of the reaction would also double.

parts.content: Describe a graphical method to confirm this order experimentally.
parts.markscheme:
- Plot $\ln[\mathrm{H_2O_2}]$ vs time
<marks>1 mark</marks>
- Straight line with negative gradient confirms first order
<marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Identify the reaction order
steps.content:
The provided rate expression shows that the rate depends on the concentration of $\mathrm{H_2O_2}$ raised to the power of 1. 

$$\text{Rate} = k[\mathrm{H_2O_2}]^1$$

This tells us the reaction is **first order** with respect to hydrogen peroxide. To confirm this experimentally using a concentration-time graph, we use the integrated rate law for first-order reactions.

steps.title: Apply the integrated law
steps.content:
For a first-order reaction, the relationship between concentration and time is given by the integrated rate law:

$$\ln[\mathrm{H_2O_2}]_t = -kt + \ln[\mathrm{H_2O_2}]_0$$

This equation follows the form of a straight line, $$y = mx + c$$, where:
- $y$ is $\ln[\mathrm{H_2O_2}]$
- $x$ is the time ($t$)
- $m$ (the gradient) is $-k$

steps.title: Plot the graph
steps.content:
To confirm the first-order kinetics, you should collect concentration data over time and **plot a graph of $\ln[\mathrm{H_2O_2}]$ (y-axis) against time (x-axis)**.

This step earns the first mark in the markscheme.

steps.title: Analyze the result
steps.content:
If the reaction is indeed first order, the resulting plot will be a **straight line (linear)** with a **negative gradient**.

- The linearity confirms the order is 1.
- The gradient of this line is equal to $-k$ (the negative of the rate constant).

steps.title: Summary for marks
steps.content:
To get full marks on this question, your description must include:
1. The specific variables to plot: **$\ln[\mathrm{H_2O_2}]$ vs. time**.
2. The expected observation: A **straight line** with a **negative gradient**.

steps.title: Analyze the rate expression
steps.content:
The question provides the rate expression for the decomposition of hydrogen peroxide: 

$$\text{Rate} = k[\mathrm{H_2O_2}]$$

This expression shows that the reaction rate is directly proportional to the concentration of $\mathrm{H_2O_2}$ raised to the power of 1. This identifies the reaction as **first-order** with respect to $\mathrm{H_2O_2}$. To confirm this graphically, we can use a **Rate-Concentration plot**.

steps.title: Identify the graph axes
steps.content:
To create a Rate-Concentration plot, you need to measure the initial rate of reaction at several different initial concentrations of $\mathrm{H_2O_2}$. 

On your graph, you would plot:
- **Y-axis**: Reaction Rate (e.g., in $\text{mol dm}^{-3} \text{s}^{-1}$)
- **X-axis**: Concentration of $[\mathrm{H_2O_2}]$ (e.g., in $\text{mol dm}^{-3}$)

steps.title: Interpret the resulting line
steps.content:
Since the rate expression follows the linear form $y = mx$ (where $y$ is the rate, $x$ is the concentration, and $m$ is the rate constant $k$), a first-order reaction will produce a **straight line passing through the origin**.

If the experimental data points form a linear trend starting at $(0,0)$, it confirms that the rate is directly proportional to the concentration, verifying the first-order kinetics.

steps.title: Summary for marks
steps.content:
In an IB exam, even though the markscheme often focuses on concentration-time or log-concentration-time graphs, the rate-concentration plot is a valid and commonly used alternative. 

To earn the marks using this approach, you must clearly state:
1. Plot **Rate** against **Concentration**.
2. A **straight line through the origin** confirms the first-order relationship.

steps.title: Identify the reaction order
steps.content:
The provided rate expression is:

$$\text{Rate} = k[\mathrm{H_2O_2}]$$

Since the concentration of $\mathrm{H_2O_2}$ is raised to the power of 1, this is a **first-order reaction**. To confirm this experimentally using a graphical method, we can analyze the relationship between concentration and time.

steps.title: Plot concentration against time
steps.content:
The first step in a **Half-life Consistency Analysis** is to plot the concentration of the reactant, $[\mathrm{H_2O_2}]$, on the y-axis against time ($t$) on the x-axis. 

For a first-order reaction, this graph will show an exponential decay curve. To confirm the order, we look for a unique property of first-order kinetics: the **constant half-life** ($t_{1/2}$). This is the time taken for the concentration to decrease by exactly half.

steps.title: Verify constant half-life
steps.content:
On your graph of $[\mathrm{H_2O_2}]$ vs. time, pick several starting concentrations and measure how long it takes for each to halve. 

- Time for $1.0 \text{ mol dm}^{-3}$ to reach $0.5 \text{ mol dm}^{-3}$
- Time for $0.5 \text{ mol dm}^{-3}$ to reach $0.25 \text{ mol dm}^{-3}$

If these time intervals are equal (constant), it confirms the reaction is **first order**. This is a hallmark of first-order reactions, as the half-life is independent of the initial concentration ($t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{k}$).

gdc.instructions: Graph the function f(x) = 2^(-x). Observe that the value of y halves every time x increases by 1 unit. This constant horizontal distance between 'halving points' illustrates a constant half-life.

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [Y=].
2. Enter Y1 = 2^(-X) to simulate a first-order decay where the half-life is 1 unit of time.
3. Press [GRAPH].
4. Use [TRACE] to see that at X=1, Y=0.5; at X=2, Y=0.25; at X=3, Y=0.125.

desmos.instructions: Graph y = e^{-0.1x}. Observe how long it takes for y to go from 1 to 0.5, then from 0.5 to 0.25. The time interval (x-axis distance) remains constant.

calculator.description: Visualize the decay and constant half-life on a graph.

steps.title: Align with exam requirements
steps.content:
While measuring half-lives on a curve is a valid analysis, IB markschemes often look for the **linearized** version of this method to confirm consistency more accurately. 

By taking the natural logarithm of the concentration, the exponential decay is transformed into a linear relationship. 

1. Plot **$\ln[\mathrm{H_2O_2}]$ vs. time**.
2. A **straight line with a negative gradient** confirms that the half-life is constant and the reaction is first order.

highlights.text: Straight line with negative gradient confirms first order

questions.specification:
The reaction between $\mathrm{NO}$ and $\mathrm{H_2}$ has the following mechanism:

Step 1: $2\mathrm{NO} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{N_2O_2}$ (fast)

Step 2: $\mathrm{N_2O_2} + \mathrm{H_2} \rightarrow \mathrm{N_2O} + \mathrm{H_2O}$ (slow)

Step 3: $\mathrm{N_2O} + \mathrm{H_2} \rightarrow \mathrm{N_2} + \mathrm{H_2O}$ (fast)

parts.content: Identify the rate-determining step.
parts.markscheme:
- Identify Step 2 as the rate-determining step. <marks>1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Define rate-determining step
steps.content:
In chemistry, a reaction mechanism often consists of several elementary steps. The **rate-determining step (RDS)** is defined as the slowest step in the sequence. Much like a bottleneck in a pipe, the overall speed of the reaction cannot be faster than this slowest step.

This concept is covered in **Topic R2.2.6: Reaction mechanisms and rate-determining steps**.

steps.title: Identify the 'slow' label
steps.content:
To find the rate-determining step using the **Slow Step Identification** approach, we simply look at the descriptions provided for each step in the mechanism. 

We are looking for the step explicitly labeled as **(slow)**, as this indicates it has the highest activation energy and governs the overall rate of the reaction.

steps.title: Select the correct step
steps.content:
Looking at the provided mechanism:
- Step 1 is labeled as (fast)
- Step 2 is labeled as (slow)
- Step 3 is labeled as (fast)

Therefore, Step 2 is the rate-determining step.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The rate-determining step is **Step 2**.

In an IB exam, simply identifying the step correctly earns the mark.

parts.content: Write the rate expression for this reaction.
parts.markscheme:
- State the rate expression: $\text{rate} = k[\mathrm{N_2O_2}][\mathrm{H_2}]$ <marks>1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Identify the rate-determining step
steps.content:
In a multi-step reaction mechanism, the overall rate of the reaction is determined by the slowest step, known as the **rate-determining step (RDS)**. 

Looking at the provided mechanism, we can see that **Step 2** is labeled as the slow step.

steps.title: Write the rate expression
steps.content:
For an elementary step in a mechanism, the rate law is derived directly from the stoichiometry of its reactants. In Step 2, the reactants are one molecule of $\mathrm{N_2O_2}$ and one molecule of $\mathrm{H_2}$. 

Therefore, the rate expression based on this step is:

$$\text{rate} = k[\mathrm{N_2O_2}][\mathrm{H_2}]$$

This matches the requirement for the markscheme.

parts.content: Deduce the overall equation and identify any intermediate(s).
parts.markscheme:
- Deduce the overall equation by summing the steps and cancelling intermediates: $2\mathrm{NO} + 2\mathrm{H_2} \rightarrow \mathrm{N_2} + 2\mathrm{H_2O}$ <marks>1</marks>

- Identify $\mathrm{N_2O_2}$ as an intermediate. <marks>1</marks>

- Identify $\mathrm{N_2O}$ as an intermediate. <marks>1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Sum all chemical species
steps.content:
To find the overall reaction using the summation method, we first list all the reactants and products from the three elementary steps. 

**Reactants:**
- Step 1: $2\mathrm{NO}$
- Step 2: $\mathrm{N_2O_2}$ and $\mathrm{H_2}$
- Step 3: $\mathrm{N_2O}$ and $\mathrm{H_2}$

**Products:**
- Step 1: $\mathrm{N_2O_2}$
- Step 2: $\mathrm{N_2O}$ and $\mathrm{H_2O}$
- Step 3: $\mathrm{N_2}$ and $\mathrm{H_2O}$

Adding these together, we get a giant temporary equation:
$$2\mathrm{NO} + \mathrm{N_2O_2} + 2\mathrm{H_2} + \mathrm{N_2O} \rightarrow \mathrm{N_2O_2} + \mathrm{N_2O} + \mathrm{N_2} + 2\mathrm{H_2O}$$

steps.title: Cancel common species
steps.content:
Now, we look for species that appear on **both** the reactant side and the product side. These are species produced in one step and used up in a subsequent step. 

We can cancel out:
1. $\mathrm{N_2O_2}$: Found on both sides.
2. $\mathrm{N_2O}$: Found on both sides.

$$\require{cancel} 2\mathrm{NO} + \cancel{\mathrm{N_2O_2}} + 2\mathrm{H_2} + \cancel{\mathrm{N_2O}} \rightarrow \cancel{\mathrm{N_2O_2}} + \cancel{\mathrm{N_2O}} + \mathrm{N_2} + 2\mathrm{H_2O}$$

steps.title: State the overall equation
steps.content:
After cancelling the common species, we are left with the final balanced overall equation. This represents the total change that occurs during the reaction sequence.

$$2\mathrm{NO} + 2\mathrm{H_2} \rightarrow \mathrm{N_2} + 2\mathrm{H_2O}$$

steps.title: Identify the intermediates
steps.content:
In a reaction mechanism, **intermediates** are species that are produced in an early step and consumed in a later step. They do not appear in the final overall equation because they were cancelled out during our summation process.

Based on our cancellation in Step 1, the intermediates are:
- $\mathrm{N_2O_2}$
- $\mathrm{N_2O}$

parts.content: Explain why the rate expression depends on the concentration of $\mathrm{N_2O_2}$ even though it is an intermediate.
parts.markscheme:
- Explain that the rate of the reaction is determined by the slow step (Step 2), and $\mathrm{N_2O_2}$ is a reactant in this step. <marks>1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Identify the rate-determining step
steps.content:
In a multi-step reaction mechanism, the overall rate of the reaction is governed by the slowest step, known as the **rate-determining step (RDS)**. 

Looking at the provided mechanism, we can see that Step 2 is explicitly labeled as the slow step.

steps.title: Analyze the RDS reactants
steps.content:
The rate of the rate-determining step depends on the concentrations of the species reacting in that specific step. 

In Step 2:
$$\mathrm{N_2O_2} + \mathrm{H_2} \rightarrow \mathrm{N_2O} + \mathrm{H_2O}$$

Even though $\mathrm{N_2O_2}$ is an intermediate (it is produced in Step 1 and consumed in Step 2), it is a **reactant** in this slow step. Therefore, the frequency of collisions in the RDS—and consequently the overall reaction rate—is directly proportional to the concentration of $\mathrm{N_2O_2}$.

steps.title: Summarize the dependency
steps.content:
To earn the mark, you must clearly link the "slow step" to the presence of the intermediate in the rate expression. 

Since the overall rate is determined by Step 2, and $\mathrm{N_2O_2}$ is a reactant in that step, its concentration must appear in the rate expression.

highlights.text: Explain that the rate of the reaction is determined by the slow step (Step 2), and N_2O_2 is a reactant in this step.

labeledSubSubTopics.title: R2.2.3 Factors affecting the rate of reaction
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks how increasing temperature affects reaction rate; temperature is a key factor affecting rate.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Reactivity 2. How much, how fast and how far? > R2.2 How fast? The rate of chemical change > R2.2.2 Collision theory
labeledSubSubTopics.title: R2.2.2 Collision theory
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Correct option refers to increasing the frequency of successful collisions, which is explained by collision theory.

options.markscheme: The activation energy ($E_{\text{a}}$) is a constant for a specific reaction pathway and is not affected by temperature; it is only lowered by the addition of a catalyst.

options.content: It increases the frequency of collisions only
options.markscheme: While increasing temperature does increase the frequency of collisions, it also increases the proportion of particles with energy greater than or equal to the activation energy ($E \ge E_{\text{a}}$), so 'only' makes this statement incorrect.

options.content: It increases the frequency of successful collisions
options.markscheme: Increasing temperature increases both the frequency of collisions and the proportion of collisions that have energy $E \ge E_{\text{a}}$. Consequently, the frequency of successful (effective) collisions increases, which increases the reaction rate.
The rate constant ($k$) increases with temperature, as described by the Arrhenius equation: $k = A e^{-E_{\text{a}}/RT}$.

questions.specification:
Photosynthesis can be summarized by the chemical equation:

$$6 \mathrm{CO}_2(\mathrm{g}) + 6 \mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l}) \xrightarrow{\text{light energy}} \mathrm{C}_6\mathrm{H}_{12}\mathrm{O}_6(\mathrm{s}) + 6 \mathrm{O}_2(\mathrm{g})$$

The energy profile diagram shows the progress of this reaction, indicating the requirement of light energy and a high activation energy ($E_a$).

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/plots/bb4fe046-94ab-47e5-bc8f-20b717e283f3.png)

parts.content: Describe how activation energy relates to the energy profile diagram.
parts.markscheme:
- **State** that activation energy is the minimum energy required for reactants to reach the transition state <marks>A1</marks>
- **Identify** that on the diagram, it is the energy difference between the reactants and the peak (transition state) <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Define activation energy conceptually
steps.content: In chemistry, for a reaction to occur, reactant particles must collide with sufficient energy to break existing bonds. This **activation energy ($E_a$)** is defined as the minimum kinetic energy required for reactants to undergo a successful collision and reach the **transition state**.

steps.title: Identify $E_a$ on the graph
steps.content:
Visually, the activation energy is the "energy barrier" or the "hill" that the reactants must climb. On an energy profile diagram, it is the **vertical distance** measured from the energy level of the **reactants** to the **peak** of the curve (which represents the transition state).

$$\text{Activation Energy } (E_a) = \text{Energy of Transition State} - \text{Energy of Reactants}$$

steps.title: Relate to the photosynthesis diagram
steps.content: In the provided diagram for photosynthesis, you can see that the reactants ($6\mathrm{CO}_2$ and $6\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$) start at a lower energy level. To reach the products, they must first gain enough energy (from light) to reach the highest point on the graph. The difference between that starting line and the peak is the activation energy.

parts.content: Explain why photosynthesis is an endothermic process, referring to both enthalpy change and the energy diagram.
parts.markscheme:
- **Explain** that photosynthesis is endothermic because the products have a higher potential energy than the reactants <marks>A1</marks>
- **State** that the enthalpy change ($\Delta H$) is positive, indicating energy absorption <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Analyze energy levels
steps.content:
To understand why photosynthesis is endothermic, we first look at the vertical axis of the energy profile diagram, which represents **potential energy**. 

In the provided diagram, notice that the energy level of the **products** (glucose and oxygen) is significantly higher than the energy level of the **reactants** (carbon dioxide and water). This indicates that the products have a higher chemical potential energy than the starting materials.

highlights.text: products have a higher potential energy than the reactants

steps.title: Determine enthalpy change sign
steps.content:
The enthalpy change, denoted as $\Delta H$, represents the difference in energy between the products and the reactants. It is calculated as:

$$\Delta H = H_{\text{products}} - H_{\text{reactants}}$$

Because the energy of the products is greater than the energy of the reactants in this reaction, the value of $\Delta H$ must be **positive** ($ \Delta H > 0 $). This is a defining characteristic of an endothermic process.

steps.title: Explain energy absorption
steps.content:
For the reactants to reach the higher energy state of the products, the system must **absorb energy** from its surroundings. 

In photosynthesis, this energy is provided by sunlight (as indicated by "light energy" over the reaction arrow). Since energy is taken in from the environment to drive the reaction forward, the process is classified as **endothermic**.

highlights.text: enthalpy change (\Delta H) is positive, indicating energy absorption

parts.content: Explain the role of light energy in this reaction with reference to the activation energy.
parts.markscheme:
- **Explain** that light provides the energy required to overcome the activation energy barrier ($E_a$) <marks>A1</marks>
- **State** that this allows the reactants to reach the transition state and for the reaction to proceed <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Recall Collision Theory requirements
steps.content:
To understand this reaction, we first look at **Topic R2.2: Collision theory**. For a chemical reaction to occur, reactant particles must collide with a minimum amount of energy. This threshold is known as the **activation energy** ($E_a$). 

Without reaching this energy level, the reactant molecules ($\mathrm{CO}_2$ and $\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$) will simply bounce off each other without reacting.

steps.title: Light as an energy source
steps.content:
The energy profile diagram shows a significant barrier between the reactants and products. The **light energy** absorbed during photosynthesis provides the external energy needed for the reactants to overcome this **high activation energy ($E_a$)** barrier.

Basically, the light provides the "boost" needed to get the molecules moving or vibrating with enough energy to break their existing bonds.

steps.title: Reaching the transition state
steps.content: By providing the necessary energy to meet the $E_a$, light allows the reactants to reach the **transition state** (the peak of the curve). At this state, the old bonds are breaking and new bonds are beginning to form, allowing the reaction to proceed and ultimately produce glucose and oxygen.

steps.title: Analyze the energy profile
steps.content:
In this energy profile diagram, notice that the reactants (at the start) are at a lower energy level than the products. To transform into products, the reactants must first climb a large "hill" on the graph. This hill represents the **activation energy** ($E_a$), which is the minimum energy required for a chemical reaction to occur.

This concept is a key part of **Topic R2.2: Activation energy** in your syllabus.

steps.title: Overcome the energy barrier
steps.content: The first mark is awarded for explaining that light energy provides the specific energy input needed to overcome this barrier. In the diagram, the arrow labeled "light energy" indicates that the absorption of light gives the reactants enough energy to reach the top of the activation energy ($E_a$) curve.

steps.title: Reach the transition state
steps.content: The second mark is for stating the result of this energy input. By providing enough energy to overcome $E_a$, the light energy allows the reactants to reach the **transition state** at the peak of the curve. Once the reactants reach this state, bonds can break and reform, allowing the reaction to proceed and form the products.

parts.content: Describe how the energy profile diagram would change if an enzyme (biological catalyst) were used.
parts.markscheme:
- **State** that an enzyme would lower the activation energy, making the curve rise to a lower peak <marks>A1</marks>
- **State** that the overall energy change ($\Delta H$) remains the same <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Define the enzyme's role
steps.content: In chemistry, an enzyme acts as a biological catalyst. According to **Topic R2.2.5 (Catalysts)** in your syllabus, a catalyst works by providing an alternative reaction pathway that requires less energy to get started.

steps.title: Lowering the activation energy
steps.content: Because the enzyme provides a pathway with a lower **activation energy** ($E_a$), the curve on the energy profile diagram would not rise as high. This results in a **lower peak** compared to the original uncatalyzed reaction.

steps.title: Analyze the enthalpy change
steps.content: It is crucial to remember that a catalyst does not change the energy of the reactants or the energy of the products. Since the starting and ending energy levels remain the same, the **enthalpy change** ($\Delta H$) of the reaction is **unaffected**.

steps.title: Summarize for marks
steps.content:
To earn full marks on this question, you must mention both qualitative changes:
1. The peak of the curve is **lowered** (due to lower $E_a$).
2. The vertical distance between reactants and products ($\Delta H$) **remains the same**.

highlights.text: enthalpy change (\Delta H) remains the same

parts.content:
The Arrhenius equation is given by:

$$k = A e^{-\frac{E_a}{RT}}$$

Using this equation, explain how a decrease in $E_a$ by the enzyme affects the rate constant $k$ at constant temperature.
parts.markscheme:
- **Explain** that a decrease in $E_a$ makes the exponent $\left( -\frac{E_a}{RT} \right)$ less negative (or larger) <marks>M1</marks>
- **Conclude** that therefore, $k$ increases, meaning the reaction rate increases <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify the Arrhenius equation
steps.content:
To explain the effect of an enzyme on the rate constant $k$, we first look at the Arrhenius equation provided in the data booklet:

$$k=A e^{\frac{-E_{a}}{R T}}$$

Where:
- $k$ is the rate constant.
- $A$ is the pre-exponential factor.
- $E_a$ is the activation energy.
- $R$ is the gas constant.
- $T$ is the absolute temperature.

This formula is covered in **Topic R2.2: How fast? The rate of chemical change**.

steps.title: Analyze the exponent
steps.content:
An enzyme works by providing an alternative reaction pathway with a lower activation energy ($E_a$). 

If we keep the temperature ($T$) constant, a **decrease** in $E_a$ means that the fraction $\frac{E_a}{RT}$ becomes a smaller positive number. 

Consequently, the exponent term, which is the negative of this fraction $\left( -\frac{E_a}{RT} \right)$, becomes **less negative** (or mathematically larger).

highlights.text: decrease in $E_a$ makes the exponent $\left( -\frac{E_a}{RT} \right)$ less negative (or larger)

steps.title: Determine the effect on k
steps.content:
In the function $e^x$, as $x$ increases, the value of $e^x$ also increases. 

Since the exponent $\left( -\frac{E_a}{RT} \right)$ has become less negative (larger), the entire exponential factor $e^{-\frac{E_a}{RT}}$ increases. 

Because $k$ is directly proportional to this factor ($k = A \times e^{-\frac{E_a}{RT}}$), the value of the **rate constant $k$ increases**, which ultimately increases the overall reaction rate.

highlights.text: k increases, meaning the reaction rate increases

steps.title: Start with Arrhenius equation
steps.content:
To understand the relationship between the activation energy ($E_a$) and the rate constant ($k$), we begin with the exponential form of the Arrhenius equation provided in the question and your data booklet:

$$k = A e^{-\frac{E_a}{RT}}$$

This equation is a key part of **Topic R2.2: How fast? The rate of chemical change**.

steps.title: Apply logarithmic transformation
steps.content:
We can transform this into a linear form by taking the natural logarithm ($\ln$) of both sides of the equation. This makes it much easier to see the mathematical relationship:

$$\ln k = \ln A - \frac{E_a}{RT}$$

In this form, $\ln k$ is expressed as a constant ($\ln A$) minus a specific term ($\frac{E_a}{RT}$).

steps.title: Analyze the energy term
steps.content:
Now, let's look at what happens when an enzyme is added. An enzyme provides an alternative pathway with a lower activation energy ($E_a$). 

At a constant temperature ($T$), if $E_a$ decreases, the value of the entire term $\frac{E_a}{RT}$ also decreases. Since we are *subtracting* this term from $\ln A$, subtracting a smaller number results in a larger value for $\ln k$. 

Mathematically, the exponent term $-\frac{E_a}{RT}$ becomes **less negative** (or larger).

steps.title: Conclude the effect
steps.content:
Because $\ln k$ has increased, the rate constant $k$ itself must also increase. 

A larger rate constant $k$ directly corresponds to a faster reaction rate, which is why enzymes are so effective at speeding up biological processes like photosynthesis.

questions.specification:
The following reaction is exothermic:

$$\mathrm{A(g)} + \mathrm{B(g)} \rightarrow \mathrm{C(g)} + \text{heat}$$

labeledSubSubTopics.title: R2.2.2 Collision theory
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Parts (a)–(c) focus on how temperature changes reaction rate via Maxwell–Boltzmann distribution, collision frequency, and collision energy—core collision theory ideas.

labeledSubSubTopics.title: R2.2.3 Factors affecting the rate of reaction
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (a) directly asks how temperature affects reaction rate, which is a key factor affecting rate of reaction.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Structure 1. Models of the particulate nature of matter > S1.1 Introduction to the particulate nature of matter > S1.1.3 Temperature and Kinetic Energy
labeledSubSubTopics.title: S1.1.3 Temperature and Kinetic Energy
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Temperature’s effect is explained through changes in average kinetic energy and energy distribution of gas particles (Maxwell–Boltzmann), linking to temperature and kinetic energy.

parts.content: Describe how temperature affects the rate of this reaction.
parts.markscheme:
- Rate increases <marks>A1</marks>
- More particles have sufficient energy to overcome $E_a$ <marks>R1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify the overall effect
steps.content:
In chemistry, the rate of reaction is almost always increased by raising the temperature, regardless of whether the reaction is exothermic or endothermic. For this specific reaction, as the temperature increases, the rate of reaction increases.

This is the first key point required for your marks.

highlights.text: Describe how temperature affects the rate of this reaction

steps.title: Increase in kinetic energy
steps.content:
According to the **Kinetic Molecular Theory** (Topic S1.1.3), temperature is a measure of the average kinetic energy of the particles. 

When we increase the temperature, the particles gain more kinetic energy and move faster. This leads to two specific consequences in collision theory: more frequent collisions and more energetic collisions.

steps.title: Higher collision frequency
steps.content: Because the particles are moving faster at higher temperatures, they will physically bump into each other more often. This increase in the **frequency of collisions** contributes to an increase in the reaction rate, although this is usually the less significant of the two factors.

steps.title: Overcoming the activation energy
steps.content:
The most significant factor is that at higher temperatures, a much larger proportion of the particles possess kinetic energy that is greater than or equal to the **activation energy** ($E_a$). 

In IB exams, it is crucial to state that more particles have sufficient energy to overcome the energy barrier ($E_a$). This means that a higher percentage of the collisions that occur will be "successful" or "effective," leading to a product. This fulfills the second requirement for the marks.

steps.title: Final Summary
steps.content:
To summarize for your answer:
1. The **rate increases**.
2. More particles have **sufficient energy** (energy $\ge E_a$) to react upon collision.

Note: While the position of equilibrium for this exothermic reaction would shift toward the reactants (left) if temperature were increased, the *rate* at which the reaction occurs always increases with temperature.

steps.title: State the direct effect
steps.content:
In chemical kinetics, increasing the temperature will always result in an **increase in the rate of reaction**. 

Even though this specific reaction is exothermic, the temperature effect on the *rate* (how fast it happens) is distinct from its effect on the *equilibrium position*.

highlights.text: Describe how temperature affects the rate of this reaction.

steps.title: Understand the Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution
steps.content:
To explain *why* the rate increases, we use the Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution curve. This graph shows the distribution of kinetic energies among particles in a gas at a specific temperature.

At a higher temperature:
1. The curve **shifts to the right**, indicating that the average kinetic energy of the particles has increased.
2. The curve **flattens** (the peak becomes lower) because the total number of particles remains constant, so the distribution must spread out over a wider range of higher energies.

steps.title: Analyze the Activation Energy
steps.content:
The **activation energy** ($E_a$) is the minimum energy required for a collision between particles to be successful (resulting in a reaction). 

On the Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution, $E_a$ is a fixed point on the x-axis. The area under the curve to the right of $E_a$ represents the number of particles that possess enough energy to overcome this barrier.

steps.title: Link area to reaction rate
steps.content:
When the temperature is increased and the curve shifts/flattens, the **area under the curve beyond the activation energy threshold increases**. 

This means a significantly larger fraction of particles now have energy $\ge E_a$. Consequently, there is a higher frequency of successful collisions, which directly increases the reaction rate. 

This concept is a core part of **Topic R2.2: How fast? The rate of chemical change** in your syllabus.

highlights.text: More particles have sufficient energy to overcome $E_a$

steps.title: Identify the overall trend
steps.content:
The first step in answering this question is to state the general relationship: for almost all chemical reactions, an increase in temperature leads to an increase in the reaction rate. 

This is true regardless of whether the reaction is exothermic or endothermic. In this specific case, increasing the temperature will make the reaction between $\mathrm{A(g)}$ and $\mathrm{B(g)}$ go faster.

steps.title: The Arrhenius Equation
steps.content:
To explain *why* this happens using the Arrhenius approach, we look at the relationship between the rate constant ($k$) and temperature ($T$) found in the **Key Chemistry Equations** section of your data booklet:

$$k=A e^{\frac{-E_{a}}{R T}}$$

Where:
- $k$ is the rate constant (which is proportional to the reaction rate).
- $A$ is the frequency factor.
- $E_a$ is the activation energy.
- $R$ is the gas constant.
- $T$ is the absolute temperature in Kelvin.

steps.title: Analyze the mathematical relationship
steps.content:
Let's look at what happens to the math when $T$ increases:

1. As temperature ($T$) increases, the value of the fraction $\frac{E_a}{RT}$ decreases.
2. This means the exponent, $-\frac{E_a}{RT}$, becomes **less negative** (closer to zero).
3. Mathematically, as an exponent becomes less negative, the value of $e^{\text{exponent}}$ increases.

Since $k$ is directly proportional to $e^{\frac{-E_{a}}{R T}}$, the value of the rate constant $k$ increases as temperature increases.

gdc.instructions: Evaluate the expression e^(-50000/(8.31*300)) and compare to e^(-50000/(8.31*310)).

calculator.description: Compare the value of the exponential factor at 300K and 310K assuming an activation energy of 50 kJ/mol.

steps.title: Relate to activation energy
steps.content:
Physically, the term $e^{\frac{-E_{a}}{R T}}$ represents the fraction of molecules that possess kinetic energy equal to or greater than the activation energy ($E_a$). 

By increasing the temperature, you increase the average kinetic energy of the particles. Consequently, a much larger proportion of the particles will have sufficient energy to overcome the energy barrier (the activation energy) during collisions, leading to a higher frequency of successful collisions and a faster rate.

highlights.text: More particles have sufficient energy to overcome $E_a$

steps.title: Final Conclusion
steps.content:
In summary:

- The **rate increases** with temperature.
- This is because the rate constant ($k$) increases exponentially with temperature as shown by the Arrhenius equation.
- An increased $k$ means more reactant particles have the required activation energy to react upon collision.

parts.content: Explain how an increase in temperature affects the Maxwell–Boltzmann distribution.
parts.markscheme:
- Distribution flattens and shifts to the right <marks>A1</marks>
- Area under curve beyond $E_a$ increases <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify the distribution features
steps.content: A Maxwell–Boltzmann distribution curve shows the number of particles with a specific kinetic energy at a given temperature. When temperature increases, the average kinetic energy of the particles also increases. This fundamental concept is covered in **Topic S1.1.3: Temperature and Kinetic Energy**.

highlights.text: Explain how an increase in temperature affects the Maxwell–Boltzmann distribution.

steps.title: Describe the horizontal shift
steps.content: As the temperature increases, more particles possess higher kinetic energy. Graphically, this causes the entire distribution to spread out. The peak of the curve, which represents the most probable energy of the particles, **shifts to the right** toward higher energy values on the x-axis.

steps.title: Describe the vertical flattening
steps.content: The total area under the Maxwell–Boltzmann curve represents the total number of particles in the system, which remains constant. Because the curve has shifted right and broadened, the peak must become lower to maintain the same total area. Therefore, the distribution **flattens**.

steps.title: Relate to activation energy
steps.content: The activation energy ($E_a$) is a specific energy threshold on the x-axis. Because the curve has flattened and shifted to the right, a significantly larger **area under the curve beyond $E_a$** (to the right of the $E_a$ line) is created. This indicates a greater proportion of particles now have energy equal to or greater than the activation energy.

steps.title: Final Summary for Marks
steps.content:
To earn full marks on this IB question, ensure you mention both the shape change and the area change:

1. The distribution **flattens and shifts to the right**.
2. The **area under the curve beyond $E_a$ increases**.

highlights.text: Distribution flattens and shifts to the right

parts.content: Discuss how this affects both the frequency and energy of collisions.
parts.markscheme:
- Collisions are more frequent <marks>A1</marks>
- Higher proportion of successful (energetic) collisions <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify the temperature change
steps.content:
The chemical equation shows that the reaction is exothermic because 'heat' is listed as a product:

$$\mathrm{A(g)} + \mathrm{B(g)} \rightarrow \mathrm{C(g)} + \text{heat}$$

In an exothermic reaction, energy is released to the surroundings, which causes the temperature of the reaction mixture to increase. This temperature rise is the driving force for the changes in how particles collide.

steps.title: Link temperature to motion
steps.content:
According to the **Kinetic Molecular Theory** (which you can review in Topic S1.1), temperature is a measure of the average kinetic energy of the particles. 

As the heat from the reaction increases the temperature, the gas particles gain kinetic energy and move at higher average speeds. Because they are moving faster, they will run into each other more often.

steps.title: Analyze collision frequency
steps.content:
Since the particles are moving faster, the number of collisions occurring in a specific amount of time increases. In IB Chemistry terms, we say there is an increase in the **frequency of collisions**.

This accounts for the first mark in the markscheme.

steps.title: Analyze collision energy
steps.content:
Not only do the particles collide more often, but they also collide with more force (energy). For a collision to result in a reaction, it must have energy equal to or greater than the **activation energy** ($E_a$).

At a higher temperature, a **higher proportion of particles** have enough energy to overcome this barrier. This leads to a higher proportion of successful (energetic) collisions, which earns the second mark.

steps.title: Link heat to kinetic energy
steps.content:
In an exothermic reaction, energy is released as **heat**. According to the **Kinetic Molecular Theory** (Topic S1.1), temperature is a measure of the average kinetic energy of the particles. 

Therefore, an increase in heat within the system raises the temperature, which increases the average kinetic energy ($E_{k}$) of the gas molecules $\mathrm{A}$ and $\mathrm{B}$.

steps.title: Analyze collision frequency
steps.content:
Since the average kinetic energy ($E_{k} = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$) increases, the particles move at higher average speeds. 

Because the particles are moving faster within the same volume, they will encounter each other more often. This results in **more frequent collisions** between the reactants.

steps.title: Analyze collision energy
steps.content:
Using the **Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution**, we can see that a temperature increase shifts the energy distribution curve to the right and flattens it. 

This shift means that a much larger proportion of the particles now possess energy equal to or greater than the activation energy ($E \ge E_{a}$). Consequently, there is a **higher proportion of successful collisions** that result in a reaction.

questions.specification: Catalysts are used in many industrial processes.

labeledSubSubTopics.title: R2.2.5 Catalysts
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: All parts focus on catalysts: defining a catalyst, whether it is consumed, and explaining its effect on reaction rate (alternative pathway/lower activation energy).
