labeledSubSubTopics.title: R2.2.3 Factors affecting the rate of reaction
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Catalysts are a key factor affecting reaction rate; part (c) asks generally how a catalyst changes the rate, fitting within factors affecting rate as well.

parts.content: Define the term catalyst.
parts.markscheme:
A substance that increases the rate of a reaction without being consumed
<marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Identify the primary function
steps.content:
A catalyst is defined primarily by its effect on how fast a reaction occurs. Its job is to **increase the rate of a chemical reaction**.

This is covered in **Topic R2.2: How fast? The rate of chemical change** in your syllabus. If you need to review how they work (like providing an alternative pathway with lower activation energy), that is a great place to start!

steps.title: Consider the chemical state
steps.content: Unlike reactants, which are converted into products, a catalyst is not "used up." This means it must be **chemically unchanged** or **not consumed** by the end of the process. While it might participate in intermediate steps, it is always regenerated.

steps.title: Formulate the standard definition
steps.content:
To earn the mark, you must combine both key properties into a single concise statement. 

**Definition:** A catalyst is a substance that increases the rate of a chemical reaction without being consumed (or permanently changed) in the process.

highlights.text: A substance that increases the rate of a reaction without being consumed

parts.content: State whether a catalyst is used up in a chemical reaction.
parts.markscheme:
No
<marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Define a catalyst
steps.content: In chemistry, a **catalyst** is defined as a substance that increases the rate of a chemical reaction without undergoing any permanent chemical change itself. This concept is covered in **Topic R2.2.5: Catalysts** of the IB syllabus.

steps.title: Analyze the consumption
steps.content: While a catalyst does participate in the reaction mechanism (often by forming a temporary intermediate), it is always regenerated at the end of the reaction. Because it is regenerated, the total mass of the catalyst remains constant from the start to the end of the process.

highlights.text: State whether a catalyst is used up in a chemical reaction.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content: Since the catalyst is not permanently changed or consumed, the answer is **no**, it is not used up.

parts.content: Explain how a catalyst affects the rate of reaction.
parts.markscheme:
- Provides an alternative pathway with lower activation energy
<marks>1 mark</marks>
- More particles have energy $\geq E_a$
<marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Define activation energy
steps.content: To understand how a catalyst works, we first need to recall **activation energy** ($E_a$). This is the minimum amount of energy that colliding particles must possess for a reaction to occur. This concept is covered in **Topic R2.2.2: Collision theory**.

steps.title: Provide alternative pathway
steps.content:
A catalyst increases the rate of reaction by providing an **alternative reaction pathway**. This new pathway has a **lower activation energy** ($E_a$) than the uncatalyzed reaction. This is the first key point required for your marks.

In the data booklet, the relationship between activation energy and the rate constant is shown in the Arrhenius equation:

$$k=A e^{\frac{-E_{a}}{R T}}$$

highlights.text: Provides an alternative pathway with lower activation energy

steps.title: Analyze particle energy
steps.content: Because the energy barrier ($E_a$) is now lower, a greater proportion of the reactant particles have sufficient kinetic energy ($E$) to overcome it. This means that more particles have energy $E \geq E_a$.

steps.title: Increased collision frequency
steps.content: Since more particles possess the required energy, there is a **higher frequency of successful (fruitful) collisions** between reactant particles. This results in an increased rate of reaction.

steps.title: Define Catalyst Action
steps.content: A catalyst increases the rate of a chemical reaction by providing an **alternative reaction pathway** that has a **lower activation energy ($E_a$)** than the uncatalyzed reaction.

highlights.text: Provides an alternative pathway with lower activation energy

steps.title: Analyze the Energy Threshold
steps.content:
In a Maxwell–Boltzmann distribution, the x-axis represents kinetic energy and the y-axis represents the number of particles. The **activation energy ($E_a$)** acts as a threshold value. 

Only particles with kinetic energy greater than or equal to this threshold ($KE \geq E_a$) can undergo a successful collision upon impact. On the graph, this proportion is represented by the **area under the curve** to the right of the $E_a$ line.

steps.title: Visualize with Graphing Tools
steps.content: You can visualize this by sketching a distribution curve. When a catalyst is added, the vertical line for $E_a$ shifts to the left (to a lower energy value, $E_{a\text{ (cat)}}$).

gdc.instructions: Graph a probability density function that mimics the distribution. Observe how moving a vertical line (representing Ea) to the left captures more area under the curve.

ti84.instructions:
While the TI-84 doesn't have a built-in Maxwell-Boltzmann function, you can approximate the shape using a Chi-Square distribution with 3 degrees of freedom. 
1. Press [Y=].
2. Enter Y1 = χ²pdf(X, 3). 
3. Use [WINDOW] to set Xmin=0, Xmax=10, Ymin=0, Ymax=0.5.
4. Use [2nd][CALC][7:∫f(x)dx] to find the area under the curve from an 'activation energy' (e.g., X=5) to the end (X=10). Then try a lower value (e.g., X=3) to see the area increase.

desmos.instructions:
1. Plot a Maxwell-Boltzmann-like function: f(x) = x^2 * e^(-x^2). 
2. Create a slider 'a' for activation energy. 
3. Shade the area to the right: y < f(x) {x > a}.
4. Slide 'a' to the left (decreasing Ea) and watch the shaded area (number of reacting particles) grow significantly.

calculator.description: Visualize the Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution and the effect of shifting the activation energy threshold.

steps.title: Relate Area to Rate
steps.content:
By lowering the activation energy from $E_a$ to $E_{a\text{ (cat)}}$, the threshold moves to the left. 

As a result, a significantly **larger area under the curve** now lies to the right of the threshold. This means a **greater proportion of particles** possess the minimum energy required to react ($E \geq E_{a\text{ (cat)}}$), leading to a higher frequency of successful collisions and an increased reaction rate.

steps.title: Define the catalyst's role
steps.content:
A catalyst works by providing an **alternative reaction pathway**. This new pathway has a **lower activation energy** ($E_a$) than the uncatalyzed reaction. 

This is the first essential point for earning marks in this question.

highlights.text: Provides an alternative pathway with lower activation energy

steps.title: Analyze the Arrhenius equation
steps.content:
To understand why a lower $E_a$ increases the rate, we look at the **Arrhenius equation** from the data booklet:

$$k = A e^{\frac{-E_a}{RT}}$$

In this equation:
- $k$ is the rate constant.
- $E_a$ is the activation energy.
- $R$ is the gas constant and $T$ is temperature.

Mathematically, because $E_a$ is in a **negative exponent**, decreasing $E_a$ makes the value of $e^{\frac{-E_a}{RT}}$ larger. Consequently, the rate constant $k$ increases.

steps.title: Relate $k$ to rate
steps.content:
Since the rate of reaction is directly proportional to the rate constant ($k$), an increase in $k$ leads to an increase in the overall reaction rate.

In terms of collision theory, lowering the $E_a$ threshold means that **more particles now have energy greater than or equal to the activation energy** ($E \geq E_a$) during collisions.

steps.title: Summary of marks
steps.content:
To secure both marks on an IB exam, ensure you mention:
1. The catalyst provides an **alternative pathway** with **lower activation energy** ($E_a$).
2. Therefore, a **greater proportion of particles** have sufficient energy ($E \geq E_a$) to react upon collision.

parts.content: State the balanced chemical equation for the synthesis of ethanol from ethene and water.
parts.markscheme:
$$\text{CH}_2=\text{CH}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{OH}$$
**OR**
$$\text{C}_2\text{H}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH}$$

<marks>A1</marks>

Total: [1 mark]

steps.title: Identify the reactants and product
steps.content: To write the balanced chemical equation, we first identify the substances involved in the reaction based on the question. The reaction is an **addition reaction** (specifically a hydration) where water is added to an alkene.

highlights.text: synthesis of ethanol from ethene and water

steps.title: Determine the molecular formulas
steps.content:
Using the **Molecular Formula Approach**, we represent each substance by its total atomic count. According to naming rules in **Topic S3.2: Functional groups**:

*   **Ethene**: An alkene with two carbons, so its molecular formula is $C_2H_4$.
*   **Water**: Its standard molecular formula is $H_2O$.
*   **Ethanol**: An alcohol with two carbons. Its molecular formula is often written as $C_2H_5OH$ to highlight the hydroxyl group, or $C_2H_6O$ for the total atomic count.

steps.title: Construct the balanced equation
steps.content:
Now, we combine the reactants on the left and the product on the right. In an addition reaction, all atoms from the reactants end up in the single product molecule.

$$\text{C}_2\text{H}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH}$$

Let's verify the atomic balance:
*   **Carbon**: 2 on the left ($C_2H_4$) $\rightarrow$ 2 on the right ($C_2H_5OH$).
*   **Hydrogen**: $4 + 2 = 6$ on the left $\rightarrow$ $5 + 1 = 6$ on the right.
*   **Oxygen**: 1 on the left ($H_2O$) $\rightarrow$ 1 on the right ($C_2H_5OH$).

The equation is balanced with a 1:1:1 molar ratio.

steps.title: Identify the reactants
steps.content:
To synthesize ethanol, we start with **ethene** and **water**. Using the structural formula approach, we represent ethene by its carbon-carbon double bond:

$$\text{CH}_2=\text{CH}_2$$

Water is simply represented as $\text{H}_2\text{O}$. In this reaction, the water molecule adds across the double bond of the alkene.

highlights.text: synthesis of ethanol from ethene and water

steps.title: Visualize the addition reaction
steps.content:
This process is an **addition reaction** (specifically, hydration). 

1. The double bond in $\text{CH}_2=\text{CH}_2$ breaks, leaving a single bond.
2. One hydrogen atom ($-\text{H}$) from the water molecule attaches to the first carbon.
3. The hydroxyl group ($-\text{OH}$) from the water molecule attaches to the second carbon.

Following the connectivity, the molecule transforms from $\text{CH}_2=\text{CH}_2$ into $\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{OH}$.

steps.title: Write the balanced equation
steps.content:
By combining the structural formulas of the reactants and the product, we get the final balanced chemical equation. Since all atoms from the reactants are incorporated into a single product, the stoichiometry is $1:1$.

$$\text{CH}_2=\text{CH}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{OH}$$

This format clearly shows how the functional group changes from an alkene to an alcohol.

parts.content: Draw a Maxwell-Boltzmann energy distribution for the reaction at the lower temperature $T_1$ and the higher temperature $T_2$. Label the activation energy $E_a$.
parts.markscheme:
![Maxwell-Boltzmann Distribution](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/plots/df871be1-680e-412c-92eb-ea6592b969dc.png)

Both axes correctly labelled (e.g., number of particles and kinetic energy/energy) and both curves starting at the origin. <marks>A1</marks>

$T_2$ curve peak is lower and to the right of $T_1$ curve peak AND $E_a$ is correctly labelled. <marks>A1</marks>

Total: [2 marks]

steps.title: Define the Axes
steps.content: To draw a Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution, we first need to define the axes. This graph represents the distribution of kinetic energies for a sample of gas particles. According to the IB requirements, the x-axis must represent the energy of the particles, and the y-axis represents the number of particles with that energy.

highlights.text: Both axes correctly labelled (e.g., number of particles and kinetic energy/energy)

steps.title: Convert Temperature to Kelvin
steps.content: In chemistry, we always work with absolute temperature in Kelvin. The problem mentions a temperature of $300^{\circ}\text{C}$. Let's convert this to Kelvin using the relation $T(K) = T(^{\circ}\text{C}) + 273.15$.

ti84.instructions: Add 300 and 273.15

calculator.description: Convert Celsius to Kelvin

steps.title: Sketch the T1 Curve
steps.content: Start your sketch at the origin $(0,0)$. The curve for the lower temperature, $T_1$, should rise to a peak and then descend toward the x-axis. It is important that the curve does not touch the x-axis at high energies, as there is always a theoretical possibility of a particle having very high energy.

highlights.text: both curves starting at the origin

steps.title: Sketch the T2 Curve
steps.content:
When drawing the curve for the higher temperature, $T_2$, remember that the average kinetic energy increases. To represent this correctly:
1. The peak must be **lower** than the $T_1$ peak.
2. The peak must be shifted to the **right** (higher energy).
3. The total area under the curve must remain the same (representing the same total number of particles).

highlights.text: T_2 curve peak is lower and to the right of T_1 curve peak

steps.title: Mark the Activation Energy
steps.content: The activation energy ($E_a$) is the minimum energy required for a collision to result in a reaction. Draw a vertical line on the right side of the distribution. This line is fixed; it does not change with temperature. Label it $E_a$.

steps.title: Verify Final Marks
steps.content: Ensure your final drawing clearly distinguishes the two curves and that $E_a$ is placed such that more area under the $T_2$ curve lies to its right compared to the $T_1$ curve. This demonstrates that at higher temperatures, a larger fraction of particles possess energy $\geq E_a$, leading to a faster reaction rate as described in **Topic R2.2.4** of the syllabus.

parts.content: State, with a reason, how the addition of a catalyst in the ethanol synthesis affects the rate of the reaction.
parts.markscheme:
The rate of reaction increases. <marks>A1</marks>

The catalyst provides an alternative pathway with a lower activation energy ($E_a$), resulting in a higher fraction of molecules having energy greater than or equal to $E_a$. <marks>R1</marks>

Total: [2 marks]

steps.title: State the effect
steps.content: In any chemical reaction, the addition of a catalyst increases the **rate of reaction**. This means the synthesis of ethanol from ethene and water will happen more quickly with the phosphoric acid present.

steps.title: Lowering activation energy
steps.content:
The catalyst works by providing an **alternative reaction pathway**. This new mechanism has a lower **activation energy** ($E_a$) than the original uncatalyzed pathway.

You can see the relationship between activation energy and the rate constant ($k$) in the Arrhenius equation found in your data booklet:

$$k=A e^{\frac{-E_{a}}{R T}}$$

When $E_a$ decreases, the value of $k$ increases, which directly reflects a faster reaction rate.

steps.title: Applying collision theory
steps.content:
According to **collision theory**, for a reaction to occur, particles must collide with energy greater than or equal to the activation energy ($E \ge E_a$). 

By lowering the $E_a$ barrier, a **higher fraction of molecules** now have sufficient kinetic energy to react upon collision. This leads to a higher frequency of successful (fruitful) collisions per unit time, thereby increasing the overall rate.

steps.title: Final answer summary
steps.content:
To earn full marks on this question, you must state both the effect and the scientific reason:

1.  **Effect:** The rate of reaction increases.
2.  **Reason:** The catalyst provides an alternative pathway with a lower activation energy ($E_a$), which means more particles have enough energy to react, resulting in a higher frequency of successful collisions.

This concept is a core part of **Topic R2.2: How fast? The rate of chemical change**.

steps.title: Identify the effect
steps.content: The first step is to state what happens to the speed of the reaction. When a phosphoric acid catalyst is added to the synthesis of ethanol, the **rate of reaction increases**.

steps.title: Lowering activation energy
steps.content:
To explain why the rate increases, we look at the mechanism. A catalyst provides an **alternative reaction pathway** that has a **lower activation energy** ($E_a$).

This relates to the Arrhenius equation found in your data booklet:

$$k = A e^{\frac{-E_a}{RT}}$$

Notice that if $E_a$ decreases, the value of the rate constant ($k$) increases, which directly results in a faster reaction rate.

steps.title: Analyze energy distribution
steps.content:
Using the **Maxwell–Boltzmann Distribution** approach, we consider the energy of all the molecules in the system. 

At a constant temperature, the distribution of molecular energies remains the same. However, because the catalyst lowers the activation energy threshold, a **higher fraction of molecules** now possess energy greater than or equal to the new, lower activation energy ($E \ge E_a$). 

Since more molecules have sufficient energy to overcome the energy barrier, the frequency of successful collisions increases, thus increasing the rate.

steps.title: State the overall effect
steps.content: The addition of a catalyst, such as phosphoric acid in this synthesis, **increases the rate of the reaction**. This is a fundamental concept in kinetics where catalysts speed up chemical processes without being consumed.

steps.title: Explain the mechanism
steps.content: A catalyst works by providing an **alternative reaction pathway**. This new pathway has a **lower activation energy ($E_a$)** compared to the uncatalyzed reaction. This means that at a given temperature, a greater proportion of reactant particles possess the minimum energy required for a successful collision.

steps.title: Interpret via Arrhenius equation
steps.content:
We can see why this happens by looking at the **Arrhenius equation** from the data booklet:

$$k = A e^{\frac{-E_{a}}{RT}}$$

In this equation, $k$ is the rate constant. When a catalyst **decreases $E_a$**, the term $\frac{E_a}{RT}$ becomes smaller. Since this term is negative in the exponent ($e^{-E_a/RT}$), a smaller $E_a$ results in a **larger value for the rate constant $k$**. 

Because the reaction rate is directly proportional to $k$ (e.g., in a rate law like $\text{rate} = k[A][B]$), an increase in $k$ leads to an increase in the overall reaction rate.

gdc.instructions: Evaluate e^(-5) and e^(-2).

ti84.instructions: Calculate e^(-5) and e^(-2) to see the trend.

desmos.instructions: Compare the values of the exponential terms.

calculator.description: Observe how a smaller Ea (numerator in the negative exponent) increases the result. Let's compare e^-5 to e^-2.

steps.title: Final answer for marks
steps.content:
To earn both marks, your answer should clearly state:
1. The rate of reaction **increases**.
2. The catalyst provides an alternative pathway with a **lower activation energy ($E_a$)**.

According to the Arrhenius relationship, a lower $E_a$ increases the rate constant $k$, resulting in more frequent successful collisions per unit time.

parts.content: Outline how a catalyst increases the rate of reaction.
parts.markscheme:
A catalyst provides an alternative reaction pathway <marks>1</marks>

with lower $E_\mathrm{a}$ <marks>1</marks>

steps.title: Define the Catalyst's Role
steps.content: In this reaction, sulfuric acid acts as a catalyst. A catalyst is a substance that increases the rate of a chemical reaction without undergoing any permanent chemical change itself. To understand how it works, we look at the reaction mechanism.

highlights.text: The reaction uses sulfuric acid as a catalyst.

steps.title: Alternative Reaction Pathway
steps.content: The first mark in IB exams for this topic is usually awarded for stating that a catalyst provides an **alternative reaction pathway** (or mechanism) for the reactants to turn into products.

highlights.text: A catalyst provides an alternative reaction pathway

steps.title: Lowering Activation Energy
steps.content:
This alternative pathway is significant because it has a **lower activation energy** ($E_a$) compared to the uncatalyzed route. 

Activation energy is the minimum energy that colliding particles must possess for a reaction to occur. By lowering this barrier, a greater proportion of particles will have sufficient energy to react upon collision at a given temperature.

steps.title: Final Summary
steps.content:
To get full marks on an "outline" question like this, you must mention both points:
1. The provision of an **alternative pathway**.
2. That this new pathway has a **lower activation energy** ($E_a$).

steps.title: Define the Catalyst's Role
steps.content: A catalyst, such as the sulfuric acid used in this reaction, works by providing an **alternative reaction pathway**. This new pathway has a **lower activation energy** ($E_{\text{a}}$) than the uncatalyzed reaction.

steps.title: Analyze Energy Distribution
steps.content:
According to **Collision Theory**, for a reaction to occur, particles must collide with kinetic energy ($KE$) greater than or equal to the activation energy ($E_{\text{a}}$). 

By lowering the $E_{\text{a}}$ barrier, a **greater proportion of particles** in the mixture now possess sufficient energy to react ($KE \geq E_{\text{a}}$) at the same temperature. This can be visualized on a Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution curve where the area representing particles with enough energy increases.

steps.title: Explain Successful Collisions
steps.content:
Because a larger fraction of the reactant particles (ethene and steam) now meet the energy requirement for a successful collision, the **frequency of successful collisions** increases. 

This leads to an increase in the rate of reaction, as defined by the Arrhenius relationship found in your data booklet:

$$k=A e^{\frac{-E_{\text{a}}}{RT}}$$

As $E_{\text{a}}$ decreases, the rate constant ($k$) increases.

parts.content:
Annotate the Maxwell–Boltzmann distribution curve showing the activation energies, $E_\mathrm{a}$, for the catalysed and uncatalysed reactions.
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/20c45053-cf94-4605-a57c-1b7517fd549c)
parts.markscheme:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/4a849153-186e-4646-90d6-a6a45f428910)

<marks>2</marks>

steps.title: Identify the goal
steps.content:
The question asks us to annotate the Maxwell–Boltzmann distribution curve by marking the activation energies for both the catalysed and uncatalysed versions of the reaction. 

This concept is covered in **Topic R2.2: How fast? The rate of chemical change**, which explains how catalysts affect reaction rates by providing an alternative pathway.

steps.title: Understand activation energy position
steps.content:
On a Maxwell–Boltzmann distribution curve, the horizontal axis represents the kinetic energy of the particles. 

Activation energy ($E_a$) is the minimum energy required for a successful collision. We represent this as a vertical line on the x-axis. Any particles to the right of this line have enough energy to react.

steps.title: Compare catalysed vs uncatalysed
steps.content:
A catalyst, such as the sulfuric acid mentioned in the question, works by providing an alternative reaction pathway with a **lower** activation energy. 

Since the kinetic energy increases as we move to the right along the horizontal axis, a lower energy value must be placed further to the left.

highlights.text: uses sulfuric acid as a catalyst

steps.title: Annotate the curve
steps.content:
To earn the marks, you should draw two vertical lines and label them clearly:

1. Draw a vertical line for the uncatalysed reaction ($E_{a \text{ (uncat)}}$).
2. Draw a second vertical line to the **left** of the first one for the catalysed reaction ($E_{a \text{ (cat)}}$).

$$\text{Position: } E_{a \text{ (cat)}} < E_{a \text{ (uncat)}}$$

steps.title: Final check against markscheme
steps.content:
According to the markscheme, the first mark is awarded for placing the catalysed activation energy to the left of the uncatalysed one. The second mark is for clearly labeling both. 

By placing $E_{a \text{ (cat)}}$ to the left, you show that a larger proportion of particles (the area under the curve to the right of the line) now have sufficient energy to react, which explains why the rate increases.

parts.content:
Figure 1 shows how the percentage yield of ethanol changes with pressure at different temperatures. 

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/88183bb0-016e-4312-8cb0-caf38b178965)

State the conditions that produce the highest percentage yield of ethanol.
parts.markscheme:
$300^\circ\mathrm{C}$ <marks>1</marks>

$300 \text{ atm}$ <marks>1</marks>

steps.title: Analyze the graph axes
steps.content: To find the conditions for the **highest percentage yield**, we need to look for the highest point on the vertical ($y$) axis of the graph. This axis represents the percentage yield of ethanol.

steps.title: Identify the temperature curve
steps.content:
Looking at the three curves plotted in **Figure 1**, each represents a different temperature. The curve for $300^\circ\mathrm{C}$ is positioned above the $400^\circ\mathrm{C}$ and $500^\circ\mathrm{C}$ curves at all pressure points. 

Therefore, the highest yields are achieved on the **$300^\circ\mathrm{C}$** curve.

steps.title: Determine the pressure value
steps.content:
Now, we follow the $300^\circ\mathrm{C}$ curve to its highest point. The graph shows that as pressure increases, the percentage yield also increases. 

The highest point on the $300^\circ\mathrm{C}$ curve occurs at the far right of the $x$-axis. Reading straight down from this point to the $x$-axis gives a pressure of **$300 \text{ atm}$**.

steps.title: State the final conditions
steps.content:
By directly extracting these values from the graph, we determine the conditions for the highest yield are:

- **Temperature:** $300^\circ\mathrm{C}$
- **Pressure:** $300 \text{ atm}$

In an IB exam, stating each of these values correctly would earn you one mark each, for a total of 2 marks.

steps.title: Analyze the Pressure trend
steps.content:
To find the conditions for the highest yield, we first look at how changing the pressure affects the outcome. By looking at the x-axis of the graph, we can see that as the pressure increases from $0$ to $300 \text{ atm}$, the percentage yield of ethanol increases for all temperature curves.

The highest pressure shown on the graph is $300 \text{ atm}$, which corresponds to the rightmost point on each curve.

highlights.text: percentage yield of ethanol changes with pressure

steps.title: Analyze the Temperature trend
steps.content:
Next, we compare the different temperature curves at a fixed pressure. If we look at any vertical slice of the graph, the curve for $300^\circ\mathrm{C}$ is always higher than the curves for $400^\circ\mathrm{C}$ and $500^\circ\mathrm{C}$.

This tells us that the yield increases as the temperature decreases. Therefore, the lowest temperature shown, $300^\circ\mathrm{C}$, provides the highest yield.

steps.title: Identify optimal conditions
steps.content:
By combining these two independent trends, we identify the point on the graph with the absolute highest percentage yield. This point is at the very top-right of the plot.

- **Temperature:** $300^\circ\mathrm{C}$
- **Pressure:** $300 \text{ atm}$

This aligns with Le Châtelier's principle (Topic **R2.3: How far?**), where increasing pressure shifts the equilibrium toward the side with fewer gas moles (the product side), and decreasing temperature shifts it toward the exothermic direction.

parts.content:
Figure 2 shows how the rate of reaction changes as the temperature changes at different pressures. 

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/a4d57ece-f477-4fa9-a44a-654a10c85c99)

In one reaction for the production of ethanol, the conditions are:
* $300^\circ\mathrm{C}$
* $65 \text{ atm}$
* A catalyst 

Use Figures 1 and 2 to justify the choice of conditions for the reaction.

steps.title: Evaluate Temperature Trade-off
steps.content:
In industrial chemistry, we often have to find a 'compromise' temperature. Let's look at why $300^\circ\mathrm{C}$ is chosen using both kinetics and equilibrium:

*   **Kinetics (Rate):** Looking at **Figure 2**, as temperature increases, the rate of reaction increases significantly. A higher temperature like $300^\circ\mathrm{C}$ ensures the ethanol is produced quickly enough to be commercially viable.
*   **Equilibrium (Yield):** Although not shown here, **Figure 1** typically shows that as temperature increases, the percentage yield of ethanol decreases (because the forward reaction is exothermic). 

Therefore, $300^\circ\mathrm{C}$ is a compromise: high enough for a **faster rate**, but low enough to avoid a **very low yield**.

highlights.text: a higher temperature gives a lower yield
a higher temperature gives a faster rate

steps.title: Analyze Pressure Benefits
steps.content:
Pressure is a 'win-win' for this specific reaction, though it comes with high equipment costs. Using $65 \text{ atm}$ is justified because:

*   **Kinetics (Rate):** In **Figure 2**, we can see that at any given temperature, the curve for higher pressure is higher than the curve for lower pressure. Thus, **higher pressure gives a faster rate**.
*   **Equilibrium (Yield):** According to Le Châtelier’s Principle (Topic R2.3), since there are 2 moles of gas on the left (ethene + steam) and only 1 mole on the right (ethanol), increasing the pressure shifts the equilibrium to the right. Thus, **higher pressure gives a higher yield**.

highlights.text: a higher pressure gives a higher yield
a higher pressure gives a faster rate

steps.title: Justify Catalyst Use
steps.content:
The sulfuric acid catalyst is essential for the economic feasibility of the process. 

As you learned in **Topic R2.2 (Factors affecting the rate of reaction)**, a catalyst provides an alternative reaction pathway with a lower activation energy ($E_a$). This allows the reaction to proceed at a **faster rate** without needing to increase the temperature further. By keeping the temperature at $300^\circ\mathrm{C}$ instead of much higher, the plant **saves energy costs** and maintains a better equilibrium yield.

highlights.text: a catalyst allows a lower temperature to be used and so saves energy

parts.content:
Consider the first stage of the reaction:

$$\text{CH}_4(g) + \text{H}_2\text{O}(g) \rightleftharpoons \text{CO}(g) + 3\text{H}_2(g)$$

State the expression for $K_c$ for this stage of the reaction.
parts.markscheme: $K_c = \dfrac{[\text{CO}][\text{H}_2]^3}{[\text{CH}_4][\text{H}_2\text{O}]}$ <marks>1</marks>

steps.title: Identify the chemical equation
steps.content:
To write the equilibrium constant expression ($K_c$), we first need to identify the reactants and products from the balanced chemical equation provided in the question.

$$\text{CH}_4(g) + \text{H}_2\text{O}(g) \rightleftharpoons \text{CO}(g) + 3\text{H}_2(g)$$

Here, the reactants are methane ($\text{CH}_4$) and steam ($\text{H}_2\text{O}$), and the products are carbon monoxide ($\text{CO}$) and hydrogen ($\text{H}_2$).

steps.title: Apply Law of Mass Action
steps.content:
Using the **Law of Mass Action**, the equilibrium constant $K_c$ is defined as the ratio of the product of the concentrations of the products to the product of the concentrations of the reactants. 

We represent concentrations using square brackets, such as $[\text{CO}]$. According to the equilibrium law, each concentration term must be raised to the power of its **stoichiometric coefficient** (the number in front of the molecule in the balanced equation).

steps.title: Determine the coefficients
steps.content:
Let's identify the coefficients from the equation:
- For $\text{CO}$, the coefficient is $1$.
- For $\text{H}_2$, the coefficient is $3$.
- For $\text{CH}_4$, the coefficient is $1$.
- For $\text{H}_2\text{O}$, the coefficient is $1$.

This means $[\text{H}_2]$ will be cubed ($[\text{H}_2]^3$), while the others will stay as they are (raised to the power of $1$).

steps.title: State the final expression
steps.content:
Combining these into the standard $K_c$ format (products over reactants), we get the final expression:

$$K_c = \frac{[\text{CO}][\text{H}_2]^3}{[\text{CH}_4][\text{H}_2\text{O}]}$$

In the IB markscheme, you earn **1 mark** for providing this exact expression. Ensure you use square brackets to represent molar concentrations.

parts.content: State and explain the effect of increasing temperature on the value of $K_c$.
parts.markscheme: $K_c$ increases **AND** the forward reaction is endothermic. <marks>1</marks>

steps.title: Identify the reaction type
steps.content:
To determine the effect of temperature on the equilibrium constant ($K_c$), we first need to know if the reaction is exothermic or endothermic. The steam reforming of methane is a highly **endothermic** reaction, meaning it absorbs heat from the surroundings.

$$\text{CH}_4(g) + \text{H}_2\text{O}(g) \rightleftharpoons \text{CO}(g) + 3\text{H}_2(g) \quad \Delta H > 0$$

steps.title: Apply Le Chatelier's Principle
steps.content:
According to **Le Chatelier's Principle**, if a system at equilibrium is disturbed by a change in temperature, the system will shift its position to counteract that change. 

When we increase the temperature, the system tries to "remove" the extra heat by shifting in the direction that absorbs energy (the endothermic direction).

steps.title: Determine the shift direction
steps.content: Since the forward reaction is endothermic, the equilibrium will **shift to the right** (towards the products) to absorb the added heat. This results in a higher concentration of products and a lower concentration of reactants at the new equilibrium state.

steps.title: Relate shift to Kc
steps.content:
The equilibrium constant $K_c$ is defined as the ratio of the concentrations of products to reactants. You can find more about this in **Topic R2.3: The equilibrium law and constant**.

$$K_c = \frac{[\text{products}]}{[\text{reactants}]}$$

Because the shift to the right increases the numerator (products) and decreases the denominator (reactants), the value of **$K_c$ increases**. 

*Note: Temperature is the only factor that can change the value of $K_c$ for a specific reaction.*

steps.title: Define Kc kinetically
steps.content:
To understand how temperature affects the equilibrium constant using kinetics, we first need to recall that at equilibrium, the rate of the forward reaction equals the rate of the reverse reaction. The equilibrium constant $K_c$ is defined as the ratio of the forward rate constant ($k_f$) to the reverse rate constant ($k_r$):

$$K_c = \frac{k_f}{k_r}$$

steps.title: Identify activation energy relationship
steps.content:
The steam reforming of methane is an **endothermic** reaction. For endothermic reactions, the energy of the products is higher than the reactants. This means the forward activation energy ($E_{a,f}$) is significantly greater than the reverse activation energy ($E_{a,r}$).

$$E_{a, \text{forward}} > E_{a, \text{reverse}}$$

steps.title: Analyze temperature sensitivity
steps.content:
We can look at the **Arrhenius equation** from the data booklet to see how rate constants change with temperature:

$$k=A e^{\frac{-E_{a}}{R T}}$$

According to this equation, an increase in temperature ($T$) increases the value of $k$ for both directions. However, the rate constant for the reaction with the **higher activation energy** is more sensitive to temperature changes. Since the forward reaction is endothermic and has a higher $E_a$, $k_f$ increases more than $k_r$ does when temperature rises.

steps.title: Determine effect on Kc
steps.content:
Because the forward rate constant ($k_f$) increases by a larger factor than the reverse rate constant ($k_r$), the value of the ratio $\frac{k_f}{k_r}$ must increase. 

Therefore, for this endothermic reaction, **$K_c$ increases** as temperature increases.

parts.content:
The equilibrium constant, $K_c$, has a value of $1.01$ at $298\text{ K}$ for this reaction.

Calculate $\Delta G^\ominus$, in $\text{kJ mol}^{-1}$, for this reaction. Use sections 1 and 2 of the data booklet.
parts.markscheme:
$$\Delta G^\ominus = -RT\ln K_c$$

$\Delta G^\ominus = -8.31\text{ J K}^{-1}\text{ mol}^{-1} \times 298\text{ K} \times \ln(1.01)$ <marks>M1</marks>

$= -0.0246\text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$ <marks>A1</marks>

<note>Award <marks>2</marks> for correct final answer.</note>
<note>Award <marks>1</marks> max for $+0.0246\text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$.</note>

steps.title: Identify the relevant formula
steps.content:
To find the standard Gibbs free energy change ($\Delta G^\ominus$) from an equilibrium constant ($K_c$), we use the relationship found in the IB Chemistry HL curriculum (Topic R1.4/R2.3):

$$\Delta G^\ominus = -RT \ln K_c$$

Where:
- $R$ is the gas constant ($8.31 \text{ J K}^{-1} \text{ mol}^{-1}$)
- $T$ is the absolute temperature in Kelvin
- $K_c$ is the equilibrium constant

highlights.text: equilibrium constant, K_c, has a value of 1.01

steps.title: Gather values and constants
steps.content:
From Section 2 of the data booklet, we find the value for the gas constant $R$. We also identify the temperature and equilibrium constant provided in the question.

- $R = 8.31 \text{ J K}^{-1} \text{ mol}^{-1}$
- $T = 298 \text{ K}$
- $K_c = 1.01$

highlights.text: Gas constant R = 8.31 J K^{-1} mol^{-1}

steps.title: Calculate the result
steps.content:
Substitute the values into the equation. Be careful with the units: the gas constant $R$ uses Joules ($J$), so our initial answer will be in $\text{J mol}^{-1}$.

$$\begin{aligned} \Delta G^\ominus &= - (8.31 \text{ J K}^{-1} \text{ mol}^{-1}) \times (298 \text{ K}) \times \ln(1.01) \\ \Delta G^\ominus &\approx -24.64 \text{ J mol}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter the expression on the calculation screen.

ti84.instructions: Enter the expression directly on the main screen.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression into the input bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the Gibbs free energy change in Joules.

steps.title: Convert to kilojoules
steps.content:
The question asks for the answer in $\text{kJ mol}^{-1}$. Since $1 \text{ kJ} = 1000 \text{ J}$, we divide our result by $1000$.

$$\Delta G^\ominus = \frac{-24.64 \text{ J mol}^{-1}}{1000} = -0.02464 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$$

Rounding to three significant figures (matching the precision of the given temperature $298 \text{ K}$ and constant $1.01$), we get:

$$\Delta G^\ominus = -0.0246 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$$

gdc.instructions: Divide the previous result by 1000.

ti84.instructions: Divide the previous answer by 1000.

desmos.instructions: Divide the calculation by 1000.

calculator.description: Convert Joules to Kilojoules and verify the final value.

questions.specification:
Sodium thiosulfate ($Na_2S_2O_3$) reacts with dilute hydrochloric acid ($HCl$) to produce a solid precipitate, as shown in the equation below:

$$Na_2S_2O_3(aq) + 2HCl(aq) \rightarrow 2NaCl(aq) + S(s) + SO_2(g) + H_2O(l)$$

In a series of experiments, a student investigated the effect of temperature on the rate of this reaction. They timed how long it took for a black cross placed under the flask to disappear from view due to the formation of sulfur. 

![Disappearing cross experiment setup](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-economics-1fe93708-f7f0-450f-954d-878b5daca7ef-1771937587718/a1d7a7dd-f3a7-436f-bb93-914cda9f5a18.jpeg)

The results are recorded in the table below:

| Temperature ($^\circ\text{C}$) | Time for cross to disappear (s) |
| :--- | :--- |
| 20 | 180 |
| 30 | 105 |
| 40 | 54 |
| 50 | 28 |

parts.content: State the dependent and independent variables in this experiment.

Independent variable: time / s
Dependent variable: concentration of $\mathrm{N_2O}$ / $\text{mol dm}^{-3}$ <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Identify the horizontal axis
steps.content: In scientific graphing, the **independent variable** is the factor that is controlled or chosen by the experimenter (often time) and is plotted on the horizontal x-axis. Looking at the provided graph, the x-axis represents time.

steps.title: Identify the vertical axis
steps.content: The **dependent variable** is the factor that is measured or observed as it changes in response to the independent variable. This is plotted on the vertical y-axis. On this graph, the y-axis shows the concentration of $\text{N}_2\text{O}$.

steps.title: State the variables
steps.content:
Based on the standard graphical conventions:

*   **Independent variable:** time / s
*   **Dependent variable:** concentration of $\text{N}_2\text{O}$ / $\text{mol dm}^{-3}$

steps.title: Identify the independent variable
steps.content:
In any experiment, the **independent variable** is the factor that is controlled or predetermined by the experimenter. In kinetics (the study of reaction rates), we typically want to see how a system evolves over a specific duration. 

On a standard graph, the independent variable is plotted on the **horizontal x-axis**. Looking at the provided graph, the x-axis represents the passage of time.

highlights.text: monitored the concentration of N2O over time

steps.title: Identify the dependent variable
steps.content:
The **dependent variable** is the result or response that is measured during the experiment; its value "depends" on the independent variable. 

On a graph, the dependent variable is plotted on the **vertical y-axis**. Here, the student is measuring how the amount of nitrous oxide changes as the reaction proceeds. Therefore, the dependent variable is the concentration of $\mathrm{N_2O}$.

steps.title: Final Answer Statement
steps.content:
Based on the experimental setup and the graph axes, we can state the variables clearly. It is also good practice to include the units shown on the graph.

*   **Independent variable:** time / $\text{s}$
*   **Dependent variable:** concentration of $\mathrm{N_2O}$ / $\text{mol dm}^{-3}$

highlights.text: Dependent variable: concentration of N2O / mol dm-3

parts.content: Describe the shape of the graph and what it indicates about the reaction rate.
parts.markscheme:
The graph is a straight line with a constant negative slope. <marks>A1</marks>

This shows that the concentration of $\mathrm{N_2O}$ decreases at a constant rate. <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Analyze the graph shape
steps.content:
To answer this question, we first look at the visual properties of the plotted data. The graph of $[\mathrm{N_2O}]$ versus time shows a steady decrease. Specifically, the points form a **straight line** that goes downwards from left to right. 

In mathematics and chemistry, this indicates that the relationship between concentration and time is linear with a **constant negative slope** (or gradient).

highlights.text: straight line with a constant negative slope

steps.title: Link gradient to rate
steps.content:
In a concentration-time graph, the rate of reaction is proportional to the gradient of the line. The mathematical definition of the rate of reaction for a reactant is:

$$\text{Rate} = -\frac{\Delta[\text{Reactant}]}{\Delta t}$$

Because the graph is a straight line, the gradient ($\frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}$) does not change as time progresses. We can verify this by checking the slope at different points:

- From $t=0$ to $t=100$: $\text{slope} = \frac{0.00 - 0.10}{100 - 0} = -0.001$
- From $t=0$ to $t=50$: $\text{slope} = \frac{0.05 - 0.10}{50 - 0} = -0.001$

Since the slope is the same at every point, the rate of reaction is constant.

gdc.instructions: Calculate the difference in y divided by the difference in x for two sections of the line.

ti84.instructions: Enter (0-0.10)/100 to find the overall slope, then (0.05-0.10)/50 to compare.

desmos.instructions: Calculate the slope m = (y2 - y1) / (x2 - x1).

calculator.description: Verify that the gradient is constant by calculating the slope at two different intervals.

steps.title: State final conclusion
steps.content:
Based on our graphical analysis, we can conclude two things for the marks:

1. The graph is a **straight line** with a constant negative gradient.
2. This indicates that the **rate of reaction is constant** (it does not change as the concentration of $\mathrm{N_2O}$ decreases).

This behavior is typical of a zero-order reaction, which you can learn more about in **Topic R2.2: How fast? The rate of chemical change**.

highlights.text: concentration of N2O decreases at a constant rate

steps.title: Analyze the graph shape
steps.content:
First, we look at the relationship between the concentration of $\mathrm{N_2O}$ and time. The data points form a **straight line** that goes downwards. This indicates a **constant negative slope**.

In IB Chemistry, being able to identify these graphical trends is a key skill covered in **Topic R2.2: How fast? The rate of chemical change**.

highlights.text: The graph is a straight line with a constant negative slope.

steps.title: Identify the reaction order
steps.content:
Using the **Reaction Order Identification** approach, we recognize that a linear concentration-time graph is the signature of a **zero-order reaction**. 

For a zero-order reaction, the rate equation is:
$$\text{Rate} = k[\mathrm{N_2O}]^0 = k$$

Because the concentration term is raised to the power of zero, the rate does not depend on how much reactant is present.

steps.title: Determine the rate behavior
steps.content:
Since the slope of a concentration-time graph represents the rate of reaction, and this slope is constant (a straight line), it indicates that the **reaction rate remains constant** over time. 

It does not slow down as the concentration of $\mathrm{N_2O}$ decreases.

gdc.instructions: Divide the change in concentration by the change in time for any two points on the line.

ti84.instructions: Calculate (0.100 - 0.075) / 100 for the first interval and (0.075 - 0.050) / 100 for the second.

desmos.instructions: Plot the points (0, 0.1) and (200, 0.05) and find the slope $m = (y_2 - y_1) / (x_2 - x_1)$.

calculator.description: To verify the rate is constant, we can calculate the slope (rate) at different intervals. For example, between 0 and 100 seconds, and 100 and 200 seconds.

highlights.text: This shows that the concentration of N2O decreases at a constant rate.

steps.title: Final Conclusion
steps.content:
The final answer requires two points:
1. Describe the shape: It is a **straight line** with a **constant negative slope**.
2. State the implication: The **reaction rate is constant** (independent of concentration).

parts.content: The initial concentration of $\mathrm{N_2O}$ is $0.500 \text{ mol dm}^{-3}$ and it decreases to $0.200 \text{ mol dm}^{-3}$ over $60.0 \text{ s}$. Calculate the average rate of decomposition.
parts.markscheme:
Average rate $= \frac{0.500-0.200}{60.0}=\frac{0.300}{60.0}$ <marks>M1</marks>

$= 0.00500 \text{ mol dm}^{-3} \text{ s}^{-1}$ <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Identify the given information
steps.content:
To calculate the average rate of decomposition, we first need to identify the initial and final concentrations of the reactant, $\mathrm{N_2O}$, and the total time elapsed.

*   Initial concentration ($C_{\text{initial}}$) = $0.500 \text{ mol dm}^{-3}$
*   Final concentration ($C_{\text{final}}$) = $0.200 \text{ mol dm}^{-3}$
*   Time interval ($\Delta t$) = $60.0 \text{ s}$

highlights.text: initial concentration of N2O is 0.500 mol dm-3

steps.title: Calculate change in concentration
steps.content:
The average rate of reaction is defined as the change in concentration per unit time. First, we find the total decrease in concentration:

$$\Delta \text{concentration} = C_{\text{initial}} - C_{\text{final}}$$
$$\Delta \text{concentration} = 0.500 \text{ mol dm}^{-3} - 0.200 \text{ mol dm}^{-3} = 0.300 \text{ mol dm}^{-3}$$

This calculation earned the first mark (**M1**) in the markscheme.

steps.title: Determine the average rate
steps.content:
Now, we divide the change in concentration by the total time taken to find the average rate:

$$\text{Average rate} = \frac{\Delta \text{concentration}}{\text{time}}$$
$$\text{Average rate} = \frac{0.300 \text{ mol dm}^{-3}}{60.0 \text{ s}}$$

Using a calculator for this division gives:

$$\text{Average rate} = 0.00500 \text{ mol dm}^{-3} \text{ s}^{-1}$$

gdc.instructions: Type (0.500 - 0.200) / 60.0 and execute.

ti84.instructions: Enter (0.500 - 0.200) / 60.0 and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: In a new expression line, type (0.500 - 0.200) / 60.0.

calculator.description: Calculate the average rate by dividing the change in concentration by the time.

steps.title: State final answer
steps.content:
The average rate of decomposition is $0.00500 \text{ mol dm}^{-3} \text{ s}^{-1}$. 

**Note on precision:** Since our input values ($0.500$, $0.200$, and $60.0$) all have three significant figures, we express the final answer to three significant figures as well ($0.00500$). This ensures we meet the requirements for the final mark (**A1**).

parts.content: Suggest one feature of this reaction that makes it easier to calculate the rate from this graph compared to a curved one.
parts.markscheme: The rate is constant, so the slope (and therefore rate) is the same at any point on the graph. <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Analyze the graph shape
steps.content: By observing the provided plot of $[\mathrm{N_2O}]$ against time, we can see that the data points form a **straight line**. In chemical kinetics, this linear relationship indicates that the concentration of the reactant decreases at a constant rate over time.

steps.title: Identify the constant gradient
steps.content: A defining feature of a straight-line graph is that it has a **constant gradient** (slope) at every point. This is the **Constant Gradient Approach**. Since the gradient of a concentration-time graph represents the rate of reaction, this means the rate remains unchanged throughout the monitored period.

highlights.text: The rate is constant, so the slope (and therefore rate) is the same at any point on the graph.

steps.title: Compare with curved graphs
steps.content: In most reactions, the graph is curved, meaning the rate changes over time. To find the rate on a curve, you must draw a **tangent** at a specific time and calculate its slope. Because this graph is linear, you can simply calculate the slope between any two points on the line without needing to draw any tangents, making the calculation much easier and less prone to drawing errors.

steps.title: Analyze the graph shape
steps.content: First, let's look at the data provided. The graph shows the concentration of $\mathrm{N_2O}$ plotted against time. Notice that the data points form a perfectly straight line with a negative slope.

highlights.text: monitored the concentration of N2O over time

steps.title: Identify zero-order kinetics
steps.content:
In chemistry, specifically in **Topic R2.2 (How fast? The rate of chemical change)**, we learn that the shape of a concentration-time graph reveals the order of the reaction. 

Because this graph is a straight line, it indicates a **zero-order reaction**. This means the rate of decomposition is independent of the concentration of $\mathrm{N_2O}$.

steps.title: Explain the calculation advantage
steps.content:
The "rate" of a reaction is represented by the gradient (slope) of the concentration-time graph. 

For a curved graph (like first-order or second-order reactions), the slope changes at every point, so you have to draw a tangent line to find the instantaneous rate. However, for a straight line, the **gradient is constant**.

steps.title: Final Answer
steps.content: The feature that makes it easier is that the rate is constant throughout the reaction. Therefore, the slope (and the rate) is the same at any point on the graph, so you don't need to draw tangents.

highlights.text: The rate is constant, so the slope (and therefore rate) is the same at any point on the graph.

parts.content: Explain why a straight-line decrease in concentration over time suggests a zero-order reaction.
parts.markscheme:
In a zero-order reaction, the rate is independent of the concentration of the reactant. <marks>A1</marks>

A straight-line decrease in $[\mathrm{N_2O}]$ over time means the same amount decomposes per unit time. <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Link gradient to rate
steps.content:
In a concentration-time graph, the gradient (slope) of the line represents the **rate of reaction**. 

Mathematically, for the decomposition of $N_2O$, we can express the rate as:

$$\text{Rate} = -\frac{\Delta[N_2O]}{\Delta t}$$

highlights.text: monitored the concentration of N2O over time

steps.title: Analyze the graph shape
steps.content: The graph shows a **straight-line decrease**. A straight line has a constant gradient, which tells us that the rate of reaction remains the same throughout the experiment. In other words, the same amount of $N_2O$ decomposes per unit of time, regardless of how much is left.

steps.title: Identify zero-order kinetics
steps.content:
By definition, a **zero-order reaction** is one where the rate is **independent of the concentration** of the reactant. 

Since our graph shows the rate (gradient) does not change as the concentration of $N_2O$ decreases, the reaction must be zero-order with respect to $N_2O$. 

The rate expression would be:

$$\text{Rate} = k[N_2O]^0 = k$$

steps.title: Final Summary for Marks
steps.content:
To secure the 2 marks, your explanation should clearly state:
1. In a **zero-order reaction**, the rate is independent of the reactant concentration.
2. A **straight line** indicates a constant rate (the same amount decomposes per unit time).

steps.title: Analyze the graph shape
steps.content: The graph shows a **straight-line decrease** in the concentration of $\text{N}_2\text{O}$ over time. In mathematics, a straight line follows the linear equation $y = mx + c$, where the gradient ($m$) is constant.

gdc.instructions:
1. Go to the Graphing menu.
2. Enter f1(x) = -0.5x + 10.
3. Plot the function and observe the straight line.

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [Y=].
2. Enter Y1 = -0.5X + 10 (a sample linear equation).
3. Press [GRAPH].

desmos.instructions: Enter the equation y = -0.5x + 10 to see a straight-line decrease similar to the provided graph.

calculator.description: Visualize a linear decrease to see how the slope remains constant.

highlights.text: straight-line decrease in concentration over time

steps.title: Link to Integrated Rate Law
steps.content:
For a **zero-order reaction**, the rate of reaction is constant. This relationship is expressed by the integrated rate law:

$$[\text{A}] = -kt + [\text{A}]_0$$

Where:
- $[\text{A}]$ is the concentration at time $t$
- $k$ is the rate constant
- $[\text{A}]_0$ is the initial concentration

Because this formula matches the $y = mx + c$ form of the graph, the linear decrease confirms the reaction is zero-order.

steps.title: Interpret the constant rate
steps.content:
In a concentration-time graph, the gradient (slope) represents the rate of reaction ($-\frac{\Delta [\text{A}]}{\Delta t}$). Since the line is straight, the gradient is constant throughout the experiment. 

This means the **same amount of reactant decomposes per unit time**, regardless of how much reactant is left.

steps.title: Conclusion for marks
steps.content:
To secure both marks, we summarize:
1.  In a zero-order reaction, the **rate is independent of the concentration** of the reactant.
2.  A straight-line decrease shows that the rate remains constant as the concentration changes, which is the defining characteristic of zero-order kinetics.

parts.content: A student repeats the experiment using a catalyst. Describe how the catalyst affects the rate of reaction with reference to the activation energy and collision theory.
parts.markscheme:
- A catalyst provides an alternative reaction pathway with a lower activation energy ($E_a$) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- On the Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution, the $E_a$ line shifts to the left <marks>1 mark</marks>
- This increases the frequency of successful collisions between reactant particles because more particles possess the required energy <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: On a sketch of volume versus time, compare the original data with a new curve representing the same experiment performed with the same volume of hydrochloric acid but at half the concentration. Assume hydrochloric acid is the limiting reactant.
parts.markscheme:
- The new curve should have a shallower initial gradient (slower rate) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The new curve should level off at exactly half the final volume of the original curve <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification: Which statement is true for the addition of a catalyst to a chemical reaction system?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Reactivity 2. How much, how fast and how far? > R2.2 How fast? The rate of chemical change > R2.2.5 Catalysts
labeledSubSubTopics.title: R2.2.5 Catalysts
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks what is true when a catalyst is added; the correct idea is that it provides an alternative pathway with lower activation energy and does not change equilibrium position, ΔH, or favor forward vs reverse differently.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Reactivity 2. How much, how fast and how far? > R2.2 How fast? The rate of chemical change > R2.2.4 Activation energy
labeledSubSubTopics.title: R2.2.4 Activation energy
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Several options explicitly discuss activation energy changes, which is a key concept tied to how catalysts affect reaction energetics.

options.content: It provides an alternative reaction pathway with a lower activation energy ($E_{\text{a}}$).
options.markscheme: A catalyst works by providing an alternative mechanism or pathway for the reaction which has a lower activation energy ($E_{\text{a}}$) than the original pathway.

options.content: It increases the yield of the products at equilibrium by shifting the reaction to the right.
options.markscheme: A catalyst increases the rates of both the forward and reverse reactions equally, meaning the position of equilibrium and the yield of products remain unchanged.

options.content: It decreases the activation energy of the forward reaction more than that of the reverse reaction.
options.markscheme: A catalyst lowers the activation energy ($E_{\text{a}}$) for both the forward and reverse reactions by the exact same amount.

options.content: It makes the enthalpy change ($\Delta H$) for the reaction more negative.
options.markscheme: The enthalpy change ($\Delta H$) is determined solely by the energy of the reactants and products (state functions) and is not affected by the presence of a catalyst or the reaction pathway.

questions.specification:
Sodium thiosulfate solution ($Na_2S_2O_3$) reacts with dilute hydrochloric acid ($HCl$) to form a solid precipitate. The chemical equation for this reaction is:

$$Na_2S_2O_3(aq) + 2HCl(aq) \rightarrow 2NaCl(aq) + S(s) + SO_2(g) + H_2O(l)$$

A group of students investigated how temperature affects the rate of this reaction. They placed a conical flask over a piece of paper with a black cross and measured the time taken for the cross to disappear from view.

![Experimental Setup](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-physics-new-43fa9e39-ccc6-4f83-8976-bdc4ee317688-1771929956947/466b4f59-b6e9-47bf-8d2c-78395c00d0c6.jpeg)

Their results are recorded in the table below:

| Temperature ($^\circ\text{C}$) | Time taken for cross to disappear (s) |
| :--- | :--- |
| 20 | 90 |
| 30 | 45 |
| 40 | 25 |
| 50 | 15 |

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Reactivity 2. How much, how fast and how far? > R2.2 How fast? The rate of chemical change > R2.2.3 Factors affecting the rate of reaction
labeledSubSubTopics.title: R2.2.3 Factors affecting the rate of reaction
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Main focus is investigating how changing temperature and concentration affects reaction rate and identifying control variables (factors affecting rate).

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Reactivity 2. How much, how fast and how far? > R2.2 How fast? The rate of chemical change > R2.2.2 Collision theory
labeledSubSubTopics.title: R2.2.2 Collision theory
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (b) explicitly asks for an explanation using collision theory (temperature increases frequency/energy of effective collisions).

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Reactivity 2. How much, how fast and how far? > R2.2 How fast? The rate of chemical change > R2.2.1 Rate of reaction
labeledSubSubTopics.title: R2.2.1 Rate of reaction
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Parts (c) and (e) involve calculating/using rate as 1/time and predicting changes in time as a proxy for reaction rate.

parts.content: Identify one variable, other than temperature, that the students must keep constant to ensure their results are valid.
parts.markscheme: - **Identify** a valid control variable such as the concentration of $HCl$ / concentration of $Na_2S_2O_3$ / total volume of the reaction mixture / size of the cross used <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain, in terms of the collision theory, why the reaction rate increases as the temperature of the reactants is increased.
parts.markscheme:
- **Explain** that at higher temperatures, particles have more kinetic energy and move faster <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **State** that this leads to more frequent collisions AND a higher proportion of collisions having energy greater than or equal to the activation energy ($E_a$) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
The relative rate of reaction can be estimated using the formula:

$$\text{Rate} = \frac{1}{\text{time}}$$

Using the data from the table, calculate the relative rate of reaction at $40 ^\circ\text{C}$. Give your answer to two decimal places.
parts.markscheme:
- **Substitute** the value from the table: $\text{Rate} = \frac{1}{25}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Calculate** the final value: $0.04$ (s$^{-1}$) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: State the name of the product formed in this reaction that causes the solution to become opaque and obscure the cross.
parts.markscheme: - **State** the name as sulfur <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: In a separate experiment at a constant temperature of $20 ^\circ\text{C}$, the concentration of sodium thiosulfate was doubled. Predict how this change would affect the time taken for the cross to disappear and explain your answer in terms of particles.
parts.markscheme:
- **Predict** that the time taken would decrease (the reaction would be faster) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Explain** that there are more reacting particles in a given volume, which increases the frequency of successful collisions <marks>1 mark</marks>

labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Parts (f) and (g) use temperature change and specific heat capacity to calculate heat released (q = mcΔT), i.e., energy transfer in reactions.

parts.content: State one method the student could use to measure the rate of this reaction.
parts.markscheme:
Measuring the volume of oxygen gas produced over time (using a gas syringe) <marks>1 mark</marks>

OR

Measuring the decrease in mass of the reaction vessel over time <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
The student records the relative rate of reaction under different conditions. The results are shown in the table below. In each trial, $0.20\text{ g}$ of $\text{MnO}_2(\text{s})$ is added to $40.0\text{ cm}^3$ of $\text{H}_2\text{O}_2(\text{aq})$.

| Trial | $[\text{H}_2\text{O}_2(\text{aq})]$ / $\text{mol dm}^{-3}$ | Temperature / $\text{K}$ | Relative Rate |
|-------|----------------------------------------------|--------------------------|---------------|
| 1     | 0.20                                         | 298                      | 1.0           |
| 2     | 0.40                                         | 298                      | 2.0           |
| 3     | 0.20                                         | 308                      | 3.5           |
| 4     | 0.40                                         | 308                      | 7.0           |
| 5     | 0.40                                         | 298                      | 4.8           |

Identify the trial that contains an anomalous result and justify your choice using the data in the table.
parts.markscheme:
Trial 5 <marks>1 mark</marks>

Compared to Trial 2, which has the same concentration and temperature <marks>1 mark</marks>

The rate is significantly higher than expected (4.8 vs 2.0) / does not follow the trend seen in Trials 1 and 2 where doubling concentration doubles the rate <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Using collision theory, explain the difference in the relative rates of trials 1 and 3.
parts.markscheme:
Higher temperature in trial 3 increases the average kinetic energy of the particles <marks>1 mark</marks>rks>

A greater proportion of particles have energy greater than or equal to the activation energy, leading to more frequent successful collisions <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Using the data for trials 1 and 2, deduce the order of reaction with respect to $\text{H}_2\text{O}_2(\text{aq})$.
parts.markscheme:
When the concentration of $\text{H}_2\text{O}_2$ doubles (from 0.20 to 0.40), the rate doubles (from 1.0 to 2.0) <marks>1 mark</marks>

Therefore, the reaction is first-order <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
In a separate experiment, $40.0\text{ cm}^3$ of $\text{H}_2\text{O}_2(\text{aq})$ decomposes completely to produce $120\text{ cm}^3$ of oxygen gas, measured at room temperature and pressure (RTP). Calculate the concentration of the original $\text{H}_2\text{O}_2(\text{aq})$ solution. (Molar volume of gas at RTP $= 24.0\text{ dm}^3\text{ mol}^{-1}$).

$$2\text{H}_2\text{O}_2(\text{aq}) \rightarrow 2\text{H}_2\text{O}(\text{l}) + \text{O}_2(\text{g})$$
parts.markscheme:
$n(\text{O}_2) = \frac{0.120}{24.0} = 0.00500\text{ mol}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

$n(\text{H}_2\text{O}_2) = 0.00500 \times 2 = 0.0100\text{ mol}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

$[\text{H}_2\text{O}_2] = \frac{0.0100}{0.0400} = 0.250\text{ mol dm}^{-3}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
The student monitors the temperature of the mixture during the decomposition of $40.0\text{ g}$ of $\text{H}_2\text{O}_2$ solution. The results are shown below.

| Time / s | Temperature / $^\circ\text{C}$ |
|----------|------------------------------|
| 0        | 18.50                        |
| 30       | 19.85                        |
| 60       | 20.75                        |
| 90       | 21.20                        |
| 120      | 21.40                        |
| 150      | 21.40                        |

Determine the maximum temperature change recorded during the experiment.
parts.markscheme: Max temperature change $= 21.40 - 18.50 = 2.90^\circ\text{C}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Calculate the total heat energy, in joules, released during this reaction. Assume the specific heat capacity of the solution is $4.18\text{ J g}^{-1} \, ^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$ and the mass of the solution is $40.0\text{ g}$.
parts.markscheme:
$Q = 40.0 \times 4.18 \times 2.90$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

$Q = 485\text{ J}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification:

$$2\text{H}_2\text{O}_2 (aq) \rightarrow 2\text{H}_2\text{O} (l) + \text{O}_2 (g)$$ 

A student investigated the effect of various factors on the rate of this reaction.

parts.content:
The Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution curve for a sample of hydrogen peroxide at a temperature $T$ is shown below.

![Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-economics-4944a4d3-05f5-45dd-849a-1dc965cddcff-1771937587718/5c25ec53-7b76-4a56-afbf-b995c95c4817.jpeg)

On the diagram, mark the activation energy, $E_a$, and shade the area representing the particles that have sufficient energy to react at this temperature.
parts.markscheme:
- Activation energy, $E_a$, marked on the x-axis to the right of the peak <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Correct shading of the area under the curve to the right of the $E_a$ line <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Sketch a second curve on the diagram to show the distribution of molecular energies for the same sample of hydrogen peroxide at a lower temperature.
parts.markscheme:
- Curve peak is higher and shifted to the left (lower kinetic energy) <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Curve remains below the original curve at higher energies (the tail is lower) <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>The area under both curves should be approximately equal as the number of particles remains constant.</note>

parts.content:
The volume of oxygen gas produced during the catalyzed decomposition was recorded over time as follows:

| Time / s | Volume of $\text{O}_2$ / $\text{cm}^3$ |
| :--- | :--- |
| 0 | 0 |
| 20 | 22 |
| 40 | 36 |
| 60 | 46 |
| 80 | 54 |

Calculate the average rate of reaction, in $\text{cm}^3 \text{ s}^{-1}$, over the first 40 seconds.
parts.markscheme:
- Correct identification of values from the table: $\Delta V = 36 - 0 = 36$ and $\Delta t = 40 - 0 = 40$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

- $\text{Average rate} = \frac{36 \text{ cm}^3}{40 \text{ s}} = 0.90 \text{ cm}^3 \text{ s}^{-1}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain, using collision theory, why adding a small amount of manganese(IV) oxide increases the rate of decomposition.
parts.markscheme:
- Explains that the catalyst provides an alternative reaction pathway with a lower activation energy, $E_a$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Explains that a greater proportion (or number) of particles now possess energy $\ge E_a$, leading to more frequent successful collisions <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Calculate the maximum volume of oxygen gas, in $\text{dm}^3$, produced at STP if $60.0 \text{ cm}^3$ of $1.20 \text{ mol dm}^{-3}$ hydrogen peroxide solution reacts completely. (Molar volume of a gas at STP, $V_m = 22.7 \text{ dm}^3 \text{ mol}^{-1}$)
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate the amount in moles of $\text{H}_2\text{O}_2$:
$n(\text{H}_2\text{O}_2) = 0.0600 \text{ dm}^3 \times 1.20 \text{ mol dm}^{-3} = 0.0720 \text{ mol}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Determine the moles of $\text{O}_2$ produced ($2:1$ ratio):
$n(\text{O}_2) = \frac{0.0720 \text{ mol}}{2} = 0.0360 \text{ mol}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Calculate the volume of $\text{O}_2$:
$V = 0.0360 \text{ mol} \times 22.7 \text{ dm}^3 \text{ mol}^{-1} = 0.817 \text{ dm}^3$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification:
A student investigated the rate of reaction between excess marble chips (calcium carbonate) and $50.0 \text{ cm}^3$ of $1.0 \text{ mol dm}^{-3}$ hydrochloric acid using the apparatus shown below.

![Experimental setup](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-chemistry-new-54b79262-98bc-4baf-82ca-a60421a6b52c-1771938518510/7ba19fee-7ef1-4b99-9cc9-fe13b9a5142e.jpeg)

The reaction is represented by the equation:
$$\text{CaCO}_3(s) + 2\text{HCl}(aq) \rightarrow \text{CaCl}_2(aq) + \text{H}_2\text{O}(l) + \text{CO}_2(g)$$

The student recorded the total mass of the flask and its contents over time as follows:

| Time (s) | Mass of flask and contents (g) |
| :--- | :--- |
| 0 | 200.00 |
| 20 | 199.30 |
| 40 | 198.85 |
| 60 | 198.55 |
| 80 | 198.40 |
| 100 | 198.35 |

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Reactivity 2. How much, how fast and how far? > R2.2 How fast? The rate of chemical change > R2.2.1 Rate of reaction
labeledSubSubTopics.title: R2.2.1 Rate of reaction
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Parts (b) and (c) require calculating an average rate from mass change over time and describing how rate changes during the experiment.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Reactivity 2. How much, how fast and how far? > R2.2 How fast? The rate of chemical change > R2.2.2 Collision theory
labeledSubSubTopics.title: R2.2.2 Collision theory
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (c) explicitly asks to explain the rate trend using collision theory (changing frequency of successful collisions as reactants are used up).

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Reactivity 2. How much, how fast and how far? > R2.2 How fast? The rate of chemical change > R2.2.3 Factors affecting the rate of reaction
labeledSubSubTopics.title: R2.2.3 Factors affecting the rate of reaction
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (d) asks about effect of using powdered CaCO3 vs chips (surface area) on initial rate—one of the key factors affecting rate.

parts.content: State the specific function of the cotton wool plug in the neck of the conical flask during this experiment.
parts.markscheme: - **State** that the cotton wool allows carbon dioxide gas to escape while preventing the loss of liquid spray (or acid droplets). <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Calculate the average rate of reaction, in $\text{g s}^{-1}$, over the first 40 seconds of the experiment.
parts.markscheme:
- **Calculate** the total mass loss: $200.00 - 198.85 = 1.15 \text{ g}$. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Divide** by time to find the rate: $\frac{1.15 \text{ g}}{40 \text{ s}} = 0.02875 \text{ g s}^{-1}$ (accept $0.0288$ or $0.029$). <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Using the data in the table, identify the trend in the rate of reaction over time and explain this trend using collision theory.
parts.markscheme:
- **Identify** that the rate of reaction decreases over time. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Explain** that as the reaction proceeds, the concentration of the acid (reactants) decreases, leading to a lower frequency of collisions between reactant particles. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Predict and explain the effect on the initial rate of reaction if the student repeated the experiment using the same mass of finely powdered calcium carbonate instead of large marble chips.
parts.markscheme:
- **Predict** that the initial rate of reaction would increase. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Explain** that powdered calcium carbonate has a larger surface area, which results in a higher frequency of successful collisions. <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification:
Which of the following experimental techniques is **least** suitable for monitoring the rate of the reaction shown below?

$$\text{IO}_3^-\text{(aq)} + 5\text{I}^-\text{(aq)} + 6\text{H}^+\text{(aq)} \rightarrow 3\text{I}_2\text{(aq)} + 3\text{H}_2\text{O(l)}$$

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Reactivity 2. How much, how fast and how far? > R2.2 How fast? The rate of chemical change > R2.2.1 Rate of reaction
labeledSubSubTopics.title: R2.2.1 Rate of reaction
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks which experimental technique is least suitable for monitoring reaction rate, i.e., how to measure rate via observable changes during a reaction.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Reactivity 3. What are the mechanisms of chemical change? > R3.1 Proton transfer reactions > R3.1.4 pH and the hydrogen ion concentration
labeledSubSubTopics.title: R3.1.4 pH and the hydrogen ion concentration
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: One option is pH measurement and the reaction consumes H+, so suitability depends on how [H+] (and thus pH) changes over time.

options.markscheme: The concentration of $\text{H}^+$ ions decreases as the reaction proceeds, making pH measurement a valid method for following the rate.
There are no gaseous reactants or products in this reaction, so gas collection cannot be used to monitor the rate.
The formation of aqueous iodine ($\text{I}_2$) creates a colored solution, the intensity of which can be monitored over time.
The reaction consumes 12 moles of ions for every 3 moles of product, resulting in a significant decrease in electrical conductivity.

questions.specification:
The following initial rate data was collected for the reaction $2A + B \rightarrow C$ at a constant temperature:

| Experiment | $[A]_0$ / $mol\ dm^{-3}$ | $[B]_0$ / $mol\ dm^{-3}$ | Initial rate / $mol\ dm^{-3}\ s^{-1}$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | 0.10 | 0.10 | $2.5 \times 10^{-2}$ |
| 2 | 0.20 | 0.10 | $1.0 \times 10^{-1}$ |
| 3 | 0.20 | 0.20 | $2.0 \times 10^{-1}$ |

What are the correct units for the rate constant, $k$, of this reaction?

labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks for the correct units of the rate constant k, which depends on overall reaction order determined from initial-rate data.

labeledSubSubTopics.title: R2.2.10 Order of a reaction (Higher Level Only)

labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Uses an implied rate equation (rate = k[A]^m[B]^n) inferred from initial rates to deduce k’s units.

\text{A} \to \text{B}

The data collected is summarized in the following graph:
