parts.content: Explain, using collision theory, how an increase in temperature affects the rate of reaction.
parts.markscheme:
Explain the effect of temperature increase:
- average kinetic energy of the particles increases <marks>M1</marks>
- more frequent collisions **OR** a greater proportion of particles have energy $\ge E_a$ / successful collisions <marks>A1</marks>

[2 marks total]

steps.title: Temperature and Kinetic Energy
steps.content: According to the Kinetic Molecular Theory, temperature is a direct measure of the average kinetic energy of the particles in a substance. When the temperature of the reaction mixture is increased (for example, from $20^{\circ}\text{C}$ to $50^{\circ}\text{C}$), the magnesium atoms and the hydrogen ions ($H^+$) in the hydrochloric acid gain more energy and move faster.

highlights.text: average kinetic energy of the particles increases

steps.title: Increase in Collision Frequency
steps.content: Because the particles are moving at higher velocities, they will encounter one another more often. This results in a higher **frequency of collisions** between the $Mg$ and $H^+$ ions per unit of time. In simple terms, more collisions happen every second.

steps.title: Impact on Reaction Rate
steps.content: Since the rate of reaction depends on how often reacting particles collide, the increase in collision frequency leads to a faster rate of hydrogen gas ($H_2$) production. This is clearly visible in the provided graph, where the curves for higher temperatures have a steeper initial gradient.

steps.title: Final Summary for Marks
steps.content:
To secure both marks in an IB exam, your explanation must include:
1. The increase in **average kinetic energy** of the particles.
2. The resulting increase in the **frequency of collisions** (or "more frequent collisions").

steps.title: Link temperature to energy
steps.content:
To start, we need to recall the definition of temperature in terms of particles. As the temperature of the system increases, the **average kinetic energy** of the reactant particles (magnesium and hydrochloric acid) increases.

This means that, on average, the particles are moving faster.

steps.title: Define the energy barrier
steps.content:
In collision theory, not every collision results in a reaction. For a collision to be successful, particles must collide with energy equal to or greater than the **activation energy** ($E_a$).

We can see the relationship between temperature ($T$) and the rate constant ($k$) in the Arrhenius equation from your data booklet:

$$k=A e^{\frac{-E_{a}}{R T}}$$

This formula shows that as $T$ increases, the value of $k$ (and thus the rate) increases exponentially.

steps.title: Explain the success rate
steps.content:
When the temperature is higher, the distribution of particle energies shifts. This results in a **significantly greater proportion of particles** possessing energy $E \ge E_a$.

Because a larger percentage of the colliding particles now meet the minimum energy requirement, the **frequency of successful collisions** increases, which directly leads to a faster rate of reaction.

steps.title: Link temperature to kinetic energy
steps.content:
To understand how temperature affects reaction rates, we first need to look at what temperature actually represents. In chemistry, temperature is a measure of the **average kinetic energy** of the particles in a substance. 

When you increase the temperature from $20^{\circ}\text{C}$ to $50^{\circ}\text{C}$, as seen in the experiment, you are providing more energy to the magnesium atoms and the hydrochloric acid particles. This causes them to move faster on average.

This concept is a fundamental part of **Topic S1.1.3: Temperature and Kinetic Energy**.

highlights.text: average kinetic energy of the particles increases

steps.title: Analyze the Maxwell-Boltzmann shift
steps.content:
We can visualize this change using a **Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution curve**, which shows the distribution of kinetic energies in a sample of gas or liquid particles.

As the temperature increases:
- The curve shifts to the **right** (indicating higher average energy).
- The peak of the curve becomes **lower** and broader (because the total number of particles remains the same, but they are spread over a wider range of higher energies).

This shift is crucial because it changes how many particles are capable of reacting.

steps.title: Overcoming the activation energy
steps.content:
For a collision to result in a reaction, particles must collide with energy greater than or equal to the **activation energy** ($E_a$). 

On a Maxwell-Boltzmann graph, $E_a$ is a fixed point on the x-axis. When the curve shifts to the right at a higher temperature, the **area under the curve** to the right of $E_a$ increases significantly. 

This means a much **greater proportion of particles** now possess the minimum energy required ($E \ge E_a$) to have successful collisions.

highlights.text: greater proportion of particles have energy \ge E_a

steps.title: Conclude on reaction rate
steps.content:
Finally, we combine these points to explain the rate increase. Because more particles have enough energy to react, the **frequency of successful collisions** increases. 

In the provided data, we see this clearly: at $50^{\circ}\text{C}$, the volume of $\text{H}_2$ produced in the first 30 seconds ($30 \text{ cm}^3$) is triple the volume produced at $20^{\circ}\text{C}$ ($10 \text{ cm}^3$). This confirms that the reaction is much faster at higher temperatures due to the increased proportion of high-energy particles.

gdc.instructions: Perform the division 30 / 10.

ti84.instructions: Divide the volume at 50°C by the volume at 20°C to see the factor of increase.

desmos.instructions: Enter 30/10 to find the ratio.

calculator.description: Compare the initial rate of hydrogen production at 20°C vs 50°C using the 30s data.

parts.content: Identify and discuss two possible sources of uncertainty in this experiment.
parts.markscheme:
Identify and discuss sources of uncertainty:
- gas volume measurement / reading the gas syringe / graduated cylinder <marks>M1</marks>
- leads to random uncertainty in volume readings / parallax error / gas leak from syringe <marks>A1</marks>
- temperature fluctuations during the experiment <marks>M1</marks>
- affects the rate constant/reaction rate leading to inconsistent kinetics data during a trial <marks>A1</marks>

[4 marks total]

steps.title: Identify gas volume uncertainty
steps.content:
The first source of uncertainty relates to the **instrumental resolution** of the equipment used to collect the hydrogen gas, such as a gas syringe or a graduated cylinder. 

Every measuring instrument has a limit to its precision (e.g., a $100\text{ cm}^3$ gas syringe might have graduations every $1\text{ cm}^3$), which introduces an inherent uncertainty in every reading taken at 30, 60, 120, and 180 seconds.

highlights.text: gas volume measurement / reading the gas syringe / graduated cylinder

steps.title: Discuss observational errors
steps.content:
In addition to the instrument's limit, **observational errors** occur when reading the volume. For example, if the experimenter's eye is not perfectly level with the plunger or meniscus, **parallax error** occurs.

Furthermore, because the gas is produced continuously, it is difficult to record the exact volume at the *precise* moment the stopwatch hits the interval. This leads to **random uncertainty** in the volume data, making the plotted points on the graph less precise.

highlights.text: leads to random uncertainty in volume readings / parallax error / gas leak from syringe

steps.title: Identify temperature fluctuations
steps.content:
The second source of uncertainty is the difficulty in maintaining a **constant temperature** throughout the duration of each 3-minute trial. 

While the experiment aims to test the effect of temperature, the actual temperature of the reaction mixture might fluctuate due to heat exchange with the surroundings or the fact that the reaction between magnesium and acid is **exothermic** (releasing heat).

steps.title: Discuss impact on kinetics
steps.content:
Temperature fluctuations significantly impact the results because the **rate constant** ($k$) is highly sensitive to temperature, as shown by the **Arrhenius equation** in the data booklet:

$$k = A e^{\frac{-E_a}{RT}}$$

If the temperature increases during a "$20\text{ }^{\circ}\text{C}$" trial, the particles gain more kinetic energy than intended, increasing the frequency of successful collisions. This results in inconsistent kinetics data, where the rate of gas production might appear faster than it should be for that specific set temperature.

highlights.text: affects the rate constant/reaction rate leading to inconsistent kinetics data during a trial

steps.title: Analyze the experimental setup
steps.content:
To identify uncertainties in this experiment, we need to look at how the variables were controlled and measured. In the reaction between magnesium and hydrochloric acid, we are measuring the volume of gas produced at specific temperatures. 

Two main areas where errors often occur in this type of setup are **gas containment** and **temperature stability**.

steps.title: Uncertainty 1: Gas containment
steps.content:
The first source of uncertainty relates to the apparatus used to collect the hydrogen gas. 

*   **Identification:** Potential gas leaks from the apparatus (e.g., around the rubber bung or the syringe connection).
*   **Discussion:** If the seal is not perfectly airtight, some $H_2$ gas will escape into the surroundings. This leads to a recorded volume that is lower than the actual volume produced. Additionally, there is a random uncertainty associated with **reading the gas syringe** due to parallax error or the plunger sticking, which affects the precision of the volume measurement.

highlights.text: gas volume measurement / reading the gas syringe / graduated cylinder

steps.title: Uncertainty 2: Temperature control
steps.content:
The second source of uncertainty involves the independent variable: temperature. 

*   **Identification:** Temperature fluctuations during the course of the reaction.
*   **Discussion:** The reaction between $\text{Mg}$ and $\text{HCl}$ is exothermic (it releases heat). This heat can cause the temperature of the reaction mixture to rise above the intended starting temperature. Because the rate constant $k$ is highly sensitive to temperature (as shown in the Arrhenius equation), these fluctuations lead to inconsistent kinetics data and make it difficult to determine the exact effect of the initial temperature on the rate.

highlights.text: affects the rate constant/reaction rate leading to inconsistent kinetics data during a trial

steps.title: Link to Data Booklet
steps.content:
The reason temperature fluctuations are so critical is found in the **Arrhenius equation** from your data booklet:

$$k = Ae^{\frac{-E_a}{RT}}$$

Since $T$ (temperature in Kelvin) is in the exponent, even a small fluctuation in temperature during the 3-minute measurement period can significantly change the rate constant ($k$), introducing error into the results. For more on this, you can review **Topic R2.2: How fast? The rate of chemical change**.

steps.title: Summary of Marks
steps.content:
To earn the full 4 marks, you must both identify the source and discuss its impact:

1.  **Identify:** Gas volume measurement / reading the syringe (**M1**).
2.  **Discuss:** Leads to random uncertainty or gas leaks causing loss of product (**A1**).
3.  **Identify:** Temperature fluctuations during the experiment (**M1**).
4.  **Discuss:** Fluctuations change the rate constant ($k$), leading to inconsistent kinetics data (**A1**).

parts.content: Using the data for the reaction at $40^{\circ}\text{C}$, calculate the average rate of hydrogen gas production (in $\text{cm}^3\,\text{s}^{-1}$) between 30 and 120 seconds.
parts.markscheme:
Calculate average rate at $40^{\circ}\text{C}$:
- at $30\text{ s}: 25\text{ cm}^3$, at $120\text{ s}: 60\text{ cm}^3$ so $\Delta V = 35\text{ cm}^3$ and $\Delta t = 90\text{ s}$ <marks>M1</marks>
- $\text{average rate} = \frac{35}{90} = 0.39\text{ cm}^3\,\text{s}^{-1}$ <marks>A1</marks>

*Accept $0.389$.*

[2 marks total]

steps.title: Identify data for 40°C
steps.content:
To calculate the average rate, we first need to extract the specific volumes of hydrogen gas produced at the given times from the data table. We look specifically at the row for the **reaction at 40°C**.

From the table, we find:
*   Volume at $30\text{ s} = 25\text{ cm}^3$
*   Volume at $120\text{ s} = 60\text{ cm}^3$

steps.title: Calculate the changes
steps.content:
The average rate of a reaction is defined as the change in quantity (volume in this case) divided by the change in time:

$$\text{Rate} = \frac{\Delta V}{\Delta t}$$

First, calculate the change in volume ($\Delta V$):
$$\Delta V = 60\text{ cm}^3 - 25\text{ cm}^3 = 35\text{ cm}^3$$

Next, calculate the change in time ($\Delta t$):
$$\Delta t = 120\text{ s} - 30\text{ s} = 90\text{ s}$$

steps.title: Calculate the average rate
steps.content:
Now, substitute these values into the rate equation to find the average production rate:

$$\text{Average rate} = \frac{35\text{ cm}^3}{90\text{ s}} \approx 0.39\text{ cm}^3\text{ s}^{-1}$$

According to the markscheme, $0.389$ is also accepted if you use more decimal places.

gdc.instructions: Enter 35 / 90 and press EXE or ENTER.

ti84.instructions: Enter 35 divided by 90 on the main screen and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type 35 / 90 into the expression bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the average rate by dividing the volume change by the time change.

steps.title: Final Result
steps.content:
The average rate of hydrogen gas production between 30 and 120 seconds at $40^{\circ}\text{C}$ is **$0.39\text{ cm}^3\text{ s}^{-1}$**.

You earn one mark for identifying the correct change in volume and time ($35/90$), and a second mark for the final numerical answer.

steps.title: Identify the data points
steps.content:
To find the average rate of reaction between two points in time, we look at the change in volume of hydrogen gas over that specific time interval. For the reaction at $40^{\circ}\text{C}$, we need to identify the volume at $30\text{ s}$ and $120\text{ s}$.

Looking at the data table for $40^{\circ}\text{C}$:
- At $t_1 = 30\text{ s}$, $V_1 = 25\text{ cm}^3$
- At $t_2 = 120\text{ s}$, $V_2 = 60\text{ cm}^3$

highlights.text: Using the data for the reaction at 40°C

steps.title: Calculate the changes
steps.content:
The average rate of reaction is equivalent to the gradient (slope) of the secant line connecting these two points on the graph. We first calculate the change in volume ($\Delta V$) and the change in time ($\Delta t$).

$$\begin{aligned} \Delta V &= V_2 - V_1 \\ &= 60\text{ cm}^3 - 25\text{ cm}^3 \\ &= 35\text{ cm}^3 \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \Delta t &= t_2 - t_1 \\ &= 120\text{ s} - 30\text{ s} \\ &= 90\text{ s} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Perform the subtractions on the main calculation screen.

ti84.instructions: Enter the subtractions 60 - 25 and 120 - 30.

desmos.instructions: Calculate the differences in separate lines.

calculator.description: Calculate the difference in volume and time.

steps.title: Calculate the average rate
steps.content:
Now, we divide the change in volume by the change in time to find the average rate of production.

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Average Rate} &= \frac{\Delta V}{\Delta t} \\ &= \frac{35\text{ cm}^3}{90\text{ s}} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter 35 divided by 90.

ti84.instructions: Divide 35 by 90.

desmos.instructions: Type 35 / 90 to see the decimal result.

calculator.description: Divide the change in volume by the change in time.

steps.title: Final answer and units
steps.content:
Evaluating the division gives us the average rate:

$$\text{Average Rate} \approx 0.39\text{ cm}^3\text{ s}^{-1}$$

According to the markscheme, $0.389$ is also an acceptable answer. This value represents how much hydrogen gas was produced, on average, every second during that 90-second window.

parts.content:
Determine the activation energy $(E_a)$ for this reaction using the Arrhenius equation and the following additional information:
- The reaction at $20^{\circ}\text{C}$ has a rate constant $k_1 = 1.2 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{s}^{-1}$.
- The reaction at $40^{\circ}\text{C}$ has a rate constant $k_2 = 2.5 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{s}^{-1}$.

Assume the gas constant $R = 8.31\text{ J}\,\text{mol}^{-1}\,\text{K}^{-1}$.
Provide your answer in $\text{kJ}\,\text{mol}^{-1}$.
parts.markscheme:
Determine $E_a$:
- calculate $\ln\left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right) = \ln\left(\frac{2.5 \times 10^{-3}}{1.2 \times 10^{-3}}\right) = 0.734$ <marks>M1</marks>
- calculate change in reciprocal temperature: $\left(\frac{1}{293} - \frac{1}{313}\right) = 2.18 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{K}^{-1}$ <marks>M1</marks>
- $E_a = \frac{0.734 \times 8.31}{2.18 \times 10^{-4}} = 27978\text{ J}\,\text{mol}^{-1} = 28.0\text{ kJ}\,\text{mol}^{-1}$ <marks>A1</marks>

*Accept range $27.7$ to $28.0$ depending on intermediate rounding.*

[3 marks total]

steps.title: Identify variables and formula
steps.content:
To find the activation energy ($E_a$), we use the integrated **two-point form of the Arrhenius equation**. This equation relates the rate constants at two different temperatures:

$$\ln\left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right) = \frac{E_a}{R} \left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)$$

From the question, we have:
- $k_1 = 1.2 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{s}^{-1}$ at $T_1 = 20^{\circ}\text{C}$
- $k_2 = 2.5 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{s}^{-1}$ at $T_2 = 40^{\circ}\text{C}$
- $R = 8.31\text{ J mol}^{-1} \text{K}^{-1}$

This calculation is covered in **Topic R2.2: How fast? The rate of chemical change** (Higher Level).

highlights.text: The reaction at 20°C has a rate constant k1 = 1.2 × 10−3 s−1.
The reaction at 40°C has a rate constant k2 = 2.5 × 10−3 s−1.

steps.title: Convert temperature to Kelvin
steps.content:
In chemistry calculations involving gas constants or thermodynamics, temperature must always be in Kelvin (K). 

$$\text{T(K)} = \text{T(}^{\circ}\text{C)} + 273$$

$$\begin{aligned} T_1 &= 20 + 273 = 293\text{ K} \\ T_2 &= 40 + 273 = 313\text{ K} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate the logarithmic ratio
steps.content:
First, calculate the natural logarithm of the ratio of the rate constants ($k_2/k_1$). This accounts for how much the rate constant increases as temperature rises.

$$\ln\left(\frac{2.5 \times 10^{-3}}{1.2 \times 10^{-3}}\right) = \ln(2.0833...) \approx 0.734$$

gdc.instructions: Enter ln(2.5/1.2) into the main calculation screen.

ti84.instructions: Press [LN], enter (2.5 / 1.2), then press [ENTER].

desmos.instructions: Type ln(2.5/1.2) into an expression line.

calculator.description: Calculate the natural log of the rate constant ratio.

steps.title: Calculate temperature change
steps.content:
Next, calculate the difference between the reciprocals of the absolute temperatures:

$$\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right) = \left(\frac{1}{293} - \frac{1}{313}\right)$$

$$\frac{1}{293} - \frac{1}{313} \approx 0.003413 - 0.003195 = 2.18 \times 10^{-4}\text{ K}^{-1}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter (1/293) - (1/313) on the calculation screen.

ti84.instructions: Enter (1 / 293) - (1 / 313) then press [ENTER].

desmos.instructions: Type (1/293) - (1/313) into an expression line.

calculator.description: Calculate the difference in reciprocal temperatures.

steps.title: Solve for activation energy
steps.content:
Now, substitute these values back into the rearranged equation to solve for $E_a$:

$$E_a = \frac{R \cdot \ln\left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right)}{\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)}$$

$$E_a = \frac{8.31 \times 0.734}{2.18 \times 10^{-4}} = 27978\text{ J mol}^{-1}$$

gdc.instructions: Perform the calculation (8.31 * 0.734) / 0.000218.

ti84.instructions: Enter (8.31 * 0.734) / 2.18e-4 and press [ENTER].

desmos.instructions: Enter (8.31 * 0.734) / 0.000218 to get the result.

calculator.description: Calculate the final activation energy in Joules.

highlights.text: Gas constant R = 8.31 J K−1 mol−1

steps.title: State final answer in kJ
steps.content:
The question asks for the answer in $\text{kJ mol}^{-1}$. To convert from Joules (J) to kiloJoules (kJ), divide by $1000$:

$$E_a = \frac{27978}{1000} = 27.978\text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$$

Rounding to 3 significant figures (as per the input data):

$$E_a = 28.0\text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$$

steps.title: Convert temperature to Kelvin
steps.content:
Before using the Arrhenius equation, we must ensure the temperatures are in Kelvin (K). The gas constant $R$ uses Kelvin, so our values must match.

$$\begin{aligned} T_1 &= 20^{\circ}\text{C} + 273 = 293\text{ K} \\ T_2 &= 40^{\circ}\text{C} + 273 = 313\text{ K} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Add 273 to both temperatures.

ti84.instructions: Add 273 to both temperatures.

desmos.instructions: Add 273 to both temperatures.

calculator.description: Convert Celsius to Kelvin

steps.title: Set up simultaneous equations
steps.content:
The Arrhenius equation can be written in a linear form: $\ln k = -\frac{E_a}{RT} + \ln A$. We can set this up for both temperatures to create a system of equations.

Equation 1 (at $293\text{ K}$):
$$\ln(1.2 \times 10^{-3}) = -\frac{E_a}{R(293)} + \ln A$$

Equation 2 (at $313\text{ K}$):
$$\ln(2.5 \times 10^{-3}) = -\frac{E_a}{R(313)} + \ln A$$

steps.title: Subtract equations to eliminate A
steps.content:
By subtracting Equation 1 from Equation 2, we eliminate the constant $\ln A$. This gives us the combined Arrhenius equation used for two temperatures:

$$\ln k_2 - \ln k_1 = \left(-\frac{E_a}{RT_2} + \ln A\right) - \left(-\frac{E_a}{RT_1} + \ln A\right)$$

Using log laws ($\ln a - \ln b = \ln \frac{a}{b}$), this simplifies to:

$$\ln\left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right) = \frac{E_a}{R} \left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)$$

steps.title: Calculate the left-hand side
steps.content:
Let's calculate the ratio of the rate constants:

$$\ln\left(\frac{2.5 \times 10^{-3}}{1.2 \times 10^{-3}}\right) = \ln(2.083...)$$

Using a calculator, we find:
$$\ln(2.083...) \approx 0.734$$

gdc.instructions: Evaluate ln(2.5/1.2).

ti84.instructions: Press [ln], then (2.5E-3 / 1.2E-3), and [ENTER].

calculator.description: Calculate natural log of the rate constant ratio

steps.title: Calculate the temperature term
steps.content:
Now we calculate the difference in the reciprocals of the temperatures:

$$\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right) = \frac{1}{293} - \frac{1}{313}$$

$$\frac{1}{293} - \frac{1}{313} \approx 2.18 \times 10^{-4} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate 1/293 - 1/313.

ti84.instructions: Calculate 1/293 - 1/313.

desmos.instructions: Calculate 1/293 - 1/313.

calculator.description: Calculate the change in reciprocal temperature

steps.title: Solve for activation energy
steps.content:
Substitute the calculated values into our combined equation to solve for $E_a$. Remember to use the gas constant $R = 8.31\text{ J K}^{-1}\text{ mol}^{-1}$:

$$\begin{aligned} 0.734 &= \frac{E_a}{8.31} \times 2.18 \times 10^{-4} \\ E_a &= \frac{0.734 \times 8.31}{2.18 \times 10^{-4}} \\ E_a &\approx 27978 \text{ J mol}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

Note: To find the final answer in $\text{kJ mol}^{-1}$ as requested, divide by $1000$:

$$E_a = \frac{27978}{1000} = 28.0 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$$

ti84.instructions: Multiply ln(2.5/1.2) by 8.31, then divide by the temperature difference (1/293 - 1/313). Divide the final result by 1000.

calculator.description: Solve for Ea in Joules and convert to kiloJoules

steps.title: Convert temperature to Kelvin
steps.content:
To use the Arrhenius equation, temperatures must be in Kelvin ($K$). We add $273$ to the Celsius values.

For the first data set:
$$T_1 = 20 + 273 = 293\text{ K}$$

For the second data set:
$$T_2 = 40 + 273 = 313\text{ K}$$

steps.title: Rearrange for Pre-exponential Factor
steps.content:
The Arrhenius equation is given in the data booklet as:

$$k = A e^{\frac{-E_{a}}{R T}}$$

By taking the natural logarithm ($\ln$) of both sides, we get the linear form:

$$\ln k = \ln A - \frac{E_a}{RT}$$

To use the **Intermediate Solving for A** approach, we rearrange this to express the pre-exponential factor ($\ln A$) as a single expression using our first data set ($k_1$ and $T_1$):

$$\ln A = \ln k_1 + \frac{E_a}{RT_1}$$

steps.title: Substitute into Second Equation
steps.content:
Now, we write the linear equation for the second data set ($k_2$ and $T_2$) and substitute the expression for $\ln A$ we just found:

$$\ln k_2 = \underbrace{\left( \ln k_1 + \frac{E_a}{RT_1} \right)}_{\ln A} - \frac{E_a}{RT_2}$$

Rearranging to group the $E_a$ terms on one side:

$$\ln k_2 - \ln k_1 = \frac{E_a}{R} \left( \frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2} \right)$$ 

This can also be written using the log property $\ln(x) - \ln(y) = \ln(\frac{x}{y})$:

$$\ln\left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right) = \frac{E_a}{R} \left( \frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2} \right)$$

steps.title: Calculate Activation Energy
steps.content:
Now we substitute the known values into the equation:

- $k_1 = 1.2 \times 10^{-3}$
- $k_2 = 2.5 \times 10^{-3}$
- $R = 8.31$
- $T_1 = 293$
- $T_2 = 313$

$$\begin{aligned} \ln\left(\frac{2.5 \times 10^{-3}}{1.2 \times 10^{-3}}\right) &= \frac{E_a}{8.31} \left( \frac{1}{293} - \frac{1}{313} \right) \\ 0.734 &= \frac{E_a}{8.31} (2.18 \times 10^{-4}) \end{aligned}$$

Solving for $E_a$:

$$E_a = \frac{0.734 \times 8.31}{2.18 \times 10^{-4}} = 27978 \text{ J mol}^{-1}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the activation energy directly.

steps.title: State Final Answer
steps.content:
The question asks for the answer in $\text{kJ mol}^{-1}$. To convert from Joules to kilojoules, divide by $1000$:

$$E_a = \frac{27978}{1000} = 28.0 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$$

According to the markscheme, marks are awarded for:
- Correct calculation of the log ratio (0.734).
- Correct calculation of the temperature reciprocal difference ($2.18 \times 10^{-4}$).
- Final answer with correct units.

parts.content: State what is meant by the term activation energy, and explain how it is represented on the diagram.
parts.markscheme:
- Activation energy is the minimum energy required for the reactants to form the activated complex (transition state). <marks>1</marks>
- It is represented by the vertical distance from the reactants to the peak of the curve ($E_a = 64\ \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$). <marks>1</marks>

steps.title: Define activation energy
steps.content:
In chemistry, **activation energy** ($E_a$) is defined as the minimum energy that colliding particles must possess in order for a chemical reaction to occur. 

Another way to describe it is the energy required for the reactants to reach the **transition state** (or activated complex), which is the highest energy point along the reaction coordinate. This concept is a key part of **Topic R2.2: How fast? The rate of chemical change** in your syllabus.

steps.title: Identify diagram representation
steps.content:
On an energy profile diagram, the activation energy is represented by the **vertical distance** measured from the enthalpy level of the **reactants** to the **peak of the curve**.

Looking at the provided diagram for the combustion of methane:
- The reactants ($\text{CH}_4 + 2\text{O}_2$) are at the starting horizontal line.
- The peak represents the transition state.
- The difference between these two points is the energy barrier the molecules must overcome, which is given as:

$$E_a = 64\ \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$$

steps.title: Summary of marks
steps.content:
To earn full marks on this question, you need to provide both the theoretical definition and the visual identification:

1.  **Definition**: Minimum energy required for a successful collision or to form the transition state (1 mark).
2.  **Representation**: The vertical distance from the reactants to the peak of the curve (1 mark).

parts.content: Deduce whether the reaction is exothermic or endothermic and justify your answer using the diagram.
parts.markscheme:
- The reaction is exothermic. <marks>1</marks>
- The products are at a lower energy level than the reactants, indicating energy is released. <marks>1</marks>

steps.title: Identify reactant and product levels
steps.content:
To determine the nature of the reaction, we first look at the horizontal lines on the energy profile diagram. The line on the left represents the energy level of the **reactants** ($CH_4 + 2O_2$), and the line on the right represents the energy level of the **products** ($CO_2 + 2H_2O$).

This concept is a key part of **Topic R1.1: Measuring enthalpy change** in your IB syllabus.

highlights.text: The energy profile diagram below shows the progress of the reaction

steps.title: Compare the energy levels
steps.content:
By looking at the vertical 'Energy' axis, we can visually compare the heights of these two lines. 

Notice that the line for the **products** is physically **lower** than the line for the **reactants**. This indicates that the chemical systems' potential energy has decreased during the reaction.

highlights.text: The products are at a lower energy level than the reactants, indicating energy is released.

steps.title: Determine the reaction type
steps.content:
Because the products have less energy than the reactants, the 'missing' energy must have been released to the surroundings (usually as heat). 

When a reaction releases energy to its surroundings and the energy level of the system drops, we classify it as **exothermic**.

steps.title: Examine the energy levels
steps.content:
To determine the nature of the reaction, we first look at the energy profile diagram provided. This diagram shows the relative potential energy of the substances involved as the reaction progresses from start to finish.

On the left side of the graph, we see the energy level for the **reactants** ($CH_4 + 2O_2$). On the right side, we see the energy level for the **products** ($CO_2 + 2H_2O$).

highlights.text: justify your answer using the diagram

steps.title: Compare products and reactants
steps.content:
By observing the vertical axis (Potential Energy), we can see that the products are at a **lower energy level** than the reactants. 

In chemistry, the enthalpy change ($ΔH$) is defined as the difference between the enthalpy of the products and the enthalpy of the reactants:

$$\Delta H = H_{\text{products}} - H_{\text{reactants}}$$ 

This concept is a core part of **Topic R1.1: Measuring enthalpy change** in your syllabus.

steps.title: Analyze the enthalpy sign
steps.content:
Since the products have less energy than the reactants ($H_{\text{products}} < H_{\text{reactants}}$), the value of $\Delta H$ must be **negative** (less than zero).

$$\Delta H < 0$$

This means that the system has "lost" energy to the surroundings in the form of heat during the transition from reactants to products.

steps.title: Identify the reaction type
steps.content:
A reaction where energy is released and $\Delta H$ is negative is defined as an **exothermic** reaction. 

In summary:
1. The reaction is **exothermic**.
2. This is because the products are at a **lower energy level** than the reactants, indicating that energy has been released to the surroundings.

highlights.text: Deduce whether the reaction is exothermic or endothermic

parts.content: The enthalpy change of the reaction is $-802\ \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$. On the same diagram, sketch and label this enthalpy change.
parts.markscheme:
- Sketch a vertical arrow from reactants to products, labeled $\Delta H = -802\ \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$. <marks>1</marks>
- Arrow should point downward to show exothermic nature. <marks>1</marks>

steps.title: Identify reactant and product levels
steps.content:
In an energy profile diagram, the horizontal lines represent the potential energy of the substances involved. The level on the left represents the **reactants** ($\text{CH}_4 + 2\text{O}_2$), and the level on the right represents the **products** ($\text{CO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$).

To represent the enthalpy change ($\Delta H$), we look at the vertical difference between these two specific levels.

steps.title: Determine the arrow direction
steps.content:
The question states that the enthalpy change is $-802\ \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$. Because the value is negative, the reaction is **exothermic**, meaning energy is released to the surroundings and the products have lower potential energy than the reactants.

In diagrammatic terms, this means our arrow must point **downwards**, starting from the reactant level and ending at the product level.

highlights.text: enthalpy change of the reaction is -802\ \text{kJ mol}^{-1}
Arrow should point downward to show exothermic nature.

steps.title: Sketch and label the change
steps.content:
Now, draw a vertical line between the two horizontal levels. 

1. Start the arrow at the energy level of the reactants.
2. Draw it straight down to the level of the products.
3. Add an arrowhead pointing down.
4. Label the arrow clearly as $\Delta H = -802\ \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$.

Note: The activation energy ($E_a$) is the "hill" from the reactants to the peak, but $\Delta H$ only measures the overall change from start to finish.

highlights.text: Sketch a vertical arrow from reactants to products, labeled \Delta H = -802\ \text{kJ mol}^{-1}.

parts.content: Suggest how the presence of a catalyst affects the reaction mechanism and energy profile.
parts.markscheme:
- A catalyst provides an alternative pathway with a lower activation energy. <marks>1</marks>
- The peak of the curve would be lower in the presence of a catalyst. <marks>1</marks>

steps.title: Identify the reaction mechanism change
steps.content:
A catalyst works by changing the way a reaction happens at a molecular level. Instead of following the original route, it provides an **alternative reaction mechanism** (or pathway) for the reactants to turn into products. This is the first key point required for your marks.

This concept is a core part of **Topic R2.2: How fast? The rate of chemical change**.

highlights.text: Suggest how the presence of a catalyst affects the reaction mechanism

steps.title: Explain activation energy reduction
steps.content:
The most important feature of this alternative pathway is that it has a **lower activation energy** ($E_a$). 

In the original reaction, molecules needed at least $64\text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$ of energy to react. With a catalyst, the new "pathway" requires less energy to overcome the initial barrier, allowing more particles to have enough energy to react at a given temperature.

steps.title: Describe the energy profile change
steps.content:
On an energy profile diagram, the activation energy is represented by the distance from the reactants to the top of the curve (the peak). 

Because the catalyst lowers the activation energy, the **peak of the curve would be lower** than the original. Note that the energy of the reactants and products (and thus the overall $\Delta H$) remains exactly the same; only the "hill" in the middle changes.

steps.title: Summary of marks
steps.content:
To secure both marks for this question, ensure your answer includes these two specific points:
1. The catalyst provides an **alternative pathway/mechanism** with a **lower activation energy**.
2. The **peak of the curve** on the energy profile diagram is **lower**.

parts.content: Use the collision theory to explain how temperature affects the rate of this reaction.
parts.markscheme:
- Increasing temperature increases the kinetic energy of particles. <marks>1</marks>
- More particles have energy $\ge E_a$, so more successful collisions per second, increasing the rate. <marks>1</marks>

steps.title: Temperature and Kinetic Energy
steps.content:
To understand how temperature affects the rate, we first look at the particles themselves. According to the Kinetic Molecular Theory (which you can review in **Topic S1.1.3**), temperature is a measure of the average kinetic energy of the particles in a substance. 

When we increase the temperature of the methane and oxygen gas mixture, the particles gain energy and move faster. This directly increases their **average kinetic energy**.

steps.title: Meeting the Activation Energy
steps.content:
For a collision between methane and oxygen to actually result in a reaction (a "successful collision"), the particles must collide with a minimum amount of energy. This is called the **activation energy** ($E_a$).

In this specific reaction, the particles need at least $64\text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$ to break the existing bonds and start the reaction.

steps.title: Frequency of Successful Collisions
steps.content:
At a higher temperature, not only are there more frequent collisions because particles move faster, but more importantly, a **higher proportion** of these collisions have energy equal to or greater than the activation energy ($E \ge E_a$). 

Because more particles meet this energy threshold, there is an increase in the **frequency of successful collisions** (number of successful collisions per second).

steps.title: Final Conclusion
steps.content:
The overall rate of reaction depends on how often successful collisions occur. Since the frequency of successful collisions has increased due to the higher temperature, the **rate of reaction increases**.

To earn full marks on this question, ensure you mention:
1. The increase in **kinetic energy**.
2. More particles having **energy $\ge E_a$**, leading to more **successful collisions per unit time**.

steps.title: Link temperature to energy
steps.content:
According to the kinetic molecular theory, temperature is a measure of the average kinetic energy of the particles in a substance. When the temperature of the reaction mixture increases, the particles move faster and their average kinetic energy increases.

This concept is a prerequisite from **Topic S1.1.3: Temperature and Kinetic Energy**.

steps.title: Apply Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution
steps.content:
A Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution curve shows the distribution of kinetic energies of particles at a specific temperature. When temperature increases:
1. The peak of the curve shifts to the right (higher energy).
2. The curve flattens (the peak is lower).

This shift means that the overall distribution of particles is moved toward higher energy levels.

steps.title: Evaluate activation energy threshold
steps.content:
The question identifies a specific threshold for this reaction: the activation energy. 

$$\text{activation energy of } 64\text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$$

While the value of $E_a$ remains constant at $64\text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$, the shift in the Maxwell-Boltzmann curve means that a significantly larger area under the curve now lies to the right of this $E_a$ value.

steps.title: Relate to collision frequency
steps.content:
Since a greater proportion of particles now possess energy $\ge E_a$, there is a higher frequency of successful collisions (collisions with enough energy to overcome the energy barrier). 

As more successful collisions occur per unit of time, the overall rate of the combustion reaction increases. This aligns with the requirements of the markscheme:
- **Mark 1:** Increasing temperature increases the kinetic energy of particles.
- **Mark 2:** More particles have energy $\ge E_a$, leading to more successful collisions per second.

parts.content: Determine the activation energy for the reverse reaction. Show your reasoning.
parts.markscheme:
- Reverse reaction: $\text{CO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CH}_4 + 2\text{O}_2$ <marks>1</marks>
- $E_a(\text{reverse}) = E_a(\text{forward}) + |\Delta H| = 64 + 802 = 866\ \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$ <marks>1</marks>

steps.title: Identify the reverse reaction
steps.content:
To find the activation energy for the reverse reaction, we first need to define what that reaction is. In a reverse reaction, the products of the original equation become the reactants.

The reverse reaction is:
$$\text{CO}_2(g) + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}(l) \rightarrow \text{CH}_4(g) + 2\text{O}_2(g)$$ 

This is a critical first step as it sets the direction for our energy calculations, earning the first mark.

steps.title: Extract values from graph
steps.content:
Next, we identify the key energy values from the provided energy profile diagram and the question text:

1.  **Forward Activation Energy ($E_a(\text{forward})$):** Given as $64\ \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$. This represents the energy barrier starting from the reactants.
2.  **Enthalpy Change ($\Delta H$):** Looking at the diagram, the products are at a lower energy level than the reactants. The difference is $-802\ \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$. The negative sign indicates an exothermic reaction.

steps.title: Apply energy relationship
steps.content:
We can now use the algebraic relationship between enthalpy change and activation energies:

$$\Delta H = E_a(\text{forward}) - E_a(\text{reverse})$$

By rearranging this formula to solve for the reverse activation energy, we get:

$$E_a(\text{reverse}) = E_a(\text{forward}) - \Delta H$$

Substituting our values:

$$\begin{aligned} E_a(\text{reverse}) &= 64 - (-802) \\ E_a(\text{reverse}) &= 64 + 802 \\ E_a(\text{reverse}) &= 866\ \text{kJ mol}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter the expression to add the energies together.

ti84.instructions: Enter the forward activation energy (64) and add the absolute value of the enthalpy change (802).

desmos.instructions: Type the simple addition into the calculator cell.

calculator.description: Calculate the sum of the forward activation energy and the magnitude of the enthalpy change.

steps.title: State final answer
steps.content:
The activation energy for the reverse reaction is **$866\ \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$**.

In an energy profile, this represents the total vertical distance from the product level ($-802$) all the way up to the transition state peak ($+64$).

steps.title: Identify the reverse reaction
steps.content:
To find the activation energy for the reverse reaction, we first need to identify what that reaction is. In a reverse reaction, the products of the original equation become the reactants. 

The reverse reaction is:
$$\text{CO}_2(g) + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}(l) \rightarrow \text{CH}_4(g) + 2\text{O}_2(g)$$ 

This is a key step in IB Chemistry, as it sets the context for where we are starting on our energy diagram.

steps.title: Identify energy levels
steps.content:
Using the **Energy Profile Diagram Analysis** approach, we look at the vertical positions on the graph:

1.  **Transition State (Peak):** The peak is the highest energy point. The question states the activation energy ($E_a$) for the forward reaction is $64\ \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$, which means the peak is $64$ units above the reactants.
2.  **Products:** The diagram shows the products are at an energy level of $-802\ \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$ relative to the reactants.

For the reverse reaction, we start at the product level and must reach the transition state.

steps.title: Calculate the vertical distance
steps.content:
The activation energy for the reverse reaction ($E_a(\text{reverse})$) is the total vertical distance from the product energy level to the transition state peak.

$$\begin{aligned} E_a(\text{reverse}) &= \text{Energy of Peak} - \text{Energy of Products} \\ E_a(\text{reverse}) &= 64 - (-802) \\ E_a(\text{reverse}) &= 64 + 802 \end{aligned}$$

This calculation accounts for climbing the $802\ \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$ from the products back up to the reactant level, plus the additional $64\ \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$ to reach the peak.

gdc.instructions: Type 64 + 802 and press EXE/ENTER.

ti84.instructions: Enter 64 + 802 and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: In the calculation bar, type 64 + 802.

calculator.description: Calculate the sum of the forward activation energy and the magnitude of the enthalpy change.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Summing these values gives us the total energy required for the reverse process:

$$E_a(\text{reverse}) = 866\ \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$$

In an IB exam, you earn one mark for identifying the reverse reaction and one mark for the correct calculation and final value.

options.markscheme: No reaction expected because Sn is less reactive than Mg, so $\text{Mg}^{2+}$ is not reduced; no colored species produced/consumed so no systematic absorbance change.
Acidified $\text{MnO}_4^-$ is purple, but tertiary alcohols such as tert-butanol are not readily oxidized under typical conditions, so a clear time-dependent absorbance change is not expected.
In the acid-catalysed iodination of acetone, colored $\text{I}_2$ is consumed; its concentration (and absorbance at a suitable wavelength) decreases with time.
Neutralization produces colorless $\text{BaCl}_2\text{(aq)}$ and $\text{H}_2\text{O}$; no colored species produced/consumed so no absorbance change.

questions.specification:
A student investigates the relationship between the rate of a chemical reaction ($k$) and temperature ($T$) to understand the Arrhenius equation:

$$
k=A e^{-\frac{E_a}{R T}},
$$

where:
- $k$ is the reaction rate constant ($\mathrm{s}^{-1}$),
- $A$ is the pre-exponential factor,
- $E_a$ is the activation energy ($\mathrm{J} \, \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$),
- $R=8.314 \, \mathrm{J} \, \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \, \mathrm{K}^{-1}$ is the gas constant,
- $T$ is the temperature ($\mathrm{K}$).

The following data were recorded:
| Temperature ($T$) ($\mathrm{K}$) | Reaction Rate ($k$) ($\mathrm{s}^{-1}$) |
| :--- | :--- |
| 290 | 0.001 |
| 300 | 0.003 |
| 310 | 0.007 |
| 320 | 0.015 |
| 330 | 0.030 |
| 340 | 0.055 |

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/59d39649-9862-4d48-bcd0-36b7d91caf39)

labeledSubSubTopics.title: R2.2.12 Arrhenius equation (Higher Level Only)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question is centered on applying and interpreting the Arrhenius equation, including explaining the temperature–rate trend, determining Ea graphically (ln k vs 1/T), and calculating Ea from the linearized form.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Reactivity 2. How much, how fast and how far? > R2.2 How fast? The rate of chemical change > R2.2.13 Arrhenius factor (Higher Level Only)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: R2.2.13 Arrhenius factor (Higher Level Only)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Uses the Arrhenius factor/pre-exponential term A via ln(A)=15 and discusses how it fits into the Arrhenius relationship.

labeledSubSubTopics.title: R2.2.4 Activation energy
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Several parts focus on activation energy Ea: graphical determination, numerical calculation, and uncertainty propagation in Ea.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Reactivity 2. How much, how fast and how far? > R2.2 How fast? The rate of chemical change > R2.2.3 Factors affecting the rate of reaction
labeledSubSubTopics.title: R2.2.3 Factors affecting the rate of reaction
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Parts b–c ask for sources of experimental error affecting measured rate and improvements, which align with practical factors affecting reaction rate measurements.

parts.content: Identify two potential sources of error in this experiment and explain their impact on the measured reaction rate.
parts.markscheme:
- Inaccurate temperature control in the reaction vessel. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Temperature fluctuations change $T$ and therefore change $k$ (and increase scatter in the results). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Errors in measuring the reaction rate ($k$) due to inconsistent timing/end-point detection (e.g. human reaction time). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Leads to random (or systematic) error in $k$, reducing precision of the trend/graph. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Heat loss/gain to surroundings so the reacting mixture temperature is not the same as the measured/nominal temperature. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Changes in concentrations due to evaporation/poor mixing between runs affecting the measured rate constant. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Suggest two improvements to reduce these errors.
parts.markscheme:
- Use a thermostatically controlled water bath (or oil bath) and allow the reaction mixture to equilibrate to the set temperature before starting timing. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Use automated/continuous monitoring (e.g. colorimeter/spectrophotometer with data logger) to determine $k$ and reduce timing/end-point subjectivity. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Calibrate and use a digital temperature probe in the reaction mixture (not just the bath) to confirm the actual reaction temperature. <marks>1 mark</marks>

_**OR**_

- Repeat each temperature measurement several times and average the values (discard anomalies) to reduce random error. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Describe the trend in the graph and explain how it supports the Arrhenius equation.
parts.markscheme:
- The reaction rate constant $k$ increases as temperature $T$ increases. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The increase is non-linear/curved, consistent with an exponential-type rise. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- This supports the Arrhenius equation since $k=Ae^{-E_a/(RT)}$ predicts $k$ increases as $T$ increases (because $-E_a/(RT)$ becomes less negative). <marks>1 mark</marks>

_**OR**_

- Stating that a plot of $\ln(k)$ against $1/T$ would be linear is consistent with Arrhenius behaviour. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Suggest a method to determine the activation energy $E_a$ graphically.
parts.markscheme:
- Take natural logs: $\ln(k)=\ln(A)-\frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T}\right)$. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Plot $\ln(k)$ against $1/T$ (Arrhenius plot) to obtain a straight line. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Use gradient $m=-E_a/R$ so $E_a=-mR$. <marks>1 mark</marks>

_**OR**_

- Determine $E_a$ from the slope using two points on the best-fit line (not raw points) to reduce random error. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
Using the rearranged Arrhenius equation:

$$\ln(k) = \ln(A) - \frac{E_a}{R T}$$

Calculate $E_a$ using the data point $T = 310 \, \mathrm{K}$ and $k = 0.007 \, \mathrm{s}^{-1}$, given that $\ln(A)$ is known to be 15. Show all your working.
parts.markscheme:
- Rearrange for $E_a$: $E_a = RT\left(\ln(A) - \ln(k)\right)$. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Calculate $\ln(0.007)\approx -4.962$ and so $\ln(A)-\ln(k)=15-(-4.962)=19.962$. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- $RT=8.314\times 310=2577.34$ so $E_a=2577.34\times 19.962\approx 5.14\times 10^4\,\mathrm{J\,mol^{-1}}$. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- $E_a\approx 51.4\,\mathrm{kJ\,mol^{-1}}$ (to 3 s.f.). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Accept minor rounding differences consistent with working shown (e.g. $5.14\times 10^4$ to $5.15\times 10^4\,\mathrm{J\,mol^{-1}}$). <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: If the uncertainty in $T$ is $\pm 1 \, \mathrm{K}$ and the uncertainty in $k$ is $\pm 0.0001 \, \mathrm{s}^{-1}$ for the same data point, calculate the percentage uncertainty in $E_a$. Assume $\ln(A)$ is exact.
parts.markscheme:
- Since $E_a=RTX$ where $X=(\ln(A)-\ln(k))$, percentage uncertainty adds for multiplication: $\%\Delta E_a\approx \%\Delta T + \%\Delta X$. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- $\%\Delta T=\frac{1}{310}\times 100=0.323\%$ (≈$0.32\%$). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- $\Delta(\ln k)\approx \frac{\Delta k}{k}=\frac{0.0001}{0.007}=0.0143$ (absolute uncertainty in $\ln k$). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- $X=15-(-4.962)=19.962$ so $\%\Delta X=\frac{0.0143}{19.962}\times 100=0.0716\%$ (≈$0.07\%$). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Total $\%\Delta E_a\approx 0.323\%+0.0716\%=0.395\%$ (≈$0.39\%$). <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
A student monitored the concentration of $N_2O_5$ over time at a constant temperature of 318 K and obtained the following results:

| 0 | 0.800 |

parts.markscheme:
$$\Delta t = 5.00 \times 60 = 300 \text{ s}$$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

$$\text{Rate} = \frac{0.800 - 0.500}{300} = 1.00 \times 10^{-3} \text{ (mol dm}^{-3} \text{ s}^{-1}\text{)}$$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award [2] for correct final answer. Award [1] if the time conversion is missed ($1.00 \times 10^{-3}$ vs $0.060$).</note> <marks>2 marks</marks>

options.markscheme: This plot is linear for a zero-order reaction.
This plot is linear for a second-order reaction.
For a first-order reaction, the integrated rate law is $\ln[\text{A}] = -kt + \ln[\text{A}]_0$. A plot of $\ln[\text{A}]$ vs. time gives a straight line with a slope of $-k$.
This plot is linear for a first-order reaction (rate $=k[\text{A}]$), but it is not a concentration–time integrated rate-law plot.

questions.specification:
The reaction between hydrogen gas $\left(\mathrm{H}_2\right)$ and nitrogen dioxide $\left(\mathrm{NO}_2\right)$ was studied under controlled conditions. The experiment involved mixing known concentrations of $\mathrm{H}_2$ and $\mathrm{NO}_2$ in a reaction vessel and measuring the volume of gas produced over time using a gas syringe. Initial rates of the reaction were determined from the rate of gas production at the start of each reaction. The results are shown in the table below:

| Experiment | $\left[\mathrm{H}_2\right] / \mathrm{mol} \ \mathrm{dm}^{-3}$ | $\left[\mathrm{NO}_2\right] / \mathrm{mol} \ \mathrm{dm}^{-3}$ | Initial Rate $/ \mathrm{mol} \ \mathrm{dm}^{-3} \ \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | 0.100 | 0.200 | $1.44 \times 10^{-3}$ |
| 2 | 0.200 | 0.200 | $2.88 \times 10^{-3}$ |
| 3 | 0.100 | 0.400 | $5.76 \times 10^{-3}$ |
